name: Home
title: API Documentation Home
description: Landing page for developers to access API documentation, quick-start tasks, and global search across all docs.

core_features: Load and display high-level overview content (title, subtitle, introductory markdown, featured documentation sections, and last-updated metadata) using the getHomeOverview interface.

Display a list of dynamic quick-start tasks retrieved via getHomeQuickStartTasks, allowing users to quickly navigate to relevant API Reference, Guides, Webhooks, SDKs, or Authentication content, including deep-links to specific endpoints or SDK methods when available.
Provide a global search form that submits queries and optional filters to the searchDocumentation interface, and display pageless search results directly on the Home page.
Support deep-linking via URL parameters so that search state (query and filters) and focused quick-start tasks can be restored or shared when loading index.html.

Route users to other documentation pages (api_reference.html, guides.html, webhooks.html, sdks.html, authentication.html) based on user interactions with featured sections, quick-start tasks, and search results, using URL parameters to encode destination context such as endpoint or method identifiers.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Landing on Home and discovering key sections: 1) User navigates to index.html without URL parameters. 2) On page mount, the home-overview-section component calls getHomeOverview. 3) The component exposes heroTitle, heroSubtitle, introMarkdown, featuredSections, and lastUpdated to the page. 4) The user scans the overview and clicks a featured section item; the click handler reads the target section’s pageType/pageId from featuredSections and navigates to the corresponding documentation page (e.g., api_reference.html or guides.html) using URL parameters as needed (such as pageId or section identifiers).

Workflow 2 – Starting from a quick-start task: 1) User lands on index.html. 2) The quick-start-task-list component calls getHomeQuickStartTasks and renders the returned tasks. 3) The user selects a task that may include a targetPageId/targetPageType and optionally relatedEndpointId and/or relatedSdkMethodId. 4) The click handler builds the destination URL: for API Reference tasks it links to api_reference.html with URL parameters such as endpointId, and optionally additional parameters like resource, version, view, and lang when provided by the task or derived from related metadata; for SDK or Authentication tasks it links to the appropriate target page (e.g., sdks.html or authentication.html) with corresponding identifiers in the query string. 5) The browser navigates to the constructed URL without storing any state locally.

Workflow 3 – Running a global search from Home: 1) User places focus in the global-search-form input on index.html. 2) The user types a query and optionally adjusts client-side filter controls. 3) On form submit, the component updates the browser’s URL to index.html with query parameters (e.g., q for the search query and filters for encoded filters) and triggers searchDocumentation using the query and parsed filters. 4) The search-results-panel component receives the searchDocumentation response and renders a list of results including title, snippet, and metadata (pageId, pageType, entityType, entityId). 5) When the user clicks a result, the click handler constructs a navigation URL pointing at the appropriate documentation page (e.g., api_reference.html, webhooks.html, sdks.html, guides.html, authentication.html, or others) and, when applicable, appends entity-specific URL parameters such as endpointId or a webhook event identifier derived from entityType/entityId.

Workflow 4 – Deep-linked search or focused quick-start via URL: 1) User opens index.html with pre-set URL parameters, such as q for a query, filters for constraints, or quickStartTaskId for focusing a specific task. 2) The home-routing-controller component parses the URL on load. 3) If q is present, it passes the query and filters directly into the global-search-form and automatically triggers searchDocumentation so that the search-results-panel shows results without additional user input. 4) If quickStartTaskId is present, the quick-start-task-list component highlights or scrolls to the matching task after getHomeQuickStartTasks resolves. 5) Any subsequent changes the user makes to search or task selection update the URL parameters so the state remains shareable and restorable on reload.

Workflow 5 – Search initiated from a shared header: 1) On another documentation page, the user types a query into a global header search that routes to index.html with URL parameters representing the query and optional filters (e.g., q and filters). 2) The user is redirected to index.html with these parameters. 3) The home-routing-controller reads the URL, pre-populates the global-search-form, and automatically invokes searchDocumentation. 4) The search-results-panel shows results scoped to the query that originated from the header without requiring another submit. 5) The user may refine the search, which updates the URL parameters and re-calls searchDocumentation.
interactions: On page load (index.html without specific state): Trigger getHomeOverview and getHomeQuickStartTasks in parallel; once resolved, bind overview data to the overview section and render the quick-start task list.

On URL parameter change (e.g., when the history API is used to push a new q or filters value): Re-parse URL parameters in the home-routing-controller; if q is present and non-empty, call searchDocumentation again and refresh the search-results-panel.

On featured overview section click: Read the clicked item’s metadata from getHomeOverview.featuredSections (including pageId and pageType) and navigate to the appropriate target page filename (such as api_reference.html, guides.html, webhooks.html, sdks.html, or authentication.html) by building a URL that includes any required query parameters derived from the section’s metadata (for example, passing pageId or other section-specific identifiers).

On quick-start task click: From the clicked task (returned by getHomeQuickStartTasks), inspect targetPageType and targetPageId; if relatedEndpointId or relatedSdkMethodId are present, use them to construct URL parameters for deep-linking (for example, endpointId or sdkMethodId, plus any additional context such as resource, version, view, and lang when provided). Then navigate to the corresponding page (api_reference.html, sdks.html, authentication.html, etc.) using the constructed URL.
On search input change: Update a local query state in the global-search-form component; no API calls are executed until the user submits or URL parameters request an automatic search.

On search form submit: Prevent default browser form submission, update the index.html URL with q and filters query parameters, call searchDocumentation with these values, and pass the response to search-results-panel for rendering.

On search result click: Read the result’s pageId, pageType, entityType, and entityId from searchDocumentation.results; map these to a destination filename and construct a URL with appropriate parameters (for example, endpointId when entityType refers to an API endpoint, webhookEventId when entityType refers to a webhook event, or sdkMethodId when entityType refers to an SDK method), then navigate to that URL.
On lastUpdated display interaction (e.g., hover or focus): Optionally show additional context (like a tooltip) derived from getHomeOverview.lastUpdated without additional API calls.

On browser back/forward navigation: Detect URL changes via popstate; re-run the same routing logic as on initial load (parse q, filters, quickStartTaskId, and other supported parameters) to restore search results and quick-start focus purely from the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page derives all functional state from the URL and live interface responses, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence. The main URL is index.html with optional query parameters. A parameter q represents the current search query; when q is present and non-empty, the home-routing-controller instructs the global-search-form to show this value and triggers a call to searchDocumentation using q and the filters parameter. The filters parameter represents encoded filter criteria (for example, serialized JSON or a delimited string); it is passed unchanged into searchDocumentation.filters and also used by the global-search-form to display active filters. A parameter quickStartTaskId represents the identifier of a quick-start task to focus; after getHomeQuickStartTasks resolves, the quick-start-task-list uses quickStartTaskId to highlight or scroll to the corresponding task if it exists. Additional optional parameters, such as from, can indicate the origin of navigation (for example, from=header-search) and are only used to adjust initial UI focus without changing data fetching. The state of whether the search-results-panel is visible is derived: it is visible when a searchDocumentation call has been executed due to a non-empty q, and hidden otherwise. When users interact (submitting search, selecting filters, or clicking different quick-start tasks that should be focused), the page updates the current URL with the new parameters via the History API, and re-runs the same parsing logic so that UI state always matches the URL. Navigation to other documentation pages is implemented by constructing URLs that include IDs or context values (for example, endpointId, sdkMethodId, webhookEventId, or resource/version-related parameters when provided by the task or search result metadata) in the query string, allowing those destination pages to reconstruct their own state from URL parameters.

components.functionality: On page mount, this component calls getHomeOverview to retrieve heroTitle, heroSubtitle, introMarkdown, featuredSections, and lastUpdated, then exposes these values to the DOM and to child components responsible for rendering overview text and featured section links. It also provides click handlers that route users to the appropriate destination pages based on the metadata returned in featuredSections.

This component retrieves an array of quick-start tasks via getHomeQuickStartTasks and renders them as selectable items. Each task exposes its taskId, title, description, targetPageId, targetPageType, and any related identifiers such as relatedEndpointId or relatedSdkMethodId. When a task is clicked, the component constructs a navigation URL based on targetPageType: for API Reference it targets api_reference.html and appends endpoint-related parameters; for SDKs it targets sdks.html with SDK-related parameters; for Authentication it targets authentication.html, and so on. The component also reads the quickStartTaskId URL parameter to highlight or scroll to a specific task on load, ensuring that deep-linked tasks are emphasized.

This component manages the search input and any filter controls on the Home page. It initializes its internal state from URL parameters (q and filters) provided by the home-routing-controller. When the user submits the form, it updates the URL to include the current query and filters and then calls searchDocumentation with those values. It forwards the returned results to the search-results-panel component for display. It also exposes change events so that modifications to filters or query can be reflected immediately in the URL when desired.

This component receives the response from searchDocumentation and displays the list of results when a query is active. Each result exposes title, snippet, pageId, pageType, entityType, and entityId. The component uses this metadata to create navigation URLs pointing at the correct target docs page (e.g., api_reference.html, webhooks.html, sdks.html, guides.html, authentication.html, or others) and appends entity-specific query parameters such as endpointId, webhookEventId, or sdkMethodId when applicable. If the total count is zero, the component can show a no-results state derived solely from the searchDocumentation response.

This non-visual component centralizes URL-based state management for the Home page. On initial load and on browser navigation events, it parses URL parameters (including q, filters, quickStartTaskId, and optional from) and dispatches this state to other components such as global-search-form and quick-start-task-list. When the search form is submitted or when a quick-start task is programmatically focused, this controller updates the browser URL via the History API so that the current state is encoded in the query string. It also triggers searchDocumentation whenever a non-empty q is present, ensuring that search-results-panel stays synchronized with the URL.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s pattern: a fixed full-width header at the top, a two-column content area below (narrow left sidebar, wider right main column). Inside the main column, content flows linearly top-to-bottom: page title with underline, a brief overview/introduction, then the global search as the primary interaction, followed by quick-start tasks. The search results panel sits directly beneath the search form in the same vertical flow, expanding when used. The routing controller is non-visual and only affects which sections/states are shown.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s pattern: a fixed full-width header at the top, a two-column content area below (narrow left sidebar, wider right main column). Inside the main column, content flows linearly top-to-bottom: page title with underline, a brief overview/introduction, then the global search as the primary interaction, followed by quick-start tasks. The search results panel sits directly beneath the search form in the same vertical flow, expanding when used. The routing controller is non-visual and only affects which sections/states are shown.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header uses about 10–12% of viewport height. Below, the content container is centered with generous horizontal gutters. Left sidebar occupies ~20% of the container width; right main content ~80%. Inside the main column, each block (overview, search form, results, quick-start) spans roughly 80–90% of the main column width with consistent left alignment. Vertical spacing: 32–40px between major sections (overview, search, quick-start), and 16–24px between internal elements (titles, paragraphs, cards, result rows). Padding within cards/rows uses ~12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate spaciousness: short text snippets, comfortable line lengths, and only a handful of elements above the fold. The search bar is large and uncluttered. Quick-start tasks and search results appear as clean, single-line or short multi-line rows with ample breathing room, mirroring the endpoint rows in the mockup. Avoid stacking too many items tightly; instead, allow whitespace to separate groups.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow starts at the dark fixed header, then moves to the page title and its underline in the main column. From there, the eye flows down through the overview text, then immediately to the prominent global search bar. When results are present, attention continues downward into the results list, which uses horizontally oriented rows that echo the mockup’s endpoint cards: a colored left accent, main label, secondary text, then a right-chevron. Below that, the quick-start task list provides a secondary vertical sequence of interactive rows. The left sidebar remains visually subdued, acting as a stable vertical reference rather than a primary focal point.

layout.overall_description: The page sits under a fixed, full-width dark header containing the product logo and a highlighted “Documentation” tab. Beneath this header, a centered content container spans the width of the viewport with generous left and right gutters. Inside this container, a narrow left sidebar (about one-fifth of the width) lists documentation sections in a light, low-contrast style, while the main content column to the right (about four-fifths of the width) carries the primary narrative. At the very top of the main column, the Home page title appears in a bold, medium-large font, left-aligned, with a subtle horizontal underline stretching partway across the column. Immediately below, a concise overview section introduces the documentation in one or two short paragraphs, spaced with about 16–24px gaps to keep the text airy. Under this overview, a wide global search bar dominates the upper-middle of the screen: a single, clean input row with rounded or subtly squared corners, centered within the main column and spanning around 60–70% of its width, with generous vertical padding and whitespace above and below. As users interact, the search results panel appears directly underneath, occupying the full usable width of the main column, presenting results in vertically stacked rows that visually echo API endpoint cards: each row stretches nearly edge-to-edge, with a thin colored accent bar at the far left, a clear title and description in the center, and a chevron icon on the right. Below this search area, a quick-start task list continues the vertical rhythm, using similar row-style cards to present key actions like “Get API key” or “Make your first request.” The entire composition is calm, linear, and predictable, with the left sidebar anchoring navigation and the right column guiding the eye in a deliberate top-to-bottom flow, emphasizing readability and quick action over decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home overview section sits near the top of the main content column, directly beneath the Home page title and its underline. It is left-aligned and spans roughly 80–90% of the width of the main column, leaving consistent inner margins on the right for breathing room. The section begins with a secondary heading such as “Overview” or “Welcome,” set about 24–32px below the page title underline. Beneath this heading, one or two short paragraphs of explanatory text flow, each separated by 8–16px vertical spacing, creating a compact but airy introduction. The overall vertical block (heading plus text) occupies roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height. There is 24–32px of vertical space between the last paragraph and the component below it (the global search form), so that the overview feels clearly distinct yet closely related as a lead-in to search. On wider screens, the text lines are kept moderate by the fixed-width content container, mirroring the readable line length from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the main content flow; it scrolls naturally with the page and adapts to the main column width without changing its relative position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-start task list is placed in the main content column beneath the search area, acting as a structured follow-up for users who either do not search or have completed an initial search. There is a clear vertical gap of about 32px above it, separating it from the search results panel (or directly from the search form when no results are shown). A section heading such as “Quick Start” is left-aligned with the main column’s text baseline and spans the same width as the overview heading. Below the heading, the tasks are presented as a vertical stack of card-like rows, each stretching almost the full width of the main column (about 90–95% of the column width, leaving a small inner margin on the right). Each task row is about 48–64px tall, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. Visually, they echo the documentation’s endpoint cards: a slim colored vertical bar (e.g., a neutral accent or status color) on the far left, the task title and a short one-line description centered in the remaining content area, and an optional right-facing chevron icon aligned to the far right to suggest clickability. Rows are separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. The block as a whole occupies roughly the lower third of the initial viewport on standard desktop, extending further as more tasks are added, maintaining the clean, low-density feel of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically stacked list that expands downward with additional tasks. It remains within the main document flow, pushed down if the search results panel grows, and scrolls off-screen as the user moves upward or downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global search form is the main interactive focus near the top of the Home content. It sits directly below the home-overview-section, with approximately 24–32px of whitespace separating the overview’s final paragraph from the search component. The search form is horizontally centered within the main content column but remains aligned to the left text baseline, spanning about 60–70% of the main column’s width so it feels prominent without appearing edge-to-edge. Vertically, the form is a single, tall input row (around 48–56px high) with generous horizontal padding (16–24px) inside the field. If there is a label like “Search all docs,” it appears immediately above the input, left-aligned with 4–8px spacing, using a smaller, subdued font. The input itself extends nearly the full width of the component’s allowed width, possibly with an embedded search icon at the left inside the field, and a clear placeholder. There is about 16–24px of whitespace below the input before the search-results-panel begins, preserving the moderate spacing style. On narrower screens, the search form gracefully expands to nearly 100% of the main column width, but always maintains consistent side margins relative to the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow but visually anchored: it appears above the fold on typical desktops and becomes the central focal point after a brief scroll from the header. It does not stick to the top, but it stays logically paired with the search results panel that appears immediately below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results panel is positioned directly beneath the global search form, aligned to the same left margin and spanning nearly the full width of the main content column (around 90–95% of its width). There is a small vertical gap (16–24px) between the bottom of the search input and the top of the results list. When there are no results, this area either remains collapsed (showing nothing) or shows a minimal, low-contrast placeholder message occupying about the height of a single row, preserving the clean look. When populated, results are displayed as a vertical column of row-style cards reminiscent of API endpoint rows from the mockup: each row is a horizontal rectangle stretching across the panel, with a thin colored vertical bar at the far left (signaling result type or category), followed by the result title in bold and a short one-line description or path beneath or beside it. On the far right of each row, a right-chevron icon indicates that clicking will navigate to the selected documentation page. Rows have consistent height (around 56–72px, depending on whether two lines of text are allowed), with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. Individual rows are separated by 4–8px, and the entire block may have a subtle top border or section label like “Results” in small text to orient the user, placed just above the first row. The panel expands vertically as more results are shown, pushing the quick-start-task-list further down the page, maintaining a clear vertical hierarchy and adequate whitespace at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Dynamic, height-expanding block that appears and grows within the normal document flow below the search form. It does not overlay other content; instead, it pushes subsequent sections (like the quick-start-task-list) downward as results accumulate. When the user scrolls, the results panel scrolls naturally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home routing controller is non-visual; it functions as the logical layer determining which sections or states appear within the Home layout. Conceptually, it sits at the root of the Home page’s main content area, orchestrating whether components such as the search-results-panel are visible, whether the quick-start-task-list is collapsed or expanded, and how the page responds to route changes (e.g., deep-linking into specific doc sections from search). It does not occupy a visible region, but its behavior affects how much vertical space other components take and whether some sections appear or are hidden at any given time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No direct spatial presence in the UI. It operates behind the scenes, controlling the mounting, unmounting, or state (expanded/collapsed) of visual components within the same fixed header + sidebar + main column structure, without introducing overlays or independent positioning of its own.

name: API Reference
title: API Reference
description: Interactive API reference page where developers can browse resources and endpoints, inspect versioned documentation, run requests via a Try-It console, and copy multi-language code samples.

core_features: Load and display API resources and their endpoints in a left-hand sidebar using getApiResourcesForSidebar and getApiEndpointsForResource.
Select an endpoint and load its available versions, default version, and metadata using getApiEndpointVersions.
Display full endpoint-version documentation (path, method, description, parameters, schemas) using getApiEndpointVersionDetails.
Provide a tabbed view for endpoint sub-views ('docs', 'code', 'try') controlled by the URL view parameter.
Display multi-language request/response code samples for the selected endpoint version using getApiEndpointCodeSamples, with language selection driven by the URL lang parameter.
Allow editing of editable code samples in-place and copying of any sample via performCopyCodeSnippet.
Provide a Try-It / API Explorer console for enabled endpoint versions using getTryItConsoleConfig and executeTryItRequest, with optional auto-open via the consoleOpen URL parameter.
Highlight a specific parameter (in docs tables and Try-It controls) when a highlightParam URL parameter is present.
Allow switching between endpoint versions via a version selector, synchronized with the URL version parameter.
Support deep-linking into a specific resource, endpoint, version, view, language, and Try-It state using the resource, endpointId, version, view, lang, consoleOpen, and highlightParam URL parameters.
Provide inline navigation links from endpoint docs to related pages (Authentication, Rate Limits, SDKs, Webhooks, Docs home) with appropriate outgoing URL parameters.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Deep-link to a specific endpoint version and view: 1) User opens api_reference.html with some combination of resource, endpointId, version, view, lang, consoleOpen, and highlightParam URL parameters. 2) The page controller parses URL parameters and calls getApiResourcesForSidebar with the API Reference page id. 3) The page selects the initial resource: if resource matches a returned ApiResource.slug, that resource is selected; otherwise the first resource in the ordered list is selected. 4) The page calls getApiEndpointsForResource for the selected resourceId and selects the endpoint whose id matches endpointId if present; otherwise it selects the first endpoint in the ordered list. 5) The page calls getApiEndpointVersions with the selected endpointId and determines the active endpointVersionId: if version matches a version.apiVersion in the returned collection it selects that version; otherwise it uses defaultVersionId. 6) The page calls getApiEndpointVersionDetails, getApiEndpointCodeSamples, and getTryItConsoleConfig (if hasTryItConsole is true) for the active endpointVersionId. 7) The initial visible sub-view is chosen: if view is provided, that tab is activated; else if consoleOpen equals 'true' and the console is enabled, the 'try' tab is activated; otherwise 'docs' is activated. 8) If lang is provided and matches one of the ApiCodeSample.language values, that language tab is selected; otherwise the sample where isDefault is true is selected. 9) If highlightParam is provided, any ApiParameter with a matching name and any matching parameter controls in the Try-It console are visually highlighted. 10) The page scrolls to the selected endpoint details panel (based on endpointId), ensuring the requested state is fully reflected.

Workflow 2 – Browse resources and endpoints from the sidebar: 1) User opens api_reference.html without specifying particular URL parameters (or changes them over time). 2) The page calls getApiResourcesForSidebar and displays all ApiResource entries ordered by sidebarOrder (or another server-supplied order). 3) For the initially selected resource, the page calls getApiEndpointsForResource and renders its ApiEndpoint items as nested entries beneath that resource. 4) When the user clicks a resource in the sidebar, the selected resourceId changes, the URL parameter resource is updated to the resource.slug, and getApiEndpointsForResource is called for the new resourceId. 5) The endpoint selection is reset to the first endpoint in the new resource, endpointId URL parameter is updated, and the endpoint details panel is refreshed by calling getApiEndpointVersions and subsequent version-specific interfaces. 6) When the user clicks a specific endpoint in the sidebar, endpointId is updated in the URL, the corresponding details panel becomes active, getApiEndpointVersions is called for that endpoint, and the remainder of the endpoint-version flow (version selection, docs, code, Try-It) updates accordingly.

Workflow 3 – Compare and switch endpoint versions: 1) User views an endpoint that has versioning (hasVersioning is true on the ApiEndpoint). 2) The endpoint details panel retrieves versions via getApiEndpointVersions and populates a version selector control with each available apiVersion. 3) The currently active version is determined based on the URL version parameter or defaultVersionId. 4) When the user selects a different version from the version selector, the component updates the URL version parameter to the newly selected apiVersion, determines the corresponding endpointVersionId from the versions list, and calls getApiEndpointVersionDetails, getApiEndpointCodeSamples (if hasCodeSamples is true), and getTryItConsoleConfig (if hasTryItConsole is true) to refresh all sub-views. 5) The panel visually indicates that the documentation, parameters, schemas, code samples, and Try-It configuration now reflect the newly selected version.

Workflow 4 – Use the Try-It / API Explorer console: 1) For an endpoint version where the console.isEnabled value (from getTryItConsoleConfig) is true, the 'Try' tab is available and the console is rendered. 2) When the 'Try' tab becomes active (either by user click or via initial URL view or consoleOpen parameters), the component uses the returned parameters and sampleRequest from getTryItConsoleConfig to populate path parameters, query parameters, headers, and body JSON fields. 3) If supportsAuth is true, the console shows authentication-related input or guidance and an inline link to the Authentication page. 4) The user can modify any parameter values or HTTP method (if methodSelectorEnabled is true). 5) When the user submits the request, executeTryItRequest is called with endpointVersionId, the chosen methodOverride, pathParams, queryParams, headers, and body. 6) The console displays the response using the values returned from executeTryItRequest (statusCode, statusText, durationMs, responseHeaders, responseBody, and any errorMessage). 7) If highlightParam is present in the URL, and it matches any parameter name in the console, that input is emphasized so that the user can focus on it while executing requests. 8) If the user switches away from the 'Try' tab, the current console state remains in memory for that session but does not alter URL parameters other than view.

Workflow 5 – View and copy multi-language code samples: 1) When an endpoint version supports code samples (hasCodeSamples is true in getApiEndpointVersions), the 'Code' tab becomes available. 2) On initial render of the 'Code' tab for a given endpoint version, getApiEndpointCodeSamples is called with the endpointVersionId and returns one or more ApiCodeSample items. 3) The code-samples component selects the active language tab using the URL lang parameter if it matches a sample.language; otherwise it uses the sample where isDefault is true. 4) When the user selects a different language tab, the component updates the URL lang parameter and updates the visible code snippet accordingly. 5) If a sample has isEditable true, the user can modify the code directly in the editor; these modifications affect what is sent to performCopyCodeSnippet but do not affect server-stored samples. 6) When the user clicks a 'Copy' button for any code sample where hasCopyButton is true, the component calls performCopyCodeSnippet with sourceType (e.g., indicating an API code sample), sourceId (the ApiCodeSample.id), language (the sample.language), and the current snippet text (including any user edits). 7) Based on the response from performCopyCodeSnippet, the UI shows success or error feedback while the actual clipboard copy is handled by the client environment.

Workflow 6 – Navigate to related documentation pages from an endpoint: 1) Within the endpoint documentation sub-view, the description area includes contextual links such as 'Authentication', 'View rate limits for this endpoint', 'View Python SDK example', and 'Learn about webhooks' for relevant endpoints. 2) When the user clicks the 'Authentication' link, the page navigates to authentication.html, including a method query parameter set to a defaultAuthMethodId derived from the current authentication context. 3) When the user clicks 'View rate limits for this endpoint', the page navigates to rate_limits.html with endpointId, method, and path query parameters set from the selected ApiEndpoint and the active ApiEndpointVersion (primaryHttpMethod or endpointVersion.httpMethod and pathTemplate or endpointVersion.path). 4) When the user clicks 'View Python SDK example', the page navigates to sdk_python.html with sdkId, module, and method query parameters matching the SDK method that wraps the currently selected ApiEndpoint, as determined by page configuration. 5) When the user clicks 'Learn about webhooks', the page navigates to webhooks.html without extra parameters. 6) At any time, clicking the breadcrumb or logo navigates to index.html without extra parameters.

Workflow 7 – Highlight and share specific parameters: 1) In the endpoint docs sub-view, each ApiParameter row in the parameter table includes an interaction that can focus that parameter (for example, by clicking the parameter name). 2) When the user focuses a parameter, the component sets or updates the highlightParam URL parameter to the selected parameter name, which updates the browser URL so the user can share it. 3) The docs table applies a highlight style to the row whose ApiParameter.name matches the highlightParam value. 4) If the selected endpoint version has a Try-It console, its input control for that same parameter name also receives a highlight. 5) When another parameter is selected or highlightParam is removed from the URL (e.g., via a 'clear highlight' action), the highlight style is updated or cleared accordingly both in docs and Try-It views.
interactions: Header search: User types a search query into a header search field and submits; this triggers searchDocumentation with query and optional filters (such as limiting results to the API Reference page type). Results are displayed in an overlay or redirected search page, with each result linking to its target page using the returned pageId and entityId.

Sidebar resource selection: User clicks a resource name in the left-hand sidebar; this updates the selected resourceId, triggers getApiEndpointsForResource for that resource, updates the URL resource parameter to the resource.slug, and resets endpointId and version as needed based on the new resource’s endpoints and versions.

Sidebar endpoint selection: User clicks an endpoint under a resource; this updates the selected endpointId, updates the URL endpointId parameter, calls getApiEndpointVersions for the chosen endpoint, resolves the active version, and triggers loading of details, code samples, and Try-It configuration for that version.

Version selector change: User selects a different version from a dropdown or segmented control; this maps the selected apiVersion to the corresponding endpointVersionId from getApiEndpointVersions, updates the URL version parameter, and refetches docs (getApiEndpointVersionDetails), code samples (getApiEndpointCodeSamples), and Try-It configuration (getTryItConsoleConfig) for that version.

View tab switching: User clicks on a tab such as 'Docs', 'Code', or 'Try'; the tab component updates the URL view parameter to the selected view and shows only the corresponding sub-component. If switching to 'Try' for an endpoint version whose console is enabled and not yet loaded, the component ensures getTryItConsoleConfig has been called before rendering.

Code language switching: Within the 'Code' tab, user selects a language tab; the component switches the active ApiCodeSample based on language, updates the URL lang parameter, and shows the chosen sample’s code and metadata.

Code editing: For ApiCodeSample items where isEditable is true, user edits the code snippet directly. The component keeps the edited text in local component state and ensures that any subsequent copy interaction uses the current edited text while keeping server data unchanged.

Copy code snippet: User clicks a 'Copy' button associated with an ApiCodeSample. The component calls performCopyCodeSnippet with the configured sourceType, ApiCodeSample.id as sourceId, the sample.language, and the current code text (edited or original). Based on the returned success and message, the component shows feedback while separately delegating the actual clipboard copy to client-side mechanisms.

Try-It parameter editing: In the 'Try' tab, user modifies path, query, header, or body fields in the Try-It console; these changes update the component state that will be sent to executeTryItRequest but do not alter URL parameters. If a parameter name matches highlightParam, that field remains visually highlighted even as its value changes.

Execute Try-It request: User submits the Try-It form by clicking a 'Send' or equivalent button; the component calls executeTryItRequest with endpointVersionId, the chosen methodOverride (or leaving it undefined if the default method is used), and all current pathParams, queryParams, headers, and body. The response is rendered with status, duration, headers, and response body; errorMessage is displayed if success is false.

Parameter highlight via docs: User clicks on a parameter name in the docs parameters table; this sets highlightParam in the URL to that parameter name and causes re-rendering of docs and Try-It components to apply highlight styles to matching parameter rows/fields.

Outgoing navigation click – Authentication: User clicks an inline authentication link near an endpoint’s authorization information; the page navigates to authentication.html with a method query parameter referencing a defaultAuthMethodId determined at runtime.

Outgoing navigation click – Rate Limits: User clicks 'View rate limits for this endpoint'; the page navigates to rate_limits.html with endpointId set to the currently selected ApiEndpoint.id, method set to the active httpMethod (from ApiEndpoint or ApiEndpointVersion), and path set to the corresponding pathTemplate or endpointVersion.path.

Outgoing navigation click – SDK: User clicks 'View Python SDK example'; the page navigates to sdk_python.html with sdkId, module, and method parameters that reference the appropriate SDK structures linked to the selected ApiEndpoint.
Outgoing navigation click – Webhooks: User clicks 'Learn about webhooks'; the page navigates to webhooks.html without additional query parameters.
Home navigation: User clicks the logo or breadcrumb; the page navigates to index.html, resetting the context to the documentation home.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for cross-session and deep-linkable state, without relying on localStorage. On initial load and on any browser navigation event that changes the query string, the page controller parses the following parameters and updates internal state accordingly: 1) resource: If provided, the controller compares its value against ApiResource.slug values returned by getApiResourcesForSidebar; when a match is found, that resource is selected and getApiEndpointsForResource is called with the corresponding resourceId. If no match is found or the parameter is absent, the controller selects a default resource based on the server-defined ordering (e.g., the first in the list). 2) endpointId: After selecting a resource and loading its endpoints, if endpointId matches an ApiEndpoint.id within that resource, that endpoint becomes the active selection. If endpointId is missing or does not match, the controller selects a default endpoint (e.g., the first sorted by sidebarOrder for that resource). When the active endpoint changes, the controller updates the endpointId parameter in the URL and resets version and view as needed. 3) version: Once an endpoint is selected, getApiEndpointVersions is called with endpointId. The controller chooses an active endpointVersionId: if version is present and matches one of the returned versions.apiVersion values, the corresponding version entry is used; otherwise the entry whose id equals defaultVersionId is used. On every version change, the controller updates the version parameter to the selected apiVersion and fetches fresh data for that version via getApiEndpointVersionDetails, getApiEndpointCodeSamples, and getTryItConsoleConfig (for consoles that are enabled). 4) view: The current endpoint sub-view is controlled by the view parameter, whose supported values include 'docs', 'code', and 'try'. If view is absent on load, the controller selects a default: if consoleOpen equals 'true' and the console is enabled for the active endpoint version, 'try' is used; otherwise 'docs' is used. Whenever the user selects a tab, the controller updates view to the corresponding value. Sub-components render conditionally based on this value. 5) consoleOpen: On initial load, if consoleOpen equals 'true' and the active endpoint version has a Try-It console enabled (from getTryItConsoleConfig), the 'Try' sub-view is selected if view is not already explicitly set. The controller does not need to persist consoleOpen after that; subsequent user interactions may change view without altering consoleOpen. 6) highlightParam: When present, this parameter holds a parameter name string. The docs and Try-It components both receive this value and apply highlight styles to any ApiParameter or console control whose name matches highlightParam. When the user clicks a parameter in the docs table to focus it, the controller sets or updates highlightParam in the URL with that parameter name. Clearing the highlight (via a UI action) removes the highlightParam parameter from the URL or sets it to an empty value; components respond by removing highlight styles. 7) lang: This parameter controls the active code sample language tab. After getApiEndpointCodeSamples returns its ApiCodeSample array, the code-samples component checks whether lang matches any sample.language; if so, that sample becomes active. If not, the sample where isDefault is true becomes active. When the user changes languages, lang is updated to the selected language. Changing endpoints or versions will try to reuse lang when possible; if the chosen language is not available on the new version, the default sample is used and lang may be updated accordingly. 8) Combined changes: When the user interacts with multiple controls (e.g., changing an endpoint, then a version, then a view), each control updates its corresponding query parameter while preserving others when still valid. If a selected endpoint no longer supports a previously selected version or view (for example, no Try-It console), the controller adjusts dependent parameters (e.g., resetting view from 'try' to 'docs' and removing consoleOpen). The current URL thus always reflects a complete, shareable representation of the active resource, endpointId, version, view, lang, consoleOpen (optional), and highlightParam (optional).

components.functionality: Orchestrates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing and synchronization, selection of the active resource, endpoint, endpoint version, and sub-view, and passes derived state to all child components. On load and on any URL change, it calls getApiResourcesForSidebar, getApiEndpointsForResource for the currently selected ApiResource, getApiEndpointVersions for the selected ApiEndpoint, and then getApiEndpointVersionDetails, getApiEndpointCodeSamples, and getTryItConsoleConfig (if applicable) for the active ApiEndpointVersion. It also updates URL parameters when child components request changes to resource, endpointId, version, view, lang, or highlightParam.

Provides a search input in the global header that allows users to search across documentation. On submit, it calls searchDocumentation with the user-entered query and optional filters and then either displays results inline or triggers navigation to a dedicated search results context using the returned Page and entity identifiers.

Displays a hierarchical list of API resources and their endpoints. Uses getApiResourcesForSidebar to list ApiResource entries associated with the API Reference page and calls getApiEndpointsForResource when a resource is selected to populate its ApiEndpoint children. It reflects the current selection based on URL parameters and notifies the root controller when the user selects a different resource or endpoint so that URL parameters (resource, endpointId) and central state are updated.

Shows the selected endpoint’s primary metadata, including HTTP method, path, name, summary, and active API version. It receives the current ApiEndpoint and active ApiEndpointVersion from the root controller and renders them. This component also surfaces inline actions like 'View rate limits for this endpoint' and 'Authentication' which trigger configured outgoing navigations based on the current endpoint/version.

Allows the user to select among available versions of the current endpoint. It uses the versions information returned from getApiEndpointVersions (provided by the root controller) to populate options labeled by apiVersion. When the user selects a version, it triggers a version change event that causes the root controller to update the URL version parameter and refetch version-specific data.

Controls which sub-view for the endpoint is visible: documentation ('docs'), code samples ('code'), or Try-It console ('try'). It reads the current view state from the root controller (reflecting the URL view parameter) and ensures that only supported tabs (for example, 'code' only when hasCodeSamples is true; 'try' only when the console is enabled) are enabled. When a user selects a tab, it triggers an event to update the view parameter in the URL.

Displays the detailed documentation for the active endpoint version, including description, request and response schemas, and a parameter table. It uses getApiEndpointVersionDetails (via data provided by the root controller) to access endpointVersion, endpoint, parameters, requestSchemaJson, and responseSchemaJson. It highlights any ApiParameter whose name matches the current highlightParam and exposes an interaction on each parameter to set highlightParam in the URL for shareable links.

Shows multi-language code samples (request and response) for the active endpoint version. It receives the list of ApiCodeSample entries from getApiEndpointCodeSamples via the root controller. It uses the URL lang parameter to determine the active language tab and falls back to the sample marked isDefault when necessary. It supports inline editing for samples with isEditable true and enables a 'Copy' action for samples where hasCopyButton is true by calling performCopyCodeSnippet with the sample id, language, and current snippet text.

Provides an interactive API console for the active endpoint version when enabled. It uses getTryItConsoleConfig to obtain the TryItConsole configuration, the relevant ApiParameter list, and a sampleRequest template. It renders form fields for pathParams, queryParams, headers, and bodyJson and highlights any fields whose name matches highlightParam. When the user submits the form, it calls executeTryItRequest with the current configuration and displays the response (status, duration, headers, and body). It also offers an inline link to the Authentication page if supportsAuth is true.

Manages all inline navigational links from the endpoint context to other documentation pages, including Authentication, Rate Limits, SDK pages, Webhooks, and Docs home. It consumes the current ApiEndpoint, ApiEndpointVersion, and any contextual SDK/auth configuration to construct the correct target URLs and query parameters as defined in the outgoing navigation rules, without calling additional data interfaces.

Coordinates the highlightParam URL parameter with parameter-focused UI in both the docs and Try-It views. It listens for parameter focus events (such as clicking a parameter row in endpoint-docs-view), updates the highlightParam query parameter, and notifies relevant components when the value changes so they can apply or remove highlight styles consistently.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a fixed full-width top header, and beneath it a two-column layout: a narrow left sidebar for API resources and a wider right main content column for the active endpoint. Inside the main column, content flows linearly from the endpoint header (title + metadata) to the view tabs, then to a single active view (Docs, Code Samples, or Try-It Console), followed by secondary content like related links. Only one of the three main views is visible at a time, maintaining a clear, stacked vertical reading order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a fixed full-width top header, and beneath it a two-column layout: a narrow left sidebar for API resources and a wider right main content column for the active endpoint. Inside the main column, content flows linearly from the endpoint header (title + metadata) to the view tabs, then to a single active view (Docs, Code Samples, or Try-It Console), followed by secondary content like related links. Only one of the three main views is visible at a time, maintaining a clear, stacked vertical reading order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes about 10–12% of the viewport height and spans 100% width. Below it, the content container is centered with generous horizontal margins (about 48–64px from viewport edges). The left sidebar uses roughly 18–22% of the content width; the main content column uses the remaining 78–82%. Within the main column, the endpoint header and tabs occupy minimal vertical height, leaving most of the vertical space to the active content view, which can scroll independently with the page.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout follows a moderately spacious rhythm: major sections (header vs main content, endpoint header vs tabs, tabs vs active view, main view vs related links) are separated by 24–32px. Within sections, elements like text blocks, form fields, and code samples are separated by 8–16px. Lines of text are kept to an optimal reading width, and panels (docs content, code blocks, console sections) have comfortable internal padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). Only a few interactive elements are visible at once to avoid visual overload.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the dark, fixed header with the logo and prominent search, then drops to the main content column’s top (endpoint header). From there the eye moves down through the horizontal view tabs and into the large active panel (Docs, Code, or Console). The left sidebar runs parallel as a lighter, secondary vertical reference but never competes with the main column. Within panels, any horizontal groupings (like tabs, HTTP method badges, or language selectors) are simple and clean, supporting but not breaking the dominant top-to-bottom flow.

layout.overall_description: The API Reference page presents a modern, restrained developer documentation interface. At the very top, a fixed, full-width header bar anchors the experience, with a left-aligned logo and title on a dark background and a right-aligned, prominent search field. Below this header, the page content sits in a centered container with generous left and right margins. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: a narrow sidebar on the left listing resources like 'Questions Collection', and a wider main content column on the right showing details for the currently selected endpoint. The main column begins with a concise endpoint header bar that includes the endpoint’s name and HTTP method on the left and the version selector on the right, separated from the rest of the page by subtle spacing and a fine bottom border. Immediately below this, a horizontal row of tabs allows the user to switch between Documentation, Code Samples, and Try It views. Only one of these views is visible at a time, occupying most of the vertical space in the main column. Each view appears as a spacious, full-width panel: Documentation presents a structured narrative of parameters and responses, Code Samples shows tabbed language snippets in a card-like code area, and the Try It console displays an organized request builder above a response area. At the bottom of the main column, a lighter related-links section offers navigation to neighboring endpoints or resources. Throughout, whitespace and consistent spacing keep the UI calm and legible, with the left sidebar and related links remaining visually secondary to the central endpoint content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the overall page frame. It places a fixed, full-width top header at the very top of the viewport and a centered content container beneath it. The content container spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, with 48–64px of whitespace on each side, and is aligned centrally. Within this container, the root controller splits the space into a two-column layout starting just below the header: a narrow left sidebar occupying about 18–22% of the container width, and a wider main content column occupying the remaining 78–82%. Both columns start at the same vertical position beneath the header, with about 24–32px of top margin to create breathing room. The main content column scrolls with the page, maintaining a clear vertical stack of endpoint header, tabs, active view panel, and related links. The entire composition is aligned to an implicit 12-column grid, with the sidebar aligned to the left grid boundary and the main column aligned to the remaining columns on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the base layout. The header is fixed to the top; the two-column content area scrolls as a single document. The grid and column proportions remain stable across typical desktop widths, with responsive adjustments at narrower breakpoints (e.g., sidebar collapsing to a drawer).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits inside the fixed top header bar, on the right half, aligned vertically with the header’s content. The header is approximately 64–80px tall (about 10–12% of viewport height), and the search form occupies roughly 35–45% of the header’s horizontal content area (e.g., 320–480px on a standard desktop). It is positioned so that there is comfortable horizontal spacing from both the right edge of the viewport (about 24–32px) and from any navigation items or logo on its left (about 24px). Vertically, the search field is centered in the header’s height, with 8–12px of padding inside the input for a generous click target. The search box does not touch the header’s top or bottom edges; there is at least 12–16px of visible header background above and below it, reinforcing the spacious design DNA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the top header, always visible as the user scrolls. It maintains its position relative to the logo and header content, shrinking slightly in width on smaller viewports but staying aligned to the right side of the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API resource sidebar occupies the full height of the content area on the left side, beginning directly below the header and aligned to the left edge of the centered content container. It spans about 18–22% of the container width, leaving the remainder for the main column. At the top of the sidebar, there is a 'Reference' label with a small section title style, followed by 16–24px of vertical space before the list of resource links (e.g., 'Questions Collection'). Each resource link is set as a horizontal row with comfortable vertical padding (8–12px each) and subtle hover states, stacking from top to bottom. There is at least 24px of padding between the sidebar’s content and its left and right edges, so the links don’t hug the sides. The sidebar presents a simple, linear vertical navigation column parallel to the main content, helping orient the user without competing visually with the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned below the fixed header and may use a sticky behavior so that its top aligns just under the header and remains visible as the user scrolls. It scrolls only when the sidebar’s content exceeds the viewport height, otherwise it remains static in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the right main content column, the endpoint header bar is the first visible element at the top. It is horizontally full-width within the main column (spanning roughly 78–82% of the content container) and is positioned about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the header. The bar itself is a low-height horizontal strip (about 56–72px tall) that contains the endpoint’s HTTP method badge and name on the left, and the version selector on the far right. The title area on the left starts flush with the main column’s left padding (16–24px from the column edge), and the version selector is right-aligned with a similar horizontal padding from the main column’s right edge. The header bar has a subtle bottom border or a very light background tint to delineate it from the content below, but it preserves the minimalist, flat style. Internally, it uses 12–16px vertical padding so text is not cramped. It visually anchors the endpoint context at the top of the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content; it is not fixed. When the user scrolls down into documentation or console content, this bar moves off-screen, maintaining a simple scrolling document feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The endpoint version selector is positioned inside the endpoint-header-bar, anchored to the right side. It appears as a compact dropdown or segmented control that occupies about 15–20% of the header bar width (roughly 160–220px on desktop), aligned so its right edge is 16–24px from the main column’s right boundary. The selector is vertically centered within the header bar, matching the baseline or vertical center of the endpoint title on the left. There is at least 16px of horizontal space between the rightmost part of the title area and the left edge of the selector to avoid crowding. The control is visually minimal (flat border, neutral tones), ensuring it reads as an interactive element but does not overpower the endpoint name.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the endpoint-header-bar. It moves with the header bar as the user scrolls and does not change size except in responsive layouts, where it may compress horizontally but stays right-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view tabs sit directly beneath the endpoint-header-bar, spanning the full width of the main content column. They are positioned with about 16–24px of vertical space below the header bar’s bottom border, reinforcing the separation between metadata and content controls. The tabs are arranged in a horizontal row starting at the left edge of the main column’s content area, aligned to the same left padding as the endpoint title. Typical labels are 'Documentation', 'Code Samples', and 'Try It'. Each tab has generous horizontal padding (16–24px) and vertical padding (8–12px) and is separated from the content below by a fine tab underline or indicator that marks the active view. There is 8–12px of vertical space between the bottom of the tab row and the start of whichever main panel is active, so the tabs feel visually attached but not cramped against the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column and scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls down. Only one tab is active at a time, and the corresponding content panel directly below expands to fill the available vertical space. No separate scrolling regions are created; the page scroll remains unified.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The documentation view occupies the full width of the main content column below the endpoint-view-tabs when the 'Documentation' tab is active. It begins about 8–12px beneath the tabs and extends downward, forming a vertically stacked narrative section. The panel maintains 16–24px horizontal padding from the main column’s edges, and uses 24–32px vertical spacing between major subsections (e.g., 'Introduction', 'Parameters', 'Responses') and 8–16px between paragraphs, lists, and inline elements. Any parameter rows or example blocks are presented as full-width rows aligned to the main text block, mirroring the horizontally stretched card aesthetic from the mockup: left-aligned labels, possibly with subtle colored accents, and right-side affordances if needed. The text line length is kept moderate to enhance readability, and there are no unnecessary borders; instead, whitespace and consistent typography define the structure. This view is typically the default active panel and tall enough to require scrolling through the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed only when the corresponding tab is active. It expands naturally as content grows, pushing the related links section downward. It scrolls with the main page and does not introduce its own internal scrollbars except where necessary for long code snippets, which may scroll horizontally within their own area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the 'Code Samples' tab is active, this view replaces the docs panel in the same spatial slot beneath the tabs, occupying the full width of the main content column. At the very top of this panel, aligned to the left padding, is a horizontal language selector (small tabs or pill buttons) that spans about 40–60% of the panel width. There is 8–12px of space below the language selector before the code block area begins. The main code block appears as a single, wide, card-like panel with a subtle background contrast and rounded corners, stretching nearly the full width of the main column but leaving 16–24px of internal padding around the code text. Vertical spacing within the panel is modest (8–12px) so the code remains compact yet not cramped. If multiple sample sections exist (e.g., request vs response), they are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them. Overall, the code samples view mirrors the clean, horizontal card rows from the mockup, but in a vertical stack specialized for code.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Exclusive visibility with the docs and console views: visible only when its tab is active. It scrolls with the page as a single block and may include internal vertical scrollbars within individual code blocks if their content exceeds a comfortable height, but the primary scrolling experience remains the main document scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Try It console view also occupies the same region beneath the endpoint-view-tabs, full-width within the main column, visible when the 'Try It' tab is selected. The panel is structured as a vertical stack: a request configuration area on top and a response output area below. The request section includes input fields for path parameters, query parameters, headers, and body, arranged in compact but spacious blocks: each group is a full-width section with a subtle divide (16–24px vertical separation) and uses 8–12px vertical padding within each field row. The response area sits 24–32px below the last request control, appearing as a wide, card-like panel that stretches nearly the full column width with internal padding and perhaps a subtle border, clearly separated from the request controls. Both sections use a clean, flat design with minimal borders, relying on whitespace and grouping to convey structure. The entire console view is tall and scrollable, but the layout ensures fields are not crowded and remain vertically aligned for quick scanning.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed only when its tab is active. It scrolls within the main page flow and does not introduce separate fixed or floating sub-panels by default. Request and response areas are part of the same scrollable panel, maintaining the linear vertical reading pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related links section appears at the bottom of the main content column, underneath whichever main view (Docs, Code Samples, or Try It) is currently active. It is separated from the active view by a generous top margin (about 32–40px), visually signaling that it is a secondary, concluding section. The related links panel spans the full width of the main column, with consistent horizontal padding (16–24px) matching the content above. It starts with a small heading such as 'Related endpoints' or 'See also', followed by 8–16px of vertical space and then a vertical list of simple link rows. Each link row has 8–12px vertical padding and is left-aligned, similar in density to sidebar links but integrated into the main column. There are no heavy borders; any separation between links is handled by light dividers or simple spacing. The entire section feels lighter and less prominent than the primary content, acting as a gentle navigational footer within the endpoint view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main column content. It moves downward as more content is added above and scrolls as part of the main page, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual coordination layer rather than a visible UI block. Conceptually, it is aligned with the main content column and spans the vertical region where parameters and their uses appear: the documentation view’s parameter tables and the Try It console’s input fields. It does not occupy any explicit space on the page; instead, it enables synchronized highlighting of parameter names and related elements across views, so that hovering or focusing on a parameter in one context can subtly highlight its counterpart elsewhere within the visible content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial from the user’s perspective. It overlays interaction logic on existing components without creating its own container. It follows the scroll position of the main content in the sense that it responds only to elements currently in the DOM and view but does not alter layout, spacing, or positioning.

name: Guides Overview
title: Guides Overview
description: Displays a categorized overview of developer guides and lets users quickly find and open the guide that fits their current task.

core_features: Fetch and display categorized guides using the getGuidesOverview interface.
Allow users to switch between guide categories and see the relevant guides for the selected category.
Support deep-linking into specific categories and guides via URL parameters (category and guide).
Highlight and auto-scroll to a specific guide when the guide URL parameter is present.
Provide an in-page documentation search for guides and related docs using the searchDocumentation interface.
Navigate to the full documentation page for a selected guide (including rate limit, webhook, or authentication guides) based on the guide’s page type.
user_workflows:
Browse guides by category:
1) User navigates to guides.html without URL parameters.
2) The page-init component calls getGuidesOverview.
3) The first available category from guideCategories is selected by default.
4) The guide-category-selector shows all categories and highlights the selected one.
5) The guide-list component displays all guides for the selected category.
6) User clicks a different category; the category selector updates the URL category parameter and the guide list re-renders for the new category.

Open a specific guide from another page using deep link:
1) User clicks a link on index.html or faq.html that points to guides.html with category and/or guide query parameters.
2) On load, the page-init component reads the category and guide parameters from the URL.
3) The page calls getGuidesOverview.
4) After data is loaded, the selected category is set based on the category parameter (matching categoryId or another configured identifier).
5) The guide-list component renders the selected category’s guides and highlights the guide whose identifier matches the guide parameter (for example, matching pageId).
6) The page scrolls to this guide entry.
7) If the user clicks this guide entry, the browser navigates to the target documentation page for that guide (e.g., rate limits, webhooks, authentication, or other guide pages).

Use search to find a relevant guide:
1) User types a phrase into the guides-search-form search field.
2) On submit, the search-form component calls searchDocumentation with the query and optional filters to restrict results to guide-related content.
3) searchDocumentation returns results, including title, snippet, pageId, pageType, entityType, and entityId.
4) The search-results-list component displays the results without hardcoding any page names.
5) If the user clicks a result that corresponds to a guide already listed on this overview page, the component updates the URL guide parameter and scrolls to that guide entry.
6) If the user clicks a result that corresponds to another documentation page (for example, an external guide or detailed reference), the component navigates the browser to that page using the site’s routing rules for the result’s pageId and pageType.

Navigate specifically to rate limits, webhooks, or authentication guides from the overview:
1) User locates a guide entry in the guide-list that was returned by getGuidesOverview.
2) When they click the entry, the guide-list component inspects the guide’s pageType.
3) If the pageType indicates a rate limits page, the browser is navigated to the rate limits documentation page (e.g., rate_limits.html) according to the application’s routing for that pageType.
4) If the pageType indicates a webhooks-related page, the browser is navigated to the appropriate webhooks documentation page.
5) If the pageType indicates an authentication page, the browser is navigated to the authentication documentation page.
6) For any other pageType, the router navigates to the corresponding documentation page for that guide.
interactions: On page load, the page-init component reads the category and guide URL parameters, calls getGuidesOverview, and initializes the selected category and optional focused guide.

When the user clicks a category in the guide-category-selector, the selected category state is updated, the category URL parameter is updated (using the browser’s history API), and the guide-list is re-rendered to show guides for that category.
When the user clicks a guide in the guide-list, the component triggers navigation to the underlying documentation page for that guide using the site’s routing based on pageId and pageType.

If the user navigates using browser back/forward buttons and the category or guide parameters change, the page-init logic re-evaluates the URL parameters and updates the selected category and highlighted guide accordingly.
When the user types in the guides-search-form and submits the search, searchDocumentation is called with the query and optional filters; results are bound into the search-results-list component.

When the user clicks a search result corresponding to a guide shown in the overview, the URL’s category and guide parameters are updated (if needed), the selected category is changed, and the page scrolls to the specific guide entry instead of navigating away.

When the user clicks a search result corresponding to another documentation page (not represented as a guide entry in the overview), the browser navigates directly to that page using the routing inferred from pageId and pageType.
page_functionality.state_logic: Initial state is determined from URL query parameters and API responses. On load, the page parses the category and guide query parameters from the guides.html URL. It then calls getGuidesOverview. Once the response is available, the page determines the selected category: if a category parameter is present and matches a categoryId or another configured identifier from guideCategories, that category is selected; otherwise the first category from guideCategories is selected. The selected category is maintained in in-memory state and is also reflected in the URL by updating the category parameter when the user changes categories. For the guide parameter, after data is loaded, the page searches across all guide entries to find the one whose identifier (for example, pageId) matches the guide parameter. If a match is found, the corresponding category is set as selected, the matched guide entry is marked as focused or highlighted in the guide-list, and the page scrolls to that entry. The guide parameter is updated whenever the user jumps to a specific guide via search or direct interaction. If the URL changes because of browser navigation (back/forward) and category or guide parameters differ from the current state, the page re-runs the selection and highlighting logic without reloading the page. All state changes that should be deep-linkable (selected category, focused guide) are mirrored in the URL parameters; no localStorage or other persistent client-side storage is used. Search state (search query and returned results) can remain in memory for the current page session and does not need to be encoded into URL parameters unless required by the broader application.

components.functionality: Initializes the Guides Overview page by reading URL parameters, calling getGuidesOverview, and setting the selected category and optional focused guide based on the response.
Displays introductory content for the Guides Overview page based on introMarkdown and ensures it reflects any conceptual guidance about how to use the guides.
Renders the list of guide categories returned by getGuidesOverview, allows the user to select a category, and updates the selected category state and URL parameter.

Displays guides for the currently selected category, highlights the guide specified by the guide URL parameter, supports scrolling to that guide, and handles clicks to navigate to the full guide documentation page.
Provides a search input specific to the Guides Overview context. On submit, it calls searchDocumentation with the user’s query and optional filters to focus on guide-related content.

Displays documentation search results returned by searchDocumentation. Clicking a result either deep-links to a guide entry on this page (by updating category and guide URL parameters and scrolling) or navigates to another documentation page based on the result’s pageId and pageType.
Listens for changes to the guide URL parameter and the loaded guide data, computes which guide is targeted, scrolls to that guide within the guide-list, and applies a highlighted state.

Centralizes navigation logic when a guide entry or search result is clicked. It inspects the target item’s pageType and pageId to route to the appropriate documentation page or to update category and guide URL parameters for in-page navigation.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup within a two-column body: a fixed global top navigation bar spans the full width, and below it, a centered content container is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content column. Inside the main column, the order is: page title with underline, a short introduction paragraph, then the guides search form, then the category selector, and finally the guide list (or search results list when active). The left sidebar mirrors the mockup’s reference navigation: a section label (e.g., 'Guides') at the top, followed by a concise vertical list of links representing guide categories or anchor sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup within a two-column body: a fixed global top navigation bar spans the full width, and below it, a centered content container is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content column. Inside the main column, the order is: page title with underline, a short introduction paragraph, then the guides search form, then the category selector, and finally the guide list (or search results list when active). The left sidebar mirrors the mockup’s reference navigation: a section label (e.g., 'Guides') at the top, followed by a concise vertical list of links representing guide categories or anchor sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top navigation bar occupies about 10–12% of the viewport height and is fixed to the top. The content area below uses a central container that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving comfortable gutters on both sides. Inside that container, the left sidebar takes approximately 18–22% of the width, while the main content column uses the remaining 78–82%. The main column uses a fixed maximum width feel (roughly 880–1040px) to keep line lengths readable. Vertical spacing between major sections (title, introduction, search, category selector, lists) follows a 24–32px rhythm, while spacing between smaller elements (labels, paragraphs, list items) uses 8–16px increments.
