layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains a moderately spacious density: each major block (intro section, search form, category selector, lists) has ample whitespace around it, with no more than a handful of elements visible at a time. Text lines are kept short by constraining the main content width. Lists of guides or results are composed of horizontally stretched, single-line cards with comfortable internal padding, echoing the endpoint row styling from the mockup. Nothing feels crowded; there is clear breathing room between cards and around each section, even on smaller screens.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts at the dark, fixed top navigation bar, then drops directly to the bold 'Guides Overview' page title in the main column. The eye continues downward to the short introduction, then immediately to the search form as the primary interaction point, then to the category selector, and finally to the list of guides or search results. The left sidebar forms a secondary, supportive vertical track: its smaller text and lighter styling keep it subordinate but always visible, functioning like a table of contents. Within each guide row, the colored vertical bar on the left and label text draw the eye first, then the horizontal flow carries attention to the right-aligned chevron icon, signaling that the row is clickable.

layout.overall_description: Under a fixed, full-width top navigation bar, the page content sits inside a centered container that splits into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content column. The main column begins with a strong 'Guides Overview' page title aligned to the left, underlined by a subtle horizontal rule, followed by a short explanatory paragraph that sets context. Just below, a wide search form spans most of the column’s width, visually inviting users to start typing. The category selector sits beneath the search box, presenting guide categories as neatly spaced, pill-like filters aligned from left to right. Below that, the primary content is a vertically stacked list of guide rows that stretch almost the full width of the column, each row echoing the mockup’s endpoint cards: a thin colored status bar on the far left, guide title and short meta info just to its right, and a right-facing chevron aligned at the far right. When a search is performed, a search results list replaces the main guide list in the same vertical space, preserving the visual rhythm. To the left, the sidebar runs from just under the header down toward the bottom of the viewport, listing guide categories or key sections in a compact vertical list under a 'Guides' label. The entire layout feels modern and restrained, with generous vertical spacing, clear hierarchy, and an easy top-to-bottom reading path supported by a secondary left-hand navigation spine.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component defines the structural page frame beneath the global navigation. Directly under the fixed top header (occupying about 10–12% of the viewport height), it places a centered content container that spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width, leaving equal left and right gutters so content never touches the edges. Within this container, it establishes a two-column layout: a left sidebar column occupying about 18–22% of the container width and a right main content column using the remaining 78–82%. Both columns extend from just below the header down to the bottom of the page content, with the main column controlling the overall page height through its scrollable content. The top edge of the main column content begins with a 24–32px offset from the bottom of the header to maintain breathing room, and all inner content respects an internal horizontal padding of roughly 24px on both sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines a static, scrollable two-column layout under a fixed top navigation; content scrolls vertically while the header remains fixed at the top. The columns themselves are fixed in relative width but responsive within the central container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned at the very top of the right main content column, this block establishes the page identity. It starts approximately 24–32px below the bottom edge of the fixed top navigation bar, aligned flush with the left padding of the main column. The section spans the full usable width of the main column (about 100% of that column’s width, excluding internal padding). The first line is a bold page title, 'Guides Overview', with font size larger than any sidebar text, followed immediately by a thin horizontal underline that stretches about 25–40% of the column width, anchored to the left. Below the underline, there is 8–16px of vertical space before a concise introductory paragraph that extends no more than 70–80% of the main column width to keep line length comfortable. A 24–32px gap separates the bottom of this intro paragraph from the next major element (the search form), ensuring the section feels distinct and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the main content, acting as the starting point of the main column’s vertical flow. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form sits directly beneath the introduction section in the main content column, separated from the intro paragraph by approximately 24px of vertical space. It is left-aligned with the main column content and spans about 60–70% of the main column’s width, keeping it prominent while leaving some empty space on the right to maintain a light, open feel. The search input itself is a single horizontal field with subtle borders, roughly 40–48px high, with 12–16px horizontal padding inside. If a search icon or button is present, it sits to the right inside or immediately adjacent to the input, but the entire control remains on a single line. There is about 16–24px of vertical space below the search form before the category selector begins, visually separating interaction areas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with main content. Remains in a fixed position relative to the intro section; no sticky behavior, but its large horizontal footprint makes it a clear interaction anchor early in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears directly below the search form, separated by a 16–24px top margin. It is aligned to the left padding of the main column and can extend up to 100% of the column width, though the actual interactive elements sit comfortably within, leaving some whitespace on the right. The selector presents guide categories as a horizontal row (or two rows, if necessary) of pill-shaped filters or simple text tabs. Each pill has consistent horizontal padding (around 12–16px) and vertical padding (around 6–8px), with 8–12px gaps between pills to maintain the airy, minimalist look. When the categories wrap to a second line on narrower widths, the vertical gap between lines stays around 8px. The entire selector block occupies only enough vertical space to fit one or two rows of pills, with approximately 24px of space below it before the guide list or search results list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with main content, remaining anchored just above the listing area. No sticky behavior, but its position between search and list creates a natural filtering control zone.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guide list occupies the main content area directly beneath the category selector, sitting about 24px below it. It spans nearly the full width of the main column (roughly 100% of the inner width minus the column’s side padding), forming a strong vertical stack of guide entries. Each guide entry mirrors the mockup’s endpoint rows: a horizontally stretched card about 48–56px tall, with 12–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding. On the far left edge of each card runs a thin vertical colored bar, about 4–6px wide and full card height, indicating type, difficulty, or status. Immediately to the right of this bar is the guide’s main text block (title and optional short descriptor on a second line), left-aligned and occupying roughly 70–80% of the card width. On the far right, aligned vertically centered, sits a right-pointing chevron icon, visually separated from the text by at least 16–24px of padding. Cards are separated from each other by 8–12px of vertical space, forming a clean, consistent rhythm down the page. The list continues vertically as needed, driving the main scroll of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Forms the main scrollable content in the right column. The list grows vertically with content, pushing the page height. It is mutually exclusive with the search results list in the same space (only one is shown at a time).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results list occupies the same spatial region as the guide list, effectively replacing it when search results are available. It begins at the same vertical position—approximately 24px below the category selector—and spans the same width of the main content column. Each result is styled as a horizontally stretched card matching the guide list entries: a thin colored bar on the extreme left, text block next to it, and a right-aligned chevron. Internal padding (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and vertical gaps between cards (8–12px) are identical to maintain visual continuity. The main difference is content density: result titles are prioritized, and any additional metadata may be displayed on a subtle second line, but the overall height per card remains similar to the guide list entries to preserve rhythm. When present, this list pushes the lower content further down and becomes the primary scrollable region in the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in place of the standard guide list when a search is active, occupying the same vertical and horizontal bounds. It scrolls with the page and can collapse or disappear to reveal the original guide list when search is cleared.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual behavioral layer aligned conceptually with both the main content column and the left sidebar. It tracks the vertical positions of key sections within the main column—such as category groups or major guide clusters—and synchronizes these with the corresponding links in the left sidebar. As users scroll through the right-hand content, this manager effectively overlays the content area, mapping scroll positions to sidebar items so that the current section’s sidebar link is highlighted. Its influence is most apparent along the left edge of the page where the sidebar sits, causing appropriate items to adopt a slightly bolder or darker style as their corresponding content enters the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, but operates relative to the viewport and the scrollable main content. It assumes the sidebar itself behaves like a static column under the fixed header, potentially with internal scrolling if the list grows taller than the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is also non-visual and conceptually spans the entire interactive region of the page, covering both the left sidebar and the main content column. It listens for navigation actions—clicking on a guide entry in the list or search results, or choosing a link in the left sidebar—and then drives appropriate navigation: either opening the selected guide (possibly in a new page or detailed view) or smoothly scrolling the main content column to the corresponding section. In the mental layout picture, it sits above all interactive cards and sidebar links as an invisible interaction layer, ensuring that every clickable row with a chevron and every sidebar item is consistently handled.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial in the traditional sense, but functionally tied to the entire scrollable body area beneath the fixed header. It does not occupy its own layout space; instead, it orchestrates navigational behavior across existing visible components.

name: Rate Limits
title: Rate Limits
description: Documentation page that explains global and per-endpoint rate limiting, exposes rate limit headers and retry behavior, and provides a searchable table of rate limit rules.

core_features: Load and display rate limit explanatory content (intro, global rules, headers, retry guidance, best practices) using getRateLimitsPageContent.
Load and display a table of rate limit rules associated with this page using getRateLimitRules, including endpoint metadata (name, method, path).
Support filtering and searching of rate limit rules by HTTP method, path, scope, and endpointId using searchRateLimitRules.
Honor incoming URL parameters (endpointId, method, path) to pre-filter or highlight relevant rate limit rules.
Provide navigation from a rate limit rule row to the related API Reference endpoint details (api_reference.html) using outgoing URL parameters.
Expose inline documentation search using searchDocumentation so users can move from rate limits to other relevant docs.
Present best practices for building clients that respect rate limits and handle 429 responses, using content from getRateLimitsPageContent.
user_workflows:
View global rate limiting behavior and headers:
1. User opens rate_limits.html.
2. The page resolves the current Rate Limits Page.id and calls getRateLimitsPageContent(rateLimitsPageId).
3. The intro, globalRules, headersMarkdown, and retryGuidanceMarkdown are rendered in structured sections.
4. The user can scroll through global rules to understand the overall limits and how rate limit headers are returned.
5. The user can read retryGuidanceMarkdown for guidance on handling throttling and 429 responses.

Find the rate limit for a specific endpoint via deep link:
1. User clicks a rate limits link from api_reference.html, index.html, or faq.html that includes URL parameters such as endpointId, and optionally method and path (e.g., rate_limits.html?endpointId=...&method=...&path=...).
2. On load, the page reads the URL parameters endpointId, method, and path.
3. If any of these are present, the page constructs a filters object and calls searchRateLimitRules(filters) to get a filtered list of rules.
4. The rate limit table displays only (or highlights) rules matching the filters; the filters UI reflects the current URL state.
5. The user confirms the per-time-window limit and any description for the relevant endpoint.
6. If desired, the user clicks the endpoint name or a dedicated link in the row to navigate to api_reference.html with the endpointId passed as a URL parameter.

Scan and filter rate limit rules interactively:
1. User opens rate_limits.html without filters, or clears existing filters.
2. The page calls getRateLimitRules(rateLimitsPageId) to load all rules for the page.
3. The rate limit table displays all rules, with optional client-side pagination or grouping based on the returned data.
4. The user uses filter controls (HTTP method selector, path search input, scope toggle) which update a filters object.
5. When the user submits or changes a filter, the page calls searchRateLimitRules(filters).
6. The results update in the rate limit table; the URL query parameters (endpointId, method, path) are updated to reflect the current filter state.
7. The user may click a rule row to navigate to the corresponding API Reference endpoint or to refine filters further.

Navigate from a rate limit rule to full endpoint documentation:
1. User locates a row in the rate limit table that corresponds to an endpoint of interest.
2. The user clicks the endpoint name or path in the row.
3. The page navigates to api_reference.html with URL parameters { endpointId: selectedRule.endpointId, view: 'docs' }.
4. Alternatively, the user clicks a 'View endpoint details' link in the row, which navigates to api_reference.html with { endpointId: selectedRule.endpointId, highlightParam: 'none' }.
5. The API Reference page uses the endpointId to show the full endpoint documentation.

Search documentation from the Rate Limits page:
1. User enters a search query into an inline documentation search input on the Rate Limits page.
2. On submit, the page calls searchDocumentation(query, filters) where filters can be configured to prioritize rate_limits or api_reference content.
3. Search results are shown in a results panel or redirect flow, each with a title, snippet, and page metadata.
4. The user clicks a result to navigate to the appropriate page using the returned pageId and inferred filename.

Review best practices for handling 429 responses:
1. User scrolls to the 'Best practices' section of the Rate Limits page.
2. Content is loaded from bestPracticesMarkdown returned by getRateLimitsPageContent(rateLimitsPageId).
3. The user reads guidance for implementing client behaviors that respect rate limits and handle 429 responses (e.g., backoff, retries based on headers, concurrency control).
4. The user may then refer back to the rate limit table or other docs using the provided navigation links.
interactions:
Initial page load:
- Trigger: User navigates to rate_limits.html (optionally with endpointId, method, path query parameters).
- Behavior: Page determines rateLimitsPageId (Page.id for this filename), calls getRateLimitsPageContent(rateLimitsPageId) to render explanatory content, and either getRateLimitRules(rateLimitsPageId) or searchRateLimitRules(filters) depending on the presence of URL parameters.
- State: The initial filter state is derived from URL query parameters and used throughout the page.

Filter rate limit rules by HTTP method:
- Trigger: User changes the HTTP method selection in the filter controls (e.g., a dropdown or segmented control).
- Behavior: The filter state is updated with the selected method; a filters object is constructed and searchRateLimitRules(filters) is called.
- State: The method URL parameter is updated to match the current selection; if the method is cleared, the parameter is removed.

Filter rate limit rules by path substring or pattern:
- Trigger: User types into the path search input and submits or pauses, depending on chosen UX.
- Behavior: The filter state is updated with the path value; a filters object is constructed and searchRateLimitRules(filters) is called.
- State: The path URL parameter is updated to match the current search string; if cleared, it is removed from the URL.

Focus the table on a specific endpoint via endpointId:
- Trigger: User arrives with an endpointId in the URL, or selects an endpoint via another UI element within this page.
- Behavior: The filter state includes endpointId; searchRateLimitRules(filters) is called, and matching rows are displayed (others may be hidden or visually de-emphasized based on functional requirements).
- State: The endpointId URL parameter persists while the focus is active; clearing focus removes endpointId from the URL and reverts to generic filtering.

Clear all filters:
- Trigger: User clicks a 'Clear filters' or equivalent control.
- Behavior: Filter state is reset to its default (no method, path, or endpointId filters), and getRateLimitRules(rateLimitsPageId) is called to obtain the full rules set.
- State: endpointId, method, and path URL parameters are removed from the URL; the table and filter controls reflect the unfiltered state.

Click a rate limit rule row:
- Trigger: User clicks on a rule row (endpoint name or path).
- Behavior: The row click handler reads selectedRule.endpointId and navigates to api_reference.html with URL parameters { endpointId: selectedRule.endpointId, view: 'docs' }.
- State: The user leaves the Rate Limits page and arrives at the API Reference page focused on the selected endpoint.

Click 'View endpoint details' within a row:
- Trigger: User clicks the explicit 'View endpoint details' action within a rule row.
- Behavior: The click handler navigates to api_reference.html with URL parameters { endpointId: selectedRule.endpointId, highlightParam: 'none' }.
- State: Similar to the row click, but the API Reference page may interpret highlightParam to control its UI.

Use breadcrumb navigation:
- Trigger: User clicks the breadcrumb link pointing to the documentation homepage.
- Behavior: Navigate to index.html without additional parameters.
- State: Any Rate Limits filters are abandoned; the user returns to the docs home.

Use inline documentation search:
- Trigger: User enters a query and submits the search form on the Rate Limits page.
- Behavior: The page calls searchDocumentation(query, filters). Depending on implementation, results may be displayed inline or the user may be redirected to a dedicated search results page using the returned pageId and metadata.
- State: The current Rate Limits filters remain unchanged; search behavior is independent of the rate limit table filters.

Expand or collapse explanatory sections:
- Trigger: User toggles collapsible sections for intro, headers, retry guidance, or best practices.
- Behavior: The content already loaded via getRateLimitsPageContent(rateLimitsPageId) is shown or hidden; no new data is fetched.
- State: Section open/closed state is managed in memory and does not persist to the URL unless explicitly chosen; it does not rely on localStorage.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is primarily driven by the URL query parameters and in-memory component state.

URL parameters:
- endpointId (string): When present, the page treats this as a focus filter for the rate limit rules.
  - On initial load: If endpointId exists in the URL, it is added to the filter state. The page constructs a filters object that includes endpointId and calls searchRateLimitRules(filters) instead of getRateLimitRules.
  - During interaction: When the user selects or clears a specific endpoint focus (e.g., from a UI control), the endpointId parameter is set or removed from the URL accordingly. Clearing the endpoint focus removes this parameter and may revert to getRateLimitRules.

- method (string): Represents the HTTP method filter (values correspond to the allowed HTTP methods defined in RateLimitRule.httpMethod and related models).
  - On initial load: If method exists, it is applied to the filter state, and searchRateLimitRules(filters) is called.
  - On change: When the user selects a method in the filter UI, the method parameter is updated in the URL. Clearing the method filter removes the parameter; the table is refreshed based on the new filter state.

- path (string): Represents a path template or substring filter for endpoints.
  - On initial load: If path exists, it is applied to the filter state used to call searchRateLimitRules(filters).
  - On change: As the user updates the path filter, the path parameter is updated in the URL. Clearing it removes the parameter and triggers a new rules query.

Data loading logic:
- The Rate Limits Page.id (rateLimitsPageId) is determined based on the current Page entity that matches filename 'rate_limits.html' and pageType 'rate_limits'.
- Explanatory content (introMarkdown, globalRules, headersMarkdown, retryGuidanceMarkdown, bestPracticesMarkdown) is always loaded via getRateLimitsPageContent(rateLimitsPageId) on initial page load. This content is static with respect to the query parameters.
- Rate limit rules are loaded using either getRateLimitRules or searchRateLimitRules:
  - If none of endpointId, method, or path are present in the URL, the page calls getRateLimitRules(rateLimitsPageId) to retrieve the full rules list.
  - If any of endpointId, method, or path are present, the page constructs a filters object from the current URL state and calls searchRateLimitRules(filters).
- Subsequent filter changes on the page always go through searchRateLimitRules(filters), and the URL is kept in sync with the filter state by updating endpointId, method, and path parameters.

Navigation logic:
- Clicking a rule row or its 'View endpoint details' link navigates away from rate_limits.html by constructing URLs for api_reference.html with the appropriate endpointId and other parameters as specified in outgoing_connections.
- Clicking the breadcrumb back to the documentation homepage navigates to index.html with no additional query parameters.

No persistent client-side storage is used for this page: all persistent or shareable state is encoded in the URL query parameters. Any additional UI state (e.g., expanded/collapsed sections or client-side table sorting) is held only in memory for the current page session and is not written to localStorage or similar mechanisms.

components.functionality: Top-level controller for the Rate Limits page. Resolves rateLimitsPageId, parses URL parameters, coordinates data loading via getRateLimitsPageContent and either getRateLimitRules or searchRateLimitRules, and passes data and callbacks to child components.
Displays a breadcrumb trail for navigation, including a link back to the documentation homepage. Handles navigation to index.html when the corresponding breadcrumb is clicked.
Renders the functional page title and a short description, using the Page entity and optional introMarkdown excerpt. Does not fetch data directly but receives processed content from the page shell.
Displays introMarkdown and a structured representation of globalRules from getRateLimitsPageContent, showing overall rate limiting behavior and any global constraints.
Renders headersMarkdown and retryGuidanceMarkdown as separate subsections, explaining how to interpret rate limit headers and how to handle retries when a limit is reached.
Displays bestPracticesMarkdown from getRateLimitsPageContent, providing client design guidance for respecting rate limits and handling 429 responses.

Allows users to filter the rate limit rules by HTTP method, path, and endpointId. Initializes its state from URL parameters and propagates changes back to the page shell, which then calls searchRateLimitRules with a constructed filters object.

Displays a list of rate limit rules, either from getRateLimitRules(rateLimitsPageId) or searchRateLimitRules(filters). Each row shows endpointName, primaryHttpMethod, pathTemplate, and values from the associated RateLimitRule. Supports row click and a dedicated 'View endpoint details' action for navigation to api_reference.html.

Represents a single rule in the rate limit table. Binds to a RateLimitRule instance plus associated endpoint metadata (endpointName, primaryHttpMethod, pathTemplate) provided by getRateLimitRules or searchRateLimitRules. Emits events when the row or its 'View endpoint details' control is clicked.

Inline documentation search input allowing users to query across documentation content while staying on the Rate Limits page. On submit, calls searchDocumentation(query, filters) and either displays results inline or triggers navigation based on selected result.
Displays results from searchDocumentation, including title, snippet, and basic metadata for each result. Handles clicks on results to navigate to the relevant documentation pages.

Below these narrative sections, place the interactive rate limit rules area: filter bar (including inline search), then either the searchable rules table or a search results panel. layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed header consumes about 10–12% of the viewport height at the top. Below it, the content area fills the remaining height. Inside this, the left sidebar uses ~20% of the page width and the right main content column uses ~80%, both centered within a max-width container so content doesn’t touch viewport edges. layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the user’s eye from the fixed top header down into the main column: first the breadcrumbs to establish context, then the bold page title with its underline, then through the three explanatory sections in order. Once the conceptual understanding is set, the eye moves down to the interactive rules area: first the filter bar and inline search (as an obvious control surface), then into either the rules table or search results panel. The left sidebar provides a parallel vertical reference for orientation but remains visually lighter and less prominent than the main column. Horizontal micro-flows exist within the filter bar (from left filters to right-aligned search), and within table rows (from left-most identifying column across to the right).

layout.overall_description: The page appears as a modern, minimalist developer documentation screen framed by a fixed, full-width top header and a two-column layout beneath it. Under the header, content is centered on the page in a wide container, with a narrow navigation sidebar on the left and a broad main documentation column on the right. At the very top of the main column sit subtle breadcrumbs, followed by a prominent 'Rate Limits' title and a fine underline that visually anchors the page. Below this, three vertically stacked sections explain global limits, headers and retry behavior, and best practices, each separated by generous vertical spacing so the content feels breathable and easy to scan. The lower half of the main column is dedicated to a clean, searchable rate limit rules area: a horizontal filter bar with inline search spans most of the column, and directly beneath it the rules appear either as a structured table or as a focused search results panel, both stretching nearly the full width of the main column with consistent padding. The left sidebar quietly lists the 'Reference' label with links such as 'Rate Limits', aligning vertically with the main content headings but remaining visually subdued. The overall impression is that of a carefully structured, content-first documentation layout that guides the reader linearly from explanation to exploration without unnecessary decoration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell occupies the full viewport width and height, serving as the structural container for the fixed top header, left navigation sidebar, and right main content column. The shell centers a max-width content container (roughly 1040–1200px wide) within the viewport, leaving equal left and right margins so that neither the sidebar nor the main column touches the screen edges. The top header bar sits fixed at the very top, spanning 100% of the viewport width and about 10–12% of its height. Below the header, the shell splits horizontally into two columns: a left sidebar occupying about 18–22% of the content container width and a main content column occupying the remaining 78–82%. The vertical scroll affects only the content area (sidebar + main column), with the header remaining visible. Within the shell, vertical spacing between major blocks (breadcrumbs, page header, sections, filters, table) follows the 24–32px rhythm, maintaining a consistent, modern documentation feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed header region at the top; scrolling content area beneath. The shell itself is static but manages a fixed-position header and a vertically scrolling two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the main content column, just below the fixed header’s bottom edge. It is left-aligned, spanning approximately 90–100% of the main column width but with modest vertical height. There is a small top margin of about 16–24px from the underside of the header and an 8–12px gap below it before the page header begins. The breadcrumb text uses a smaller, lighter type than section headings, so it visually recedes but still provides context. It sits flush with the left edge of the main column content, aligning vertically with all major headings and with the left edge of tables and cards below, reinforcing the strong left-hand alignment of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content; no fixed or sticky behavior. It remains at the top of the document flow within the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Rate Limits page header block is positioned immediately below the breadcrumbs, left-aligned within the main content column. It consists of a bold page title line ('Rate Limits') and an optional short descriptive subtitle or paragraph under it. The title text is large enough to be the dominant element on first scroll, with roughly 24–32px of top margin from the breadcrumbs and 8–16px of bottom margin before the underline. A thin horizontal underline (a 1–2px rule) extends beneath the title (or beneath the block as a whole) to about 40–60% of the main content width, anchored to the left edge, mirroring the underline style observed in the mockup. Below the underline, an 16–24px vertical gap leads into the first content section. The entire header block spans roughly 80–100% of the main column width but remains text-centric, with no large graphical elements, maintaining the minimalist documentation aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll; it sits early in the vertical flow without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is the first major content block beneath the page header. It starts with a section heading (e.g., 'Introduction & Global Rate Limits') aligned to the left edge of the main column. The heading is separated from the underline above by about 24–32px of vertical space. Directly under the heading, one or two concise paragraphs explain how rate limiting works globally. Each paragraph is separated by roughly 8–12px. If global rules are listed (e.g., bullet points or a small table), they appear directly below the text, with 16–24px of spacing from the preceding paragraph, and each list item or row maintains generous line height so the content remains easy to scan. The section spans nearly the full width of the main column but keeps comfortable internal margins on the left and right, so line length stays within readable bounds. At the bottom of the section, there is a 24–32px vertical gap before the next section heading begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fully part of the normal vertical flow; no special positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The headers-and-retry section appears directly below the intro/global section, separated by a distinct vertical gap of about 24–32px to signal a new conceptual block. A left-aligned section heading (e.g., 'Rate Limit Headers & Retry Behavior') anchors the top of the section, aligned with all other headings in the main column. Below the heading, one or two medium-length paragraphs describe the HTTP headers, their meanings, and recommended retry behavior. Any inline code or header names are presented in-line with slight styling but do not disrupt the clean layout. If additional detail is needed (e.g., a small, two-column key-value reference for header names and descriptions), it appears as a compact block beneath the explanatory text, using 12–16px of internal padding and 16–24px of space above. The overall width mirrors the intro section, maintaining consistent left and right text boundaries. A 24–32px space separates this section from the best practices section below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky block within the vertical content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is positioned below the headers-and-retry block with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation. A left-aligned heading (e.g., 'Best Practices for Handling Rate Limits') introduces the section. Underneath, concise bullet points or short paragraphs outline practical recommendations, each item separated by 8–12px vertically. If bullets are used, they are indented slightly from the main text alignment but stay within the same overall column width so line lengths remain comfortable. The content is kept succinct to maintain the moderately spacious feel, avoiding dense, long-form text. The section spans nearly the full width of the main column and creates a conceptual bridge between theory and the more data-heavy rules area that follows. At its bottom, a clear vertical gap of 32px leads into the filter bar and rules table, visually signaling the transition from explanation to reference.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, part of the normal vertical flow, with no overlapping or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is the first element in the interactive rules area and sits directly below the best practices section, separated by about 32px of vertical space to distinguish it as a functional control surface. It forms a horizontal bar spanning approximately 100% of the main content column width, with internal horizontal padding of about 16–24px on the left and right, and vertical padding of 12–16px. On the left side of the bar, aligned with the main column’s left edge, are filter controls such as dropdowns or segmented toggles (e.g., 'Scope: Global/Per-endpoint', 'Authentication: Required/Optional'). These controls are laid out from left to right with about 16–24px horizontal spacing between them, ensuring a clean, uncluttered row. On the far right side of the bar, aligned to the same vertical centerline as the filters, sits the inline search component, visually docked so that the bar reads as a single, unified strip of controls. The filter bar has a subtle background tint or border to differentiate it from plain text sections while maintaining the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page; if desired, it can become sticky within the main column when the user scrolls down the table, but its default behavior is as a standard block element directly above the table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules table is positioned immediately below the filter bar, with a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans nearly the full width of the main content column, aligning its left edge with the column’s text baseline and leaving a small margin on the right for breathing room. The table header row is visually distinct but simple—slightly stronger weight or subtle background—organized in columns such as 'Endpoint', 'Method', 'Scope', 'Limit', and 'Window.' The header row has 12–16px vertical padding, while each data row below (composed of rate-limit-rule-row components) has similar or slightly larger height to ensure easy scanning, approximately 40–56px per row. Columns are sized to prevent crowding: endpoint information occupies a larger left portion, with numeric or categorical columns (limits, window) occupying narrower columns to the right. The table height adjusts with content; if there is more data than fits on the screen, the entire page scrolls rather than the table alone, preserving the simple document feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element; scrolls naturally with the page. No internal scrollbars; the table grows vertically with its content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each rule row is a horizontally stretched row within the rules table, running nearly the full width of the main content column between the table’s left and right boundaries. Vertically, each row is comfortably tall—about 40–56px—to keep text and click targets easy to interact with. The row is divided into aligned columns: the left-most cell contains the endpoint path and possibly a concise label, followed by method, scope, limit, and window columns progressing to the right. Content in each cell is left-aligned (or right-aligned for numeric values) to keep scanning predictable. Rows use subtle borders or alternating background tones to separate entries without heavy lines. If the rows are interactive (e.g., expandable or clickable), a right-aligned affordance (such as a chevron icon) can appear in the far-right column, creating a slight horizontal micro-flow from the left-most endpoint text toward the interaction icon. Horizontal padding within each cell is about 12–16px, matching the overall breathing space pattern in the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard table row; repeats vertically and expands the table’s height. If clickable, the entire row acts as a single hit area but remains inline within the table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline search field is positioned within the rate-limit-filters bar, anchored to the far right of the bar. It is horizontally aligned with the filter controls and vertically centered within the filter bar’s height. The search input spans roughly 30–40% of the filter bar’s width but shrinks responsively on narrow screens, ensuring it doesn’t overwhelm the filters. There is about 8–16px of horizontal space between the last filter control and the left edge of the search input. The search box has clear but minimal styling—a subtle border or understated background fill—with internal horizontal padding of about 12–16px so that the placeholder and text don’t feel cramped. A search icon may be placed inside the left of the field or at the far right, but in either case, the control remains visually simple and integrated into the filter bar. Vertically, there is 4–8px of breathing room above and below the field within the bar’s padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Logically bound to the filter bar; it moves with the filter bar and scrolls normally. If the filter bar becomes sticky, the search input does as well.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results panel appears in the same general vertical region as the rules table, directly beneath the filter bar and inline search. When active, it either replaces the visible rules table or overlays it in a way that still respects the overall vertical flow (e.g., the panel pushes the table down or is shown while the table is hidden). The panel spans nearly the full width of the main content column, aligned flush with the table’s left edge and respecting the same right margin. Inside, individual search results are listed vertically with clear separation—each result row having padding similar to or slightly less than a table row (around 24–40px height) and 12–16px of horizontal padding. Results might show endpoint, method, and limit details in a compact, card-like row, but the styling remains flat and minimalist to align with the documentation aesthetic. A subtle header within the panel (e.g., 'Search Results') can appear at the top, aligned left, with a small vertical gap (8–12px) before the list begins. When no results are found, a centered, concise message appears within the panel area. The vertical spacing above (to the filter bar) is around 16–24px, matching the spacing that the table uses.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally based on search state but remains anchored in the normal content flow below the filter bar. It scrolls with the rest of the content when results overflow the viewport.

name: Webhooks Overview
title: Webhooks Overview
description: Provides a structured overview of webhooks, including concepts, delivery, retries, signatures, configuration, event groups, and related documentation search.

core_features: Load and render structured explanatory content for webhooks (introduction, delivery mechanics, retries, signature verification, configuration, troubleshooting) using getWebhooksOverviewContent.
Fetch and display a dynamic list of webhook event groups with event counts using getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview, supporting highlight and context when a group is passed via the URL query parameter.
Enable navigation from an event group in the list to the corresponding webhook event group detail page by constructing a URL that includes the selected group slug as the group query parameter.
Provide inline documentation search scoped to webhook-related content using searchDocumentation, with filters applied so results are relevant to webhook overview and webhook event group pages.

Support deep-linking from other pages (e.g., docs home, FAQ) into a specific webhook event group by reading the group query parameter and visually emphasizing the corresponding event group in the list.

Guide users from conceptual sections (configuration, delivery, troubleshooting) to the appropriate detailed API reference or group-level documentation via contextual links that update the URL with relevant query parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Understand webhook behavior and configuration
1. User navigates to webhooks.html (no group query parameter).
2. On page initialization, the data loader component calls getWebhooksOverviewContent() to retrieve markdown sections and calls getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview(webhooksOverviewPageId) to retrieve event groups and counts.
3. The overview sections component renders the intro, delivery mechanics, retries, signature verification, configuration, and troubleshooting sections based on the returned markdown fields.
4. The user scrolls through sections; internal anchor links within the markdown (for example, to configuration or troubleshooting) update the URL hash and scroll to the relevant subsection without additional data fetching.
5. When the user clicks any inline link that refers to managing webhook endpoints via API, the browser navigates to api_reference.html with query parameters (such as resource=webhook_endpoints) appended to the URL.
6. If the user needs more detail on specific events, they scroll to the event group list and select a group to navigate to its detail page.

Workflow 2: Deep-link into a specific webhook event group from another page
1. The user arrives on webhooks.html with a group query parameter in the URL (e.g., webhooks.html?group=<slug>), set by another page such as index.html or faq.html.
2. On page initialization, the URL state manager reads the group parameter and stores it as the selectedGroupSlug state.
3. The data loader fetches event groups via getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview(webhooksOverviewPageId).
4. The event group list component receives the groups and selectedGroupSlug, finds the matching WebhookEventGroup (if any) by comparing group.slug to selectedGroupSlug, and visually emphasizes that group (highlight, pinned position, or similar behavior).
5. A contextual banner or highlight area shows a short summary based on the selected group data and provides a primary action, such as a button to open the full event group details page.
6. When the user clicks the primary action or the emphasized list item, the app constructs the target URL for that group's detail page (using routing that resolves group.pageId to a filename) and appends group=<group.slug> as a query parameter, then performs navigation.
7. If the user clicks "clear selection" in the banner, the component removes the group query parameter from the URL (via navigation or history APIs) and updates selectedGroupSlug to null, causing the event group list to render without any highlighted selection.

Workflow 3: Discover the right event group from the list
1. User lands on webhooks.html without a specific group selected.
2. The data loader calls getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview(webhooksOverviewPageId) and passes the resulting list to the event group list component.
3. The list component displays each WebhookEventGroup with its name, description, and eventCount.
4. The user scans or filters (client-side only, e.g., text input bound to the list, if implemented) until they identify a relevant group.
5. The user clicks a group list item; the click handler constructs the URL to the group's detail documentation page based on group.pageId and includes group=<group.slug> as a query parameter.
6. The browser navigates to the group-specific page (for example, a page like webhooks_invoices.html managed by the router), where more detailed event documentation is displayed.

Workflow 4: Search documentation from Webhooks Overview
1. The user focuses the inline search field on the Webhooks Overview page.
2. As the user types, the component updates internal state for the current query but does not yet call searchDocumentation until the user submits (by pressing Enter or clicking a search button).
3. On submit, the search component calls searchDocumentation with the query string and a filters object configured to prioritize webhook-related content (for example, including filters for pageType and/or entityType corresponding to webhooks_overview and webhooks_group).
4. When searchDocumentation resolves, the component displays a list of results (title and snippet) and the total result count.
5. When the user clicks a search result that points to the Webhooks Overview page, the component navigates to webhooks.html, optionally including a group query parameter if the result is tied to a specific WebhookEventGroup or WebhookEvent.
6. When the user clicks a search result that points to a webhook event group page, the component navigates directly to that page, preserving any relevant query parameters (including group).

Workflow 5: Navigate between docs home, Webhooks Overview, and API Reference
1. The user starts on the documentation home page (index.html) and uses global navigation or content links to open webhooks.html.
2. The breadcrumbs component shows a trail including a link back to index.html and the current Webhooks Overview item.
3. If the user clicks the breadcrumb pointing to the docs home, the app navigates to index.html.
4. From the Webhooks Overview content, the user clicks a contextual link referencing endpoint management; the browser navigates to api_reference.html with a resource parameter that selects the webhook endpoint resource within the API reference.
5. The user can then use the browser back button to return to webhooks.html, preserving any group query parameter that was in the URL.
interactions: On page load (webhooks.html with or without group query parameter), the data loader component triggers getWebhooksOverviewContent() and getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview(webhooksOverviewPageId); once resolved, it passes the content and groups to child components for rendering.

When the URL query parameter group changes (either via navigation from another page or in-page navigation updating the URL), the URL state manager recomputes selectedGroupSlug and notifies the event group list and highlight components to recompute the selected group and update their visual state.
Clicking the docs home breadcrumb item navigates the user to index.html without altering any other state (no interface calls are needed for this interaction).

Clicking any inline link in the configuration, delivery, retries, signature verification, or troubleshooting sections that refers to API endpoint management navigates the user to api_reference.html with appropriate query parameters (such as a resource parameter) appended to the URL.

Clicking an event group list item triggers onGroupItemClick, which constructs a URL for the selected group's detail page based on routing metadata (resolving group.pageId to a filename) and appends group=<group.slug> as a query parameter, then navigates to that URL.

When a group is selected via the group query parameter, the highlight banner component is shown; clicking a primary action within this banner (such as "View details") navigates to the same group detail page URL as the list item click would, including group=<group.slug> in the query string.

Clicking a "clear selection" action in the highlight banner removes the group query parameter from the current URL (e.g., via history.replaceState) and sets selectedGroupSlug to null, which instructs the event group list component to remove any item highlight and for the highlight banner to hide.

Typing into the inline search field updates internal state for currentQuery; when the user submits the search (onSubmit), searchDocumentation is called with { query: currentQuery, filters: configuredWebhookFilters } and the results are displayed in a search results panel on the same page.

Clicking on a search result triggers onResultClick, which reads the target pageId and pageType from the result object and navigates to the appropriate page (e.g., webhooks.html, a webhook event group page, or api_reference.html), appending any relevant query parameters such as group where appropriate.

Using in-section anchor links (for example, links within the markdown like #delivery-mechanics) updates the URL hash segment and scrolls to the corresponding subsection; this does not trigger any new interface calls, only a client-side scroll behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary persistent state for this page is the group URL query parameter. When the page loads, routing logic parses the current URL to extract the group value (if present), which is stored as selectedGroupSlug. This state is not stored in localStorage; it is derived solely from the URL. The data loader calls getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview with the known webhooksOverviewPageId and passes the resulting list of WebhookEventGroup objects to the event group list component along with selectedGroupSlug. The event group list reduces over the groups to find the one whose slug equals selectedGroupSlug; if found, that group is treated as the selected group for highlighting and for the context banner. If no matching group is found or if group is absent in the URL, no selection is assumed. When the user interacts with the UI to clear the selection, the group query parameter is removed from the URL (e.g., by navigating to webhooks.html without query parameters or by using history.replaceState to update the URL). This implicitly resets selectedGroupSlug to null, causing components dependent on that state (highlight banner, list item highlight) to re-render accordingly. When a user clicks on a specific event group list item or a primary action related to an event group, the navigation logic constructs a new URL for the corresponding event group detail page (based on routing using group.pageId) and appends group=<group.slug> as a query parameter. For search, currentQuery and filters are maintained as transient in-memory component state, not in the URL and not in localStorage; search results are computed on demand via searchDocumentation(query, filters) and discarded when the user leaves or reloads the page. The overview markdown sections themselves are not encoded into state; they come from getWebhooksOverviewContent on each load and are rendered directly.

components.functionality: Initializes the Webhooks Overview page by fetching explanatory markdown content and the list of webhook event groups, then distributes the data to child components.
data_binding: getWebhooksOverviewContent() -> { introMarkdown, deliveryMechanicsMarkdown, retriesMarkdown, signatureVerificationMarkdown, configurationMarkdown, troubleshootingMarkdown }
webhooksOverviewPageId (provided by the routing layer for this Page)

components.functionality: Manages URL-derived state for the Webhooks Overview page, primarily the group query parameter used to determine which webhook event group (if any) is selected or highlighted.
data_binding: WebhookEventGroup[] (from getWebhookEventGroupsForOverview) -> selectedGroup (resolved where group.slug === selectedGroupSlug)

components.functionality: Displays a breadcrumb trail for navigation between the documentation home and the Webhooks Overview page, and handles navigation when breadcrumbs are clicked.

Renders the core explanatory sections for webhooks (introduction, delivery mechanics, retries, signature verification, configuration, troubleshooting) using markdown content from getWebhooksOverviewContent and exposes internal anchor navigation and contextual links to related documentation.

Displays a list of webhook event groups with their descriptions and event counts, highlights the group selected by the group URL parameter, and allows navigation to the corresponding webhook event group detail page when an item is clicked.

When a specific webhook event group is indicated by the group URL parameter, shows a contextual banner that summarizes the selected group and offers quick actions such as navigating to its detailed documentation or clearing the selection.
data_binding: selectedGroup (resolved from WebhookEventGroup[] where group.slug === selectedGroupSlug)

components.functionality: Provides an inline search field for searching documentation relevant to webhooks, calls searchDocumentation with the user-entered query and webhook-specific filters, and renders a result list with links to matching pages.
data_binding: currentFilters (local state configured for webhook-related content, e.g., pageType and/or entityType values)

components.functionality: Synchronizes in-page anchor navigation (e.g., #delivery-mechanics, #retries) with the URL hash and scroll position so users can jump directly to specific sections within the Webhooks Overview content.
data_binding: Section anchors generated from markdown headings in getWebhooksOverviewContent()

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom reading order within a two-column layout, mirroring the mockup. At the very top is the fixed global header (outside this spec). Below it, the main content container is centered with generous left/right margins. Inside this container, the left column houses the anchor navigation, while the right column holds the breadcrumbs at the top, followed by the 'Webhooks Overview' page title (with underline), then the introduction copy, and subsequently the main sections in a linear vertical stack. Inline utilities like search, contextual banners, and lists are embedded within this main column at the relevant points in the narrative (e.g., search near the overview/searchable content, banner and event group list within the event group section), maintaining the strong linear flow observed in the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom reading order within a two-column layout, mirroring the mockup. At the very top is the fixed global header (outside this spec). Below it, the main content container is centered with generous left/right margins. Inside this container, the left column houses the anchor navigation, while the right column holds the breadcrumbs at the top, followed by the 'Webhooks Overview' page title (with underline), then the introduction copy, and subsequently the main sections in a linear vertical stack. Inline utilities like search, contextual banners, and lists are embedded within this main column at the relevant points in the narrative (e.g., search near the overview/searchable content, banner and event group list within the event group section), maintaining the strong linear flow observed in the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly the top 10–12% of the viewport height. Below, a central content container spans about 80–90% of the viewport width, with outer gutters on both sides. Within this container, the left column (anchor navigation) takes about 18–22% of the width, and the right main content column takes the remaining 78–82%. Vertical spacing between major sections (e.g., between 'Webhooks Overview', 'Delivery', 'Retries', 'Signatures', 'Event Groups') is around 32–40px. Minor elements (e.g., between headings and paragraphs, between a section heading and an embedded component like the search box or banner) use 8–16px spacing. Components such as the banner and event group list stretch nearly the full width of the main column, with internal padding around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a moderately spacious density similar to the mockup: short text blocks, comfortable line length, and few interactive elements per screen height. Cards and list rows (e.g., event group list items) are single-line or two-line maximum with ample horizontal padding and clear separation between items. There is no attempt to compress content; instead the hierarchy is created through spacing and typography. Inline elements like the search bar are given breathing room above and below so they read as distinct but integrated parts of the page, not cramped utilities.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow is dominantly vertical. The eye moves from the dark fixed header down to the breadcrumbs, then to the bold 'Webhooks Overview' title with an underline, then sequentially through each section heading and its supporting content. The left anchor navigation forms a lighter, secondary vertical rail that assists orientation but does not compete with the main column. Within specific sections, horizontal micro-flows are created: for instance, event group list rows use a left accent (colored bar or label) leading the eye rightwards toward the row label and any chevron indicating drill-down. The search bar and contextual banner act as visual punctuation points within the vertical flow, but they stay aligned with the main text column so they feel part of the same reading path.

layout.overall_description: Beneath a fixed, full-width top header, the page content sits inside a centered, fixed-width container with generous side gutters, giving the layout a calm, documentation-focused feel. The content area is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main column. The left sidebar starts just below the header and runs vertically, showing an unobtrusive anchor navigation that gently tracks the structure of the page. To the right, the main column begins with compact breadcrumbs, then a prominent 'Webhooks Overview' title underlined in a simple rule, establishing the page focus. An introductory paragraph sits under the title with modest spacing, followed by a stack of clearly separated sections on webhooks concepts, delivery, retries, signatures, configuration, and event groups. Within these sections, the inline search field, an event group highlight banner, and a vertical list of event groups appear as full-width blocks aligned to the main text column, each surrounded by comfortable white space. List items are lean horizontal rows with a colored accent on the left and a chevron on the right, echoing the endpoint card style from the mockup. A loader overlays the main column when data is fetching, while URL state behavior and state management are invisible. Overall, the page feels structured, airy, and highly legible, with a strong vertical reading rhythm and a quiet, neutral visual language.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The content loader is visually confined to the right main content column. When active, it appears as a semi-translucent overlay covering the full height and width of the main content column (from just below the breadcrumbs down to the current bottom of the content), without obscuring the left anchor navigation. It dims the underlying content slightly and centers a minimal loading indicator (e.g., a spinner or progress glyph) both horizontally and vertically within that column. The overlay respects the main content’s left and right padding, so the loader aligns with the text column edges rather than the full viewport. When inactive, it occupies no visible space and does not affect spacing between other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay within the main content column container, pinned to its top and left edges, and extending to its full width and current height. It scrolls with the main content container (not fixed to the viewport), appearing only during loading states and otherwise not influencing layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and does not render any visible component in the layout. It operates behind the scenes, coordinating the URL with the page’s current section and any selected event groups or search query, without reserving any space on screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical, no spatial footprint. It is conceptually attached to the page-level container but neither occupies nor alters any visual layout regions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs navigation sits at the top of the right main content column, directly beneath the fixed global header and horizontally aligned with the main text column. It starts flush with the left edge of the main column’s content (respecting the same left padding as body text) and extends across roughly 60–70% of the main column width, leaving some whitespace to the far right. Vertically, there is about 16–20px of top padding between the bottom edge of the global header and the breadcrumbs. The breadcrumbs themselves occupy a single line under normal conditions, with small, subtly styled text that feels subordinate to the upcoming page title. A 16–24px gap separates the bottom of the breadcrumbs from the 'Webhooks Overview' title, maintaining a clear visual separation while keeping them grouped as page-level orientation elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the main content column; it scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed or sticky. It participates in normal document flow and helps anchor the starting point of the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview sections component occupies the entire right main content column, forming the primary vertical narrative. At its top, directly under the breadcrumbs, is the 'Webhooks Overview' page title, left-aligned and spanning most of the main column width. A simple horizontal underline runs beneath this title, matching the text width or slightly shorter, reinforcing its importance without visual heaviness. There is about 8–12px of space between the title and the underline, and another 16–24px below the underline before the introductory paragraph. The introduction is a short paragraph with a comfortable line length, aligned to the same left edge as the title. Below the introduction, major sections (e.g., 'Concepts', 'Delivery and Retries', 'Signatures', 'Configuration', 'Event Groups', 'Search the Webhooks Docs') are stacked vertically. Each section heading (H2) is left-aligned and full-width within the main column, with roughly 32–40px of top margin from the previous section’s last element, and 8–16px of space before its first paragraph or embedded component. Inline pieces such as the search form and the event group highlight banner sit as full-width blocks under the relevant headings, maintaining the same left and right text alignment. The entire component respects the main content column width, leaving about 18–22% of the full page width free for the left sidebar, and keeps consistent horizontal padding so that all text, forms, and banners share the same vertical column alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, flowing content container within the main column. It scrolls normally, with no fixed or sticky behavior, and provides the anchor targets for the left-side anchor navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event group list appears within the 'Event Groups' section of the main content column, typically just below the event group highlight banner. It stretches almost the full width of the main column, aligned with the text column’s left edge and ending before the right padding. The list is a vertical stack of horizontally-oriented rows, each row resembling a slim card. Every item has a subtle background (either white on a lightly tinted section background or a faintly tinted card) and internal padding of about 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. On each row, a narrow vertical accent bar or colored stripe sits flush against the far left inside the card, occupying roughly 4–6% of the row width. To the right of this accent bar, the event group name is left-aligned and vertically centered. On the far right of each row, a right-facing chevron icon is aligned vertically with the text and inset by the same horizontal padding as the left side. Rows are separated by 8–12px vertical gaps, giving the list breathing room while maintaining the impression of a cohesive group. The list’s top edge is spaced about 16–24px below the banner or section copy above it, and the bottom edge has roughly 24–32px margin before the next major section starts.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list embedded within the normal flow of the 'Event Groups' section. It scrolls with the page and does not pin or resize; items may respond to hover/focus within their allotted fixed-height rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event group highlight banner sits within the 'Event Groups' section of the main content column, above the event group list and below the section heading and any explanatory paragraph. It appears as a full-width, horizontally oriented block aligned to the main text column, with a light, neutral background to differentiate it from the plain content area. The banner has generous padding—approximately 16–20px vertically and 20–24px horizontally—so that its heading and short body text feel comfortably spaced. On the left edge of the banner content, a subtle vertical accent (either a thin border or a colored bar) visually ties it to the list style and the overall documentation design language without becoming dominant. Any optional link or call to action inside the banner is aligned to the left, beneath the main text, rather than floating right, to preserve the simple, stacked reading order. There is about 16–24px of vertical space above the banner (from the section heading or paragraph) and 16–24px below before the event group list begins, giving the banner clear separation as a contextual highlight.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the main column’s normal flow. It occupies a fixed-height area determined by its content and scrolls with the page, with no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline search form is placed within the main content column at the point where users are encouraged to search related webhook documentation—typically near the end of the introductory sections or under a dedicated 'Search the Webhooks Docs' heading. The search box is left-aligned and spans about 60–70% of the main content column width, stopping well short of the far right edge to maintain an airy feel. It appears as a single, wide input field with a clear border or subtle inset, matching the minimalist style. A search icon may be placed inside the left side of the input, while a placeholder text explains that it searches webhook-related docs. There is a 16–24px gap between the heading or paragraph above and the search field, and another 16–24px below before the next section heading or text begins. The field height is comfortably large (around 40–48px) to provide a generous click/tap target, and it visually aligns with the rest of the content: its left edge lines up with headings and paragraphs, reinforcing the consistent column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline form control within the main column. It scrolls with the content and does not remain sticky; its width adapts within the main column but remains anchored to the left alignment of the text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The anchor navigation occupies the narrow left sidebar column that runs alongside the main content. It begins just below the global header, aligning its top edge horizontally with the top of the breadcrumbs in the main column, and extends downward as far as needed. The sidebar width is roughly 18–22% of the central content container, leaving ample space for the wider main column on the right. Inside the sidebar, there is a small top padding (about 16–24px) before a subtle label such as 'On this page' or a section title like 'Webhooks', rendered in a light, small-caps or semi-bold style. Beneath this label, a vertical list of anchor links corresponds to the major sections in the main column (Overview, Delivery, Retries, Signatures, Configuration, Event Groups, Search). Each link is a single line of text aligned to the left edge of the sidebar content, with 8–12px vertical spacing between items. Text is smaller and lighter than main headings, ensuring the sidebar remains visually subordinate. The anchor navigation’s background matches the page background, with no heavy bounding box, so it reads as a light reference rail parallel to the primary content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar within the content region: it remains visible as the user scrolls, pinned near the top of the viewport with an offset equal to the height of the fixed header plus a small margin (e.g., 16px). It does not overlap the main content column and respects the grid, maintaining its width and position relative to the centered container.

name: Webhooks  Invoice Events
title: Invoice Webhook Events
description: Documentation page that lists all invoice-related webhook events, lets users open detailed sections for each event, view example deliveries and raw JSON payloads, and copy those payloads. The page supports deep-linking to specific invoice events via URL parameters.

core_features: Load an invoice webhook event group and its events based on the URL parameter for the group, using getWebhookEventGroupDetails.
Display a navigable list of invoice webhook events with selectable items that update the URL and focus the corresponding detail section.
Show detailed documentation for a selected webhook event, including delivery behavior and one or more example payloads, using getWebhookEventDetails.
Render each example payload as raw JSON with a copy control that triggers performCopyCodeSnippet, while the UI layer handles actual clipboard copying.
Support deep-linking to a specific invoice event via the event URL parameter so that its section is automatically expanded and scrolled into view.
Provide a documentation search interaction that calls searchDocumentation and allows navigation to returned pages, including webhooks and invoice-related API reference content.
Offer contextual navigation links back to the Webhooks overview (with the invoice event group preselected) and to the related invoice REST API reference.
user_workflows: Landing on the invoice webhook events page: 1) User opens the page via a link that may include a group parameter. 2) The page-init logic reads the group parameter and resolves it to a groupId. 3) The page calls getWebhookEventGroupDetails with that groupId. 4) The sidebar event list and group header are populated with the returned WebhookEventGroup and related WebhookEvent records.

Deep-link to a specific invoice event via URL: 1) User follows a link that includes both group and event parameters. 2) The page loads the event group with getWebhookEventGroupDetails. 3) After events are loaded, the page resolves the event parameter to a specific eventId. 4) The page calls getWebhookEventDetails for that eventId. 5) The corresponding event detail section is expanded and scrolled into view while the URL remains in sync.

Browsing and switching between invoice events: 1) User clicks an event in the event list sidebar. 2) The click updates the event parameter in the URL (preserving the current group parameter). 3) The selection controller resolves the clicked event to an eventId and calls getWebhookEventDetails for that eventId. 4) The event detail panel updates to show the selected event’s delivery details and payload examples.

Inspecting and copying a webhook payload example: 1) Within an event’s details, the user selects a payload example. 2) The JSON is shown in a code block with a copy button. 3) When the user clicks the copy button, the component calls performCopyCodeSnippet with sourceType set to indicate a webhook payload, sourceId set to the corresponding WebhookPayloadExample.id, language set according to the example format, and copiedContent set to the JSON text. 4) The UI layer copies the content to the clipboard and may show a non-blocking confirmation based on the returned success state.

Searching documentation from the page: 1) User types a query into the header search input. 2) On submit, the search component calls searchDocumentation with the query and any chosen filters. 3) Results are rendered, each pointing to a documentation Page via its pageId and pageType. 4) When the user clicks a result that targets this invoice webhook group or a specific event, navigation occurs using URL parameters (group and/or event) so that the correct section is loaded and focused.

Navigating to related documentation: 1) From the breadcrumb, the user clicks the Webhooks overview link, which navigates to webhooks.html with group=invoice_events in the URL. 2) From within an event’s description, the user clicks a 'Related REST API' link, which navigates to api_reference.html with resource set to the invoice-related resource identifier so they can inspect the underlying REST endpoints.
interactions: On page initialization, the controller reads the group and event URL parameters, resolves them to internal groupId and eventId values, and triggers getWebhookEventGroupDetails and, when applicable, getWebhookEventDetails.

When the user clicks an event item in the sidebar list, the currently selected event is updated, the URL event parameter is set to that event’s key, and getWebhookEventDetails is called for the selected event.

When the user expands or collapses an event detail section, the component ensures the expanded section corresponds to the currently selected event in the URL state; collapsing an expanded section may clear the event parameter from the URL.

When the user selects a different payload example for the same event (if multiple are available), the corresponding WebhookPayloadExample is loaded into the visible code block without additional API calls, using previously loaded payloadExamples.

When the user clicks a copy control on a payload example, the component calls performCopyCodeSnippet and then, based on the returned success flag, may trigger a visual confirmation; the URL state is not changed by copy actions.

When the user submits a search query via the search bar, the component calls searchDocumentation and updates its result list; clicking a result causes navigation via standard links that may include group and event URL parameters.
When the user clicks the breadcrumb link back to Webhooks overview, the browser navigates to webhooks.html with group=invoice_events in the query string.
When the user clicks a 'Related REST API' link in an event description, the browser navigates to api_reference.html with resource set for the invoice resource in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL parameters and API responses. The group parameter in the URL determines which webhook event group is loaded; the routing layer resolves this slug to a groupId used in getWebhookEventGroupDetails. If group is missing or does not resolve, the page may show an error state or rely on a default group determined by server-side routing, but the client logic does not hardcode any group values. The event parameter represents a specific webhook event key within the loaded group. After getWebhookEventGroupDetails returns, the page finds the WebhookEvent whose name or identifying property matches the event parameter and uses its id in getWebhookEventDetails. Changing the event parameter (via user interactions or navigation) updates the selected event and the expanded detail section. The URL is kept in sync with user actions by adding or replacing query parameters using standard navigation or history APIs, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage.
