The selection of payload examples for a given event is maintained in component-local state only for rendering purposes and is not written to the URL; it is always derived from the payloadExamples returned by getWebhookEventDetails.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading based on URL parameters. Reads the group and event parameters, maps them to internal identifiers, calls getWebhookEventGroupDetails to load the invoice event group and its events, and, when an event parameter is present and resolvable, calls getWebhookEventDetails for that event.

Displays the loaded webhook event group’s name and description and provides contextual navigation controls, such as a breadcrumb link back to the Webhooks overview and a link to related invoice REST API documentation.

Renders a navigable list of invoice webhook events from the currently loaded group. Highlights the currently selected event based on the event URL parameter and allows users to click an event to change the selection, update the URL, and trigger loading of its details.

Displays an accordion-style list of all events in the group, where each event can be expanded to show its details. The event corresponding to the current event URL parameter is expanded and scrolled into view. Expanding another event updates the URL parameter, and collapsing the selected event can clear the event parameter.

Shows detailed information for the currently selected webhook event by consuming the result of getWebhookEventDetails. Renders the event’s name, description, and deliveryDetailsMarkdown, and passes any payloadExamples to the payload example viewer.

Lists one or more payload examples for the selected event and renders the currently active example’s payloadJson in a code block with an associated copy control. If multiple examples exist, it allows switching between them without additional API calls.

Handles the logical side of payload copy actions. When invoked by a UI component, calls performCopyCodeSnippet with the appropriate sourceType, sourceId, language, and copiedContent derived from the selected WebhookPayloadExample. Returns success state to the caller for UI feedback.

Provides a search input (typically in the global header) for searching documentation content. On submit, calls searchDocumentation with the user-entered query and any filters. Renders a list of results that link to their corresponding pages and, when applicable, pass group and event parameters to this page to deep-link to the relevant event.

Keeps the URL query parameters synchronized with user interactions on the page. When the selected group or event changes (for example, by clicking an event in the sidebar or expanding a different accordion section), updates the group and event parameters in the URL without reloading the page. Also listens for browser navigation events (back/forward) and triggers the appropriate updates to selected event and content.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow in the main content column: fixed global header at the very top; below that, a centered content container split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content area. Inside the main column, the order is: webhook-group-header (page title + summary), then the event-detail-accordion list. Each accordion item vertically reveals its event-detail-panel, which internally contains the payload-example-viewer. Non-visual controllers (page-init-controller, copy-payload-controller, url-state-synchronizer) sit logically around these components but do not affect the spatial flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom flow in the main content column: fixed global header at the very top; below that, a centered content container split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content area. Inside the main column, the order is: webhook-group-header (page title + summary), then the event-detail-accordion list. Each accordion item vertically reveals its event-detail-panel, which internally contains the payload-example-viewer. Non-visual controllers (page-init-controller, copy-payload-controller, url-state-synchronizer) sit logically around these components but do not affect the spatial flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed top header (which contains the documentation-search-form) occupies roughly 10–12% of the viewport height. Below it, a centered content container uses about 80–90% of the viewport width, with generous left/right gutters. Within this container, the event-list-sidebar occupies about 18–22% of the width on the left, and the main content column (webhook-group-header + event-detail-accordion + panels) takes the remaining 78–82% on the right. Event cards and panels stretch nearly the full width of the main column, with padding and spacing consistent with the 8px-based scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately spacious: short, scannable text blocks and a limited number of interactive elements per screen. Vertical gaps between major sections (like the page header and the first list of events) are about 32px; gaps between accordion items and internal sections in a detail panel are around 16–24px. Cards and code areas use comfortable internal padding (~16–24px) to avoid visual crowding and to keep line lengths readable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the dark fixed header and the documentation-search-form on the top right, then moves down to the bold page title in the main column. From there, the gaze follows the strong vertical stack of section header and accordion rows. Each event row creates a horizontal micro-flow from left to right: starting with the colored status strip and event name, then moving toward the chevron indicating expand/collapse. When expanded, attention drops into the event-detail-panel, ending at the lower payload-example-viewer, where a right-aligned copy action (driven by the copy-payload-controller) provides a clear interaction endpoint. The left event-list-sidebar acts as a secondary vertical index that anchors orientation but remains visually subordinate to the main column.

layout.overall_description: The page is structured like a modern developer documentation screen: a full-width, fixed-position top bar spans the top of the viewport with a logo and primary navigation on the left and a compact documentation search form on the right. Below this, the main content sits inside a centered container with generous side gutters. The container is split into two columns: a narrow, light-toned event-list sidebar on the left that lists invoice webhook events, and a wider content column on the right. At the top of the right column, a clear, bold 'Webhooks – Invoice Events' title is underlined, followed by a brief explanatory paragraph. Below that, the primary body of the page is a vertical sequence of horizontally stretched event rows, each styled like a flat card with a subtle colored strip on the left, event name text, and a right-facing chevron. Clicking a row expands it in place to reveal a detailed panel with human-readable explanations and, further down, a code-like payload viewer. The payload viewer resembles a compact code editor panel with tabs or toggles for different payload representations and a right-aligned 'Copy' action. Throughout, spacing is generous, text remains within comfortable line lengths, and the left sidebar quietly tracks event anchors as the main content scrolls, maintaining a structured, neutral, and highly legible documentation experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller has no visible UI. It exists at the page level, logically wrapping the entire documentation content. It initializes the state of the page—such as which invoice event is selected—based on URL parameters or defaults. Spatially, it occupies no screen space and introduces no margins or padding; it simply orchestrates which of the visible components (like the event-detail-accordion and event-detail-panel) are shown or expanded when the page first loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical layer; no rendered footprint, does not affect layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The webhook-group-header sits at the very top of the right main content column, directly below the fixed top navigation bar and aligned with the central content container. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the main content area (about 78–82% of the container width), starting roughly where the left sidebar ends and continuing to the right gutter. Vertically, it begins about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the header bar to create breathing room. The component contains the page title 'Webhooks – Invoice Events' as a prominent H1 aligned flush left with the main column, with a subtle full-width underline or bottom border extending across the column to visually anchor it. A short descriptive paragraph or summary sits directly below the title with about 8–16px of vertical spacing. Below this summary, there is an additional 24–32px vertical gap before the first event row in the event-detail-accordion, maintaining the mockup’s pattern of clear separation between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scrollable content; scrolls with the page, staying at the top of the main column until scrolled away.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-list-sidebar forms the left-hand navigation column inside the main content container. It begins directly below the fixed header bar, aligned to the left edge of the centered content container, and extends downward along the full height of the content. Horizontally, it occupies about 18–22% of the container width, leaving the remaining 78–82% to the right main content column. The sidebar includes a modest 'Invoice Events' or 'Reference' label at the top, set about 24px below the header bar, followed by a vertical list of event links. Each link is a single line with minimal iconography, stacked with around 8–12px vertical spacing between items. The text is set in a slightly smaller, lighter-weight style than the main content to underscore its secondary nature. There is generous left padding (16–24px) inside the sidebar so the text does not touch the edge. The sidebar’s right edge aligns cleanly with the left edge of the main content column, reinforcing a crisp two-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a vertically scrolling column within the page, but may employ a sticky position from just below the header bar so that its top remains pinned while the main content scrolls, keeping navigation anchors in view on taller pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event-detail-accordion occupies the upper portion of the right main content column, directly below the webhook-group-header. It sits flush left with the main column and stretches almost edge-to-edge within that column, leaving a consistent right gutter (about 24–32px) to match the left-side alignment. The accordion is a vertical stack of event summary rows, each styled like a flat card with no heavy borders. Each row has a colored vertical strip or accent line on the far left (within the card’s width, not extending into the gutter) to subtly indicate status or event type. To the right of the strip, the event name and a concise description (if present) are left-aligned, with a right-facing chevron icon aligned on the far right to indicate that the row can expand. Internal padding per row is roughly 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally, maintaining the mockup’s generous card spacing. Rows are separated by about 8–16px of vertical space to keep the list airy. When a row is collapsed, only the card-like header is visible. When expanded, the row visually remains in place and its associated event-detail-panel appears directly beneath it, maintaining the linear downward flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content; each item expands/collapses vertically, pushing content below it downward without overlapping other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: An event-detail-panel appears immediately beneath the corresponding event row within the event-detail-accordion when that row is expanded. It is visually grouped with the row above—either sharing the same background color as the card or using a slightly lighter tint to indicate nested detail. Horizontally, the panel spans the same width as its parent row, aligning exactly with the main column’s left and right edges. Inside, the panel content follows a vertical stack: at the top, a short description of what the event does; below that, structured metadata or bullet points; further down, a section heading like 'Example deliveries' or 'Payload'; and finally, the payload-example-viewer. Each internal block is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, with the outer panel using 16–24px of padding on all sides. When multiple panels are open, they appear in sequence, each panel pushing the next event row further down, preserving the clean top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears as part of the accordion’s expand/collapse behavior; occupies full available width inside the main column and only affects vertical spacing by expanding the document flow where it appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: An event-detail-panel appears immediately beneath the corresponding event row within the event-detail-accordion when that row is expanded. It is visually grouped with the row above—either sharing the same background color as the card or using a slightly lighter tint to indicate nested detail. Horizontally, the panel spans the same width as its parent row, aligning exactly with the main column’s left and right edges. Inside, the panel content follows a vertical stack: at the top, a short description of what the event does; below that, structured metadata or bullet points; further down, a section heading like 'Example deliveries' or 'Payload'; and finally, the payload-example-viewer. Each internal block is separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, with the outer panel using 16–24px of padding on all sides. When multiple panels are open, they appear in sequence, each panel pushing the next event row further down, preserving the clean top-to-bottom reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears as part of the accordion’s expand/collapse behavior; occupies full available width inside the main column and only affects vertical spacing by expanding the document flow where it appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The payload-example-viewer is embedded near the bottom of each event-detail-panel, occupying the full internal width of that panel minus its padding. It is presented as a rectangular code-oriented block with a subtle border or background contrast to distinguish it from surrounding text. At the top of the viewer, a slim header row spans the width: on the left, a set of small tabs or toggle buttons (e.g., 'Example delivery', 'JSON payload', 'Raw'); on the right, a compact 'Copy' action that is visually aligned with the right edge of the panel. Below this header, the main body of the viewer shows a scrollable monospace code area with a fixed height of roughly 240–320px so as not to overwhelm the page. The code lines maintain generous left padding (16–24px) and a light line height to preserve readability. There is about 16px of vertical spacing between the viewer and any descriptive text above it, and about 24px below it before the next section or event row begins, maintaining an airy but structured feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its parent event-detail-panel; the code content is internally scrollable if it exceeds the fixed height, while the viewer itself stays fixed in size relative to the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The copy-payload-controller has no direct visual presence. It attaches behaviorally to the 'Copy' button or icon located in the top-right portion of each payload-example-viewer. It does not add any visible controls or occupy space; instead, it simply enables interactions on existing UI elements, such as copying JSON text to the clipboard and possibly showing transient feedback overlays controlled elsewhere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual interaction layer; no layout footprint and does not alter surrounding spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The documentation-search-form is positioned in the fixed top navigation bar, aligned to the right side of the bar. The header itself runs full-width across the viewport and is about 64–72px tall (roughly 10–12% of viewport height). The search form sits horizontally to the right of the main navigation items, with approximately 16–24px of margin to the bar’s right edge. Vertically, the search input is centered within the header’s height, ensuring a balanced appearance. The input width is constrained to around 260–320px, keeping it prominent but not dominant. There is sufficient horizontal padding inside the input (around 12–16px) and clear separation from adjacent header items, following the mockup’s generous click target spacing. This placement makes global search quickly accessible without interfering with the main two-column layout below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place as part of the fixed top header; remains visible at the top-right of the viewport while the main page content scrolls beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer is invisible and sits logically alongside the page-init-controller at the page level. It keeps the current state of the page—such as which invoice event is selected or which tab in the payload-example-viewer is active—in sync with the browser’s URL (hash or query parameters). This synchronization enables deep-linking without adding any visual elements or altering the spatial layout of the page. All visible components retain their positions; only their state changes when the URL changes or when the user interacts with them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavior layer; no rendered size or position, no impact on the document flow.

name: SDKs Overview
title: SDKs Overview
description: Page that loads available SDKs, highlights a language-specific SDK when requested via URL, and provides entry points into SDK docs and related API documentation.

core_features: Fetch and display SDKs overview content and the list of available SDKs by calling getSdksOverview on page load.
Render the overview markdown content (introMarkdown) explaining SDK usage and linking to related documentation such as the API reference.
Display the SDKs as an interactive list or grid of SDK entries sourced from the returned sdks array, without hardcoding any SDKs or languages.
Pre-highlight a specific SDK entry when a language URL parameter is present by matching it against each Sdk.language value.
Allow users to navigate to a selected SDK’s detailed documentation page by clicking an SDK entry, emitting navigation intents that include the corresponding Sdk.id or Sdk.pageId.
Provide a global documentation search form that calls searchDocumentation with the user’s query and optional filters, and display the results in a contextual panel.
Allow users to navigate from search results to the appropriate documentation pages (including SDK pages and API reference) using the pageId and pageType values returned by searchDocumentation.
Expose a link or interaction within the overview content that sends users to the API reference page when they want to compare SDK usage to raw HTTP API calls.
user_workflows:
View SDKs overview and highlighted language:
1. User opens sdks.html, optionally with a language query parameter in the URL (e.g., sdks.html?language=<language-key>).
2. The page initialization logic parses the URL to read the language parameter value, if present.
3. The page calls getSdksOverview with no parameters.
4. When getSdksOverview resolves, the page stores introMarkdown and the sdks array in page state.
5. The SDK intro section renders introMarkdown as markdown, including any inline links to other documentation pages.
6. The SDK grid component renders one SDK entry for each item in the sdks array.
7. If the language parameter is defined, the grid checks each Sdk.language value and marks the first matching SDK as pre-highlighted (e.g., visually emphasized and scrolled into view).

Select an SDK and go to its language-specific documentation:
1. User scans the SDK grid and identifies the SDK they want to use.
2. User clicks the SDK entry (card or row) in the grid.
3. The SDK grid component emits an onSdkCardClick event with the selected Sdk object (including its id, pageId, language, and name).
4. The page-level navigation handler uses the Sdk.pageId (and any routing configuration provided by the host application) to construct a URL for the language-specific SDK documentation page.
5. The navigation handler triggers a location change to the resolved SDK documentation URL, optionally including a query parameter such as sdkId=<Sdk.id> if required by the target page.
6. The browser navigates to the SDK language page, where the user can see detailed SDK methods and examples.

Use the SDK vs raw HTTP explanation and go to API reference:
1. User reads the markdown content rendered from introMarkdown in the SDK intro section.
2. The introMarkdown content includes narrative about when to use SDKs versus direct HTTP calls, possibly with a link that points to the API reference page.
3. User clicks the API reference link within the markdown.
4. The markdown renderer captures the click event (onLinkClick), inspects the link target, and determines that it should navigate to the API reference page.
5. The page-level navigation handler constructs the API reference URL (for example using routing rules or a known pageType target) and updates window.location to that URL.
6. The user is taken to the API reference where they can compare SDK-based code samples with raw HTTP requests.

Run a global documentation search from the SDKs Overview page:
1. User focuses the global header search input on the SDKs Overview page.
2. User types a search phrase related to SDK usage or another topic.
3. On submit, the header search form emits an onSubmit event with the query string and optional filter configuration (e.g., filters targeting specific pageType values such as sdk_language or api_reference, if the UI exposes such options).
4. The search form component calls searchDocumentation with the query and filters object.
5. When searchDocumentation resolves, the page stores the returned results array and total count in search state, and optionally encodes the query in the URL (e.g., sdks.html?language=<value>&q=<encoded-query>) so the search can be restored on reload.
6. A search results panel component displays the list of results with title and snippet for each item.
7. User clicks a search result; the panel emits an onResultClick event with the result’s pageId and pageType.
8. The navigation handler builds the destination URL using the result metadata and navigates the browser to that page (e.g., an SDK language page, an API reference endpoint, or another documentation page).

Change highlighted SDK language via URL manipulation or internal controls:
1. User wants to quickly highlight or share a link to a specific language SDK.
2. Either the user manually adds or edits the language query parameter in the browser’s address bar, or interacts with a language filter control on the page that programmatically updates the URL to include language=<Sdk.language> for the selected SDK.
3. The page detects the URL change (via a popstate listener or a re-render triggered by navigation) and re-runs the logic that reads the language parameter.
4. The SDK grid recomputes the highlighted SDK by matching Sdk.language against the new language parameter value.
5. The newly matching SDK entry becomes highlighted, and previously highlighted entries are un-highlighted.
interactions:
Page load and data fetch:
- Trigger: Browser loads sdks.html.
- Behavior: The initialization component reads the current URL query string, extracts the language and q (search query) parameters if present, and calls getSdksOverview. No other data is fetched.
- Outcome: introMarkdown and sdks are stored in page state and passed to child components.

SDK intro section link click:
- Trigger: User clicks a link rendered from introMarkdown.
- Behavior: The markdown renderer intercepts the click via onLinkClick, checks whether the href points to an internal documentation route (e.g., API reference or another doc page). If it is an internal route, it prevents default navigation and delegates to the page’s navigation handler; otherwise, it allows the default browser navigation.
- Outcome: Navigation to the target documentation page such as the API reference, or opening an external link as appropriate.

SDK card click:
- Trigger: User clicks an SDK entry in the grid.
- Behavior: The SDK card emits an onClick event with the associated Sdk object. The SDK grid translates this into onSdkCardClick at the grid level, forwarding the Sdk data.
- Outcome: The page navigation handler constructs a URL for the SDK language documentation associated with the selected Sdk and initiates browser navigation. The URL may include a query parameter that passes Sdk.id or Sdk.language to the target page.

Language selection / filter interaction:
- Trigger: User selects a language option from a language filter UI element (e.g., a select box or segmented control) that uses dynamic language values derived from the sdks array.
- Behavior: The filter component emits onLanguageChange with the selected language value. The page updates the URL query string to set language=<selected-language>, preserving any existing search parameter. It then updates local UI state so the SDK grid recomputes which SDK is highlighted or filtered.
- Outcome: The SDK grid emphasizes the selected language’s SDK and the updated URL can be copied or bookmarked to preserve this state.

Header search submit:
- Trigger: User submits the search form in the header while on the SDKs Overview page.
- Behavior: The search form component validates that the query is non-empty, then calls searchDocumentation with the query and constructed filters (if any). It also updates the URL to include q=<encoded-query> without reloading the page, so the search can be restored.
- Outcome: The search results overlay or panel displays the results returned by searchDocumentation, and the user can click a result to navigate.

Search result click:
- Trigger: User clicks an entry in the search results panel.
- Behavior: The search results panel emits onResultClick with a data payload containing title, snippet, pageId, pageType, and entityType as returned by searchDocumentation. The navigation handler resolves this into a concrete documentation URL and navigates to it.
- Outcome: Browser navigates to the selected documentation page and the search panel closes.

Browser navigation / back-forward affecting state:
- Trigger: User navigates back or forward in the browser history, changing query parameters like language or q without a full page reload (when history.pushState/replaceState is used).
- Behavior: A popstate listener in the page initialization component re-reads the current URL parameters, updates the highlighted SDK language, and optionally replays the searchDocumentation call if a q parameter is present.
- Outcome: The UI remains in sync with the URL: SDK highlight and search results reflect the state encoded in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: The SDKs Overview page derives its state exclusively from the current URL parameters and the responses from getSdksOverview and searchDocumentation. The primary URL parameters are: 1) language: a string passed to sdks.html (e.g., from index.html or faq.html) indicating which SDK language should be pre-highlighted. On every (re)render or popstate event, the page reads language from the query string. The SDK grid then checks each Sdk in the sdks array returned by getSdksOverview; the first Sdk whose language field exactly matches the language parameter value is marked as highlighted. If no match is found or language is absent, no SDK is highlighted by default. When a user selects a new language via onLanguageChange, the page updates the URL to set language to the chosen Sdk.language and recomputes the highlighted SDK; this update must use history.pushState or history.replaceState to avoid a full page reload while still encoding the state in the URL. 2) q: an optional search query parameter used to restore global search state. When the header search form submits, it calls searchDocumentation with the query string and any UI-defined filters and then updates the URL to include q as the encoded query. On page load or on popstate, if q is present, the page can use it to prefill the header search input and optionally re-run searchDocumentation to restore search results. No SDK list data is persisted locally; the only source of truth for SDKs is the sdks array returned by getSdksOverview. No user preferences are stored in localStorage or cookies; all shareable and restorable state is expressed via URL parameters and the server-provided content.

components.functionality: Initializes the SDKs Overview page by reading URL parameters, invoking getSdksOverview, and distributing introMarkdown and the sdks collection to child components. It also computes derived state such as the currently highlighted language and, optionally, the active search query.

Renders the introMarkdown string returned by getSdksOverview as markdown. This content explains the purpose of SDKs, when to use them versus direct HTTP calls, and may contain links to other documentation pages such as the API reference. It detects clicks on internal links and delegates navigation to the page-level router.

Provides optional language selection or filtering controls for the SDK list. It derives the list of available languages from the sdks array (using each Sdk.language field) and allows the user to select one. On selection, it emits an event that updates the language query parameter in the URL and informs the SDK grid which language should be highlighted.

Displays the collection of SDKs returned by getSdksOverview as a list or grid of interactive entries. It receives the current highlighted language from the parent state and marks the corresponding SDK entry as highlighted by matching the Sdk.language property. When the user clicks an SDK entry, it emits an event that carries the selected Sdk to the navigation handler.

Represents a single SDK entry within the grid, bound to one Sdk record. It displays the SDK’s name and description from the Sdk entity and can visually indicate whether it is currently highlighted. Clicking the card signals user intent to navigate to that SDK’s detailed documentation.

Listens for navigation-related events from other components (e.g., onSdkCardClick from sdk-grid and onLinkClick from sdk-intro-section, as well as onResultClick from the search results panel). Based on the event payload and any associated pageId or language data, it constructs destination URLs (such as SDK language documentation pages or the API reference) and updates the browser location accordingly.

Implements the global documentation search input visible in the page header. It accepts a query string from the user, optionally reads or writes the q parameter in the URL for state persistence, and calls searchDocumentation with the query and any active filters when submitted.

Displays the results returned by searchDocumentation after the user submits a query from the header search form. Each result shows the title and snippet and is clickable. Clicking a result emits an event with the result metadata (pageId, pageType, entityType) so that the navigation handler can route the user to the selected documentation page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a top-to-bottom, single main column flow to mirror the developer docs mockup. Inside the fixed central content container, the order is: global header (with search), then a two-column body with a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content area. Within the main content column, the sequence is: page title + underline (SDKs Overview), sdk-intro-section as an introductory block, sdk-language-filter as a compact control row, and then sdk-grid containing repeated sdk-card items. Non-visual logic components (sdks-overview-init, sdk-navigation-handler) execute behind the scenes without affecting visual ordering.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a top-to-bottom, single main column flow to mirror the developer docs mockup. Inside the fixed central content container, the order is: global header (with search), then a two-column body with a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main content area. Within the main content column, the sequence is: page title + underline (SDKs Overview), sdk-intro-section as an introductory block, sdk-language-filter as a compact control row, and then sdk-grid containing repeated sdk-card items. Non-visual logic components (sdks-overview-init, sdk-navigation-handler) execute behind the scenes without affecting visual ordering.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies about 10–12% of the viewport height and spans the full width. Below it, the content container is centered with generous left and right gutters. The left sidebar uses roughly 18–22% of the internal content width; the main content column uses the remaining 78–82%. Within the main column, text blocks and controls are full-width or up to 60–70% width to maintain comfortable line length. sdk-grid spans the full main column width, with sdk-card items filling 100% of their grid column. Vertical section gaps between intro, filters, and grid are 24–32px; internal gaps between related elements stay around 8–16px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps a moderately spacious density. Each major section (intro, filters, grid) is visually separated with clear white space. Paragraphs are short and constrained in width for readability. sdk-card rows are not stacked too tightly: each card has ample internal padding and 16–24px vertical gaps between cards or grid rows. The search area and results panel are streamlined, with no unnecessary decoration, emphasizing quick scanning and legibility.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the dark, fixed header (logo + Documentation nav + search) down to the page title and underline in the main column, then sequentially through the sdk-intro-section, sdk-language-filter, and finally into the sdk-grid of cards. The left sidebar provides a secondary, lighter-weight vertical reference aligned with the main column but kept visually subordinate. Within each sdk-card, a colored left bar and status/language label draw attention on the left, then the gaze moves horizontally toward the right-justified chevron indicating interactivity. The search-results-panel appears directly under the header search form, creating a focused top-right overlay that temporarily competes with the main flow without disrupting the overall structure.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a modern, minimalist developer documentation screen. A fixed-height header bar spans the full width at the top, about 10–12% of the viewport height, with the product logo on the left, a “Documentation” tab near the center-left, and a compact search form aligned to the right. Below the header, the main content sits inside a centered container with generous side gutters. This container is divided into a narrow left sidebar (around one-fifth of the width) and a wider main content column (about four-fifths). The sidebar starts with a subtle “Reference” label and a short vertical list of links, including an entry for “SDKs Overview,” aligned under each other with comfortable spacing and rendered in a lighter style so they do not overpower the main content. In the main column, the bold page title “SDKs Overview” appears near the top, aligned left, with a thin underline stretching partway across the column and about 16–24px of space below it. Immediately beneath, the sdk-intro-section forms a short block of explanatory text, constrained to a comfortable line length. About 24–32px below the intro sits the sdk-language-filter row—compact controls aligned to the left that allow picking a language or SDK category while leaving open whitespace to the right. Another 24–32px below, the sdk-grid fills the rest of the visible column with one or two columns of horizontally oriented sdk-card elements depending on screen width. Each card nearly spans its grid column, with a colored vertical accent bar on the far left, concise SDK language/name text, and a right-aligned chevron icon, all laid out on a light, flat background with ample padding. As the user scrolls, the header with its search form stays pinned at the top, while the sidebar and main content move underneath it. When the search is active, a search-results-panel drops down from the header beneath the search field on the right side, overlaying the top-right of the content area but respecting the central container width, offering a tidy, scrollable list of matches without disturbing the core left-sidebar + main-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sdks-overview-init component is a non-visual data-loader that sits logically at the very top of the main content column’s stack, just after the page shell (header and sidebar) has been laid out but before the visible SDK content appears. It does not occupy any visible space or alter the spacing between visible elements; instead, it runs as an invisible layer that ensures that, by the time the page title, intro, filters, and sdk-grid render, the list of SDKs and any language-specific highlighting are already determined. Conceptually, it is anchored to the main content region, operating in the background while the layout presents a seamless loading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic component that executes before and alongside layout rendering; it does not claim any width or height and scrolls with the main content implicitly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sdk-intro-section is the first visible content block in the main column beneath the page title and its underline. It is left-aligned and spans roughly 80–90% of the main content width, keeping paragraphs constrained for optimal readability. The top of this section sits about 16–24px below the underline of the “SDKs Overview” title, creating a clear but compact separation. The section’s internal padding follows the 16–24px rhythm: a little extra space above the first line of text and below the last paragraph, but otherwise visually tight so that it reads as a single cohesive intro. It extends down the page just enough to accommodate one or two short paragraphs, with 24–32px of whitespace below it before the sdk-language-filter appears, reinforcing the vertical flow from introduction to interactive controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section within the main content column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick or overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sdk-language-filter component is positioned directly beneath the sdk-intro-section, with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation to visually mark the transition from explanatory text to controls. It forms a single horizontal row of filter controls aligned to the left edge of the main content column, spanning about 50–70% of the column’s width, leaving airy whitespace to the right. The vertical height of the filter row is modest—around 40–48px, including any labels and control elements—so that it feels compact and functional rather than dominant. On wide screens, individual filter controls (chips, buttons, or a dropdown) are arranged inline with 8–16px gaps between them; on smaller screens, they may wrap to a second line but remain visually grouped. There is about 16–24px of space beneath the filter row before the sdk-grid starts, maintaining the overall spacious rhythm of the page while reinforcing that these controls act on the grid below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal control band within the main content column; it scrolls with the content and remains anchored between the intro section above and the grid below without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sdk-grid occupies the main body of the page below the sdk-language-filter, with a vertical offset of about 16–24px to separate controls from content. It spans the full width of the main content column (about 78–82% of the central container width) and aligns flush with the same left margin as the title and intro. On larger screens, the grid arranges sdk-card items in a two-column layout: each column takes roughly 48–50% of the main column width with a 16–24px gutter between columns. On narrower screens, the grid collapses to a single column where each sdk-card stretches nearly the full width of the main column, echoing the long endpoint rows from the mockup. Vertically, rows of cards are separated by about 16–24px, ensuring that each card is distinct but still clearly part of a continuous list. The grid extends downward as long as there are SDKs, maintaining consistent vertical spacing and allowing the page to scroll naturally. The content density is moderate: enough cards are visible at once to convey variety, but with sufficient white space around and between them to avoid visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid that reflows between one and two columns depending on viewport width; otherwise static and scrolling with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each sdk-card sits inside the sdk-grid as a horizontally oriented card that fills 100% of its grid column. The card is a flat rectangle with a light background and subtle border or hairline separation from the page background. On the far left edge of the card, a narrow vertical color bar—about 4–6px wide and the full height of the card—provides an immediate visual cue (for example, language family or SDK status). Inside the card, there is 16–20px of padding on all sides, consistent with the endpoint row pattern from the mockup. The primary text (SDK language or name) is aligned to the left, slightly above center if there’s a subtitle, and the chevron icon sits aligned to the right, vertically centered, indicating that the entire card is clickable. The card height is typically around 64–80px, depending on whether a secondary line of text is present. Cards are separated from each other vertically by about 16–24px within their column, and horizontally by the grid gutter between columns. On hover, the card may slightly change background shade or cast a minimal shadow, reinforcing its interactive nature without breaking the minimalist, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static clickable item within the grid; it expands horizontally to fill its column and responds visually to hover/focus states but does not move or float relative to other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sdk-navigation-handler is an invisible navigation controller associated conceptually with each sdk-card. It does not render any UI or take up space on the page; instead, it listens to interactions with the sdk-grid and cards (for example, clicks or keyboard selection) and then updates the URL or routes to the appropriate SDK detail documentation. Spatially, it lives in the same logical layer as the sdks-overview-init, tied to the main content column, but it has no width, height, or visual representation, leaving the layout of visible components unaffected.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component bound to sdk-card interactions; it scrolls with the document implicitly but has no visual footprint or positional behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-search-form is placed inside the fixed top header bar, aligned to the right side. The header itself spans the full viewport width and approximately 10–12% of its height (typically around 64px), with the content horizontally centered according to the site’s main container width. The search form sits on the right end of this container, horizontally aligned with the logo and navigation items to its left. The input field is a horizontally oriented rectangle, roughly 260–360px wide depending on available space, with 8–16px horizontal padding inside and a height of about 32–40px to provide comfortable click and tap targets. There is a small amount of breathing room (8–16px) between the search form and the header’s top and bottom edges, so the input visually sits in the vertical center of the header. The search form remains visible at all times as the user scrolls, acting as a persistent utility. It uses subtle borders and flat styling, in line with the overall minimalist design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored inside the fixed-position header; it remains pinned to the top-right of the viewport while the body content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-panel appears only when the header-search-form is active. It is a floating panel that drops down from the bottom edge of the header bar, aligned horizontally with the search form on the right side of the central content container. The panel is slightly narrower than the search form’s horizontal region plus some padding, typically occupying about 30–40% of the viewport width but never extending beyond the main content container’s right boundary. Its top edge is attached just below the header, creating a tight 0–4px gap so it feels visually connected to the search input. Vertically, the panel can extend downwards to cover up to 50–60% of the viewport height, with its own internal scroll if necessary. Inside, search results are displayed as a vertical list with 12–16px padding around the panel edges and 8–16px spacing between results, following the same clean, readable density as the rest of the page. The panel overlays the upper portion of the main content and sdk-grid but does not obscure the header or sidebar; the left sidebar remains visible to the left, reinforcing the sense that this is a transient overlay rather than a full-screen takeover.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay panel anchored to the header area; it is positioned absolutely or fixed relative to the header, appearing and disappearing above the scrolling main content while the header remains fixed.

name: Python SDK
title: Python SDK
description: Documentation page for installing, configuring, and using the Python SDK, with navigable modules, methods, and copyable Python code examples.

core_features: Load and display Python SDK page details (installation, authentication, usage overview) using getSdkPageDetails based on the sdkId URL parameter.
Render a module sidebar for the selected SDK using getSdkModules, with URL-driven selection via the module URL parameter.
Display the methods for the selected module using getSdkModuleDetails and allow selecting a method via click or via the method URL parameter.
Show detailed information for the selected SDK method using getSdkMethodDetails, including description and associated API endpoint metadata.
Render one or more Python SDK code examples for the selected method using data from getSdkMethodDetails, with support for highlighting a specific example via the highlightExample URL parameter.
Allow users to copy Python code examples using performCopyCodeSnippet, including delete actions that take an ID parameter such as a customer ID.
Provide navigation from an SDK method detail to the underlying REST API endpoint documentation (api_reference.html) using the apiEndpointId from getSdkMethodDetails.
Provide a link from the authentication instructions on this page to the Authentication documentation (authentication.html).
Integrate with global documentation search via searchDocumentation, allowing users to search and navigate to relevant SDK and API docs.
Maintain deep-linkable state via URL parameters (sdkId, module, method, highlightExample) so that specific modules, methods, and code examples can be shared and re-opened in the same state.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Open Python SDK page from SDKs overview.
1) User clicks a Python SDK link on sdks_overview, which navigates to sdk_python.html with a sdkId URL parameter.
2) On page load, the sdk-page-loader component reads sdkId from the URL and calls getSdkPageDetails(sdkId) and getSdkModules(sdkId).
3) The page renders SDK installation, authentication, and overview content from getSdkPageDetails, and a sidebar of modules from getSdkModules.
4) If no module URL parameter is present, the sdk-page-loader selects a default module based on the modules list (selection logic is entirely derived from the API response, not hardcoded) and updates the URL accordingly.
5) The user can now browse modules and methods within the Python SDK.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific module and method from another page.
1) User clicks a link (for example from api_reference.html or faq.html) that points to sdk_python.html with sdkId, module, and method URL parameters set.
2) On page load, sdk-page-loader reads sdkId, module, and method from the URL and calls getSdkPageDetails(sdkId) and getSdkModules(sdkId).
3) After modules are loaded, the sdk-module-resolver logic selects the module whose slug matches the module URL parameter and calls getSdkModuleDetails(selectedModule.id).
4) Once module methods are loaded, the sdk-method-resolver logic finds the method whose name matches the method URL parameter and marks it as selected in the module method list.
5) sdk-method-details-panel calls getSdkMethodDetails(selectedMethod.id) to load detailed method information and Python code examples.
6) The page scrolls the selected method entry and its details into view so the user immediately sees the referenced method.

Workflow: Highlight a specific code example (e.g., delete-by-id example) from the home page.
1) User clicks an example link on index.html that navigates to sdk_python.html with sdkId, module, method, and highlightExample URL parameters.
2) The initial load workflow for module and method selection proceeds as above.
3) Once getSdkMethodDetails has returned, sdk-method-details-panel passes the highlightExample URL parameter value to the sdk-code-example-block instances.
4) Each sdk-code-example-block compares highlightExample to its bound SdkCodeExample identifier (or other matching field provided in the API response). When a match is found, that example is marked as highlighted and scrolled into view.
5) The highlighted state is kept in sync with the URL, so copying the URL preserves which example is emphasized for future visitors.

Workflow: Explore module methods and navigate to the underlying REST API endpoint.
1) User selects a module in the sdk-modules-sidebar; this updates the module URL parameter and triggers getSdkModuleDetails for that module.
2) The sdk-module-method-list displays the methods returned by getSdkModuleDetails. The user clicks one method entry.
3) The click updates the method URL parameter and triggers getSdkMethodDetails for the chosen method.
4) sdk-method-details-panel renders the method description, ID parameter semantics, and associated code examples.
5) The user clicks a 'View underlying REST API endpoint' link within the method details. The page navigates to api_reference.html with endpointId set to the method.apiEndpointId from getSdkMethodDetails, and view set to 'docs'.
6) The API Reference page uses these parameters to open the corresponding REST endpoint documentation.

Workflow: Copy a Python SDK code example for deleting a resource by ID.
1) User navigates to a module (such as a Customers-type module) and selects a delete-type method from the sdk-module-method-list.
2) sdk-method-details-panel loads method details and Python code examples via getSdkMethodDetails.
3) sdk-code-example-block renders the example code, which includes an SDK method call with an ID argument (the actual method name and parameter are taken from the SdkCodeExample.code value, not hardcoded).
4) The user clicks the copy button on the desired Python example.
5) The copy button handler calls performCopyCodeSnippet with sourceType set to indicate an SDK code example, sourceId set to the SdkCodeExample.id, language set to the example language, and copiedContent set to the code text.
6) The UI layer copies the code to the clipboard and optionally shows a confirmation message based on the performCopyCodeSnippet response.

Workflow: Learn more about authentication from the Python SDK page.
1) On initial load, getSdkPageDetails returns authMarkdown content that is rendered in the sdk-auth-section component.
2) Within this section, the user clicks a 'Learn more about authentication' link.
3) The click navigates to authentication.html without additional parameters.
4) The Authentication page uses its own data models and interfaces to present full authentication details, which can be cross-referenced with the SDK examples the user saw previously.

Workflow: Use search to find a specific SDK method.
1) The user opens the global search control in the header-search-form component and enters a query describing an SDK method or operation.
2) On submit, header-search-form calls searchDocumentation with the query and appropriate filters (for example, including filters that limit results to SDK documentation if desired; filter values are taken from configuration or user input, not hardcoded).
3) The search results list is displayed (within a search overlay or dedicated results view managed by the header-search-form component) showing titles, snippets, and associated page IDs.
4) The user clicks a result pointing to this Python SDK page or to a specific method.
5) The click navigates to sdk_python.html with the appropriate URL parameters (sdkId, and optionally module, method, and highlightExample) derived from the selected search result.
6) The page reloads or updates state based on those URL parameters, selecting the corresponding module and method, and highlighting any referenced example.
interactions: On initial page load, read sdkId, module, method, and highlightExample from the URL query string and trigger data fetching via getSdkPageDetails and getSdkModules.

When the sdkId URL parameter changes (for example, by navigation from another docs page), re-fetch SDK page details and modules using getSdkPageDetails(sdkId) and getSdkModules(sdkId), and reset module/method selection appropriately.

When the user clicks a module in the sdk-modules-sidebar, update the module URL parameter, clear or update the method and highlightExample parameters, call getSdkModuleDetails with the selected moduleId, and update the selected module state.

When the module URL parameter changes (due to navigation or manual editing), resolve the corresponding SdkModule from the modules list and fetch its methods with getSdkModuleDetails; if the parameter does not match any module, apply a fallback module selection based on API-provided metadata.

When the user clicks a method in the sdk-module-method-list, update the method URL parameter, clear or update the highlightExample parameter, call getSdkMethodDetails with the selected methodId, and set this method as selected in the list.

When the method URL parameter changes, resolve and select the corresponding SdkMethod in the current module's method list (and, if necessary, adjust the selected module so that it matches the method that was requested) before calling getSdkMethodDetails.

When getSdkMethodDetails resolves, render method details and associated code examples; if a highlightExample URL parameter is present, pass it down so that sdk-code-example-block components can determine which example to highlight.
When the user clicks a 'View underlying REST API endpoint' link in sdk-method-details-panel, navigate to api_reference.html with endpointId set from method.apiEndpointId and view set to 'docs'.
When the user clicks a 'Learn more about authentication' link in the sdk-auth-section, navigate to authentication.html without modifying other URL parameters.

When the user clicks the copy button in sdk-code-example-block, call performCopyCodeSnippet with the example's metadata and code content; use the response to update any in-page feedback state (such as copy success indication).

When the user submits a query in header-search-form, call searchDocumentation and present results; when a result is clicked, navigate using the result's pageId and any associated entity information to construct sdkId, module, method, and highlightExample URL parameters if applicable.

When the browser's back/forward navigation changes the URL query parameters, re-run the same resolution logic used on initial load to keep the visible SDK module, method, and highlighted example in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is entirely driven by URL parameters and API responses, without using localStorage or hardcoded values. On every load or URL change, the following state resolution occurs:

1) sdkId: The page reads sdkId from the URL. If sdkId is present, getSdkPageDetails(sdkId) and getSdkModules(sdkId) are called. All subsequent module and method resolution is scoped to this sdkId. If sdkId is missing or invalid, the page can show an error state or a generic message, but does not assume any default SDK identifier.

2) Module selection (module parameter): After getSdkModules returns, the page inspects the module URL parameter. If it matches the slug of a SdkModule in the returned list, that module is selected and getSdkModuleDetails is called with its id. If it does not match any module, the page selects a module based solely on information returned by getSdkModules (for example, the first module in the ordered list or one marked as preferred in the API response) and updates the module URL parameter to reflect this selection.

3) Method selection (method parameter): After getSdkModuleDetails returns methods for the selected module, the page checks the method URL parameter. If a method name in the methods list matches the method parameter, that SdkMethod is selected and getSdkMethodDetails is called with its id. If no method matches but a method parameter is present, the page can attempt to resolve the method by querying other modules (if implemented) or can fall back to a default method selection within the selected module. In all cases, no method identifiers are hardcoded; all matching is based on the SdkMethod data from the API.

4) Code example highlighting (highlightExample parameter): Once getSdkMethodDetails returns, the page passes the highlightExample URL parameter value into the sdk-code-example-block components. Each block compares this value against identifiers or fields available on its bound SdkCodeExample (such as id). If a match is found, that example is flagged as highlighted, and the UI may scroll to it or visually emphasize it. If highlightExample does not match any example, no example is specially highlighted.

5) URL updates from interactions: When the user changes the module or method via UI interactions, the page updates the module, method, and highlightExample URL parameters to reflect the new selection, using the current SdkModule and SdkMethod data. This keeps the URL in sync with the visible state and ensures that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the current context.

6) Outgoing navigation parameters: When navigating to api_reference.html from a method detail, the endpointId parameter is derived from method.apiEndpointId obtained via getSdkMethodDetails, and view is set to 'docs'. When navigating to authentication.html, no additional parameters are required, and this navigation does not alter the current page's URL parameters.

By relying solely on URL parameters and API responses, the page maintains a consistent, deep-linkable state for SDK selection, module selection, method focus, and code example highlighting, without any dependency on localStorage or hardcoded identifiers.

components.functionality: Orchestrates initial data loading and subsequent updates when URL parameters change. Reads sdkId, module, method, and highlightExample from the URL, calls getSdkPageDetails(sdkId) and getSdkModules(sdkId), and coordinates module and method selection based on the responses.
Displays high-level information about the Python SDK, including name, language, and overview content. Renders installation and usage overview markdown provided by getSdkPageDetails.
Renders the authentication-related markdown for the Python SDK using authMarkdown from getSdkPageDetails and exposes a link to navigate to the full Authentication documentation.

Shows a navigable list of SDK modules (such as resource modules for customers, orders, payments) retrieved via getSdkModules. Highlights the currently selected module based on the module URL parameter and user clicks, and notifies other components when the module selection changes.

For the currently selected module, displays the list of SDK methods returned by getSdkModuleDetails, including methods that perform delete operations or other actions. Supports selecting a method via click or method URL parameter, and triggers loading of method details when a selection is made.

Displays detailed information for the selected SDK method by calling getSdkMethodDetails with the methodId. Shows method description, linked ApiEndpoint metadata (if present), and passes associated SdkCodeExample items to code example components. Includes a control to navigate to the underlying REST API endpoint documentation.

Renders an individual Python SDK code example for the selected method, using SdkCodeExample.code and related metadata. Supports a copy action via performCopyCodeSnippet and can highlight itself when its identifier matches the highlightExample URL parameter.

Handles user search input for documentation. Calls searchDocumentation with the user's query and optional filters, displays results, and navigates to selected result pages by constructing appropriate URLs (including sdkId, module, method, and highlightExample when the destination is an SDK entity).

Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters related to SDK selection (sdkId, module, method, highlightExample). Notifies other components when URL-driven state changes, and updates the URL when components change the selected SDK, module, method, or highlighted example.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top‑to‑bottom hierarchy inside a centered main content container. At the very top is a fixed full‑width header/navigation bar. Immediately beneath it, in the main column, comes the page title and introductory Python SDK description (sdk-header-section), followed by a compact authentication summary (sdk-auth-section). Below that, the primary 'Reference' content appears: the left side of the screen is the modules sidebar (sdk-modules-sidebar), while the right side hosts the methods area, which vertically stacks the method list (sdk-module-method-list) and, directly beneath it, the selected method’s details (sdk-method-details-panel) with inline code examples (sdk-code-example-block). Invisible technical components (sdk-page-loader and url-state-manager) overlay or sit behind this structure without altering the visual hierarchy.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top‑to‑bottom hierarchy inside a centered main content container. At the very top is a fixed full‑width header/navigation bar. Immediately beneath it, in the main column, comes the page title and introductory Python SDK description (sdk-header-section), followed by a compact authentication summary (sdk-auth-section). Below that, the primary 'Reference' content appears: the left side of the screen is the modules sidebar (sdk-modules-sidebar), while the right side hosts the methods area, which vertically stacks the method list (sdk-module-method-list) and, directly beneath it, the selected method’s details (sdk-method-details-panel) with inline code examples (sdk-code-example-block). Invisible technical components (sdk-page-loader and url-state-manager) overlay or sit behind this structure without altering the visual hierarchy.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A fixed header occupies roughly 10–12% of the viewport height at the top, spanning the full width. Below it, a centered content container (about 1040–1200px wide) holds a two‑column layout: the left sidebar uses roughly 20% of the container width, and the right main content uses the remaining 80%. Within the main content, the title and intro take the full width of the right column, followed by the authentication summary, then the methods sections. Vertical gaps between major sections (intro vs reference vs details) are about 32–40px, and intra‑section spacing (between headings, paragraphs, method cards, and code blocks) is in the 12–24px range. Cards and blocks have 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, keeping content comfortably away from edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and airy: short paragraphs, generous line height, and limited items per viewport. Method rows in the list are single‑line or single‑sentence labels with a small status bar, leaving ample breathing room. Method details and code examples are spaced so each block reads clearly as a distinct unit, with no more than a few core elements visible at once. Whitespace around the sidebar, between method rows, and around code blocks ensures the page feels structured and calm rather than crowded, aligning with the minimalist, developer‑documentation aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow begins at the dark fixed header (logo and search), then moves to the bold 'Python SDK' title with a subtle underline. The eye then naturally progresses downward through the intro text and authentication summary before reaching the 'Reference' region. In that region, the main vertical flow continues down the methods area, while the left sidebar provides a secondary, lighter vertical navigation track. Within the method list, each row’s colored left bar and status label catch attention first on the left, then the gaze travels right across the row to the chevron, suggesting interactivity. Selecting a method shifts attention downward to the details and then to code examples inside the details, maintaining a consistent top‑down reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a modern, minimal developer documentation screen with a fixed, full‑width top header bar and a clean two‑column layout beneath. The central content area is constrained to a comfortable width, floating with generous side margins so nothing touches the viewport edges. Below the header, the right main column opens with a strong 'Python SDK' title and an understated underline, followed by a concise introductory paragraph and a compact authentication summary block, all aligned on a single vertical axis. Under this, the layout settles into its working state: a narrow left sidebar lists Python SDK modules and sections, while the wider right area hosts a vertically stacked method list and a method details panel that expands below the list when a method is selected. Each method appears as a full‑width row with a slim colored bar on the left, a label and meta info in the middle, and a subtle chevron on the right, echoing the endpoint card style from the mockup. When expanded, the method details occupy the full width of the right column, with code examples presented as full‑bleed, horizontally padded code blocks. The overall experience is calm and structured, with consistent spacing and a clear downward reading flow anchored by the sidebar navigation on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a lightweight overlay that sits above the entire content area but below the fixed top navigation bar. It covers the central content container (both the sidebar and main content) with a subtle translucent backdrop that spans roughly the full width of the content container and the full height of the viewport area below the header. At its center, a small spinner or progress indicator is vertically and horizontally centered, occupying only a tiny fraction of the screen (around 5–8% of the viewport width), leaving plenty of visible structure beneath to maintain orientation. When inactive, this component does not occupy any space in the layout and is effectively invisible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay positioned relative to the viewport, covering the scrollable body area beneath the fixed header; toggled on/off without shifting underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section is positioned in the main content column on the right, directly beneath the fixed top header and aligned flush with the main column’s left edge. It spans the full width of the right content area (approximately 80% of the central container) and sits to the immediate right of the sidebar zone, though visually it appears topmost because the sidebar’s content starts slightly lower. The 'Python SDK' title is placed at the top, with a horizontal underline extending roughly 40–60% of the main column width underneath it, followed by a short introductory paragraph underneath with about 16–24px of vertical spacing. There is about 32–40px of vertical space below this section before the authentication summary begins, maintaining the generous section separation seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element in the main column; scrolls naturally with page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The authentication summary sits directly beneath the header section within the right main column, maintaining the same left alignment and width (about 80% of the central container). It appears as a compact content block with a short heading like 'Authentication' and 1–2 brief lines explaining how to configure API keys or environment, separated from the intro by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. The block itself has internal padding of about 16px vertically and 20–24px horizontally, and a subtle boundary (e.g., faint border or slightly tinted background) to distinguish it without adding heavy decoration. There is a generous 32–40px gap below it before the main 'Reference' and methods content begins, preserving a clear structural step in the vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the main column; scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The modules sidebar occupies a narrow vertical column on the left side of the content area, starting just below the fixed top header and running down the page’s scrollable area. It uses roughly 20% of the central content container’s width and is left‑aligned with a small internal left padding (16–24px) so text is not flush to the window edge. At the top of the sidebar, a subtle label such as 'Reference' or 'Modules' appears, followed by a short vertical list of links (e.g., 'Overview', 'Authentication', module names) with 8–12px vertical spacing between items. The sidebar text is slightly smaller and lighter in weight than the main column headings, making it visually subordinate while still clearly navigable. Its vertical alignment visually starts around the same height as the main content’s header section, and it maintains consistent gutter spacing to the right before the main column begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport once scrolled past the header, staying pinned from the top with a small offset (e.g., 16–24px below the fixed header) while the main content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The method list is positioned in the right main content column, below the authentication summary and roughly aligned horizontally with the sidebar’s module links. It spans almost the full width of the right column (about 80% of the central container, minus standard inner padding) and is arranged as a vertical stack of method rows. Each method row is a horizontally stretched card approximately 48–56px tall, with a slim colored vertical bar (4–6px wide) on the far left of the card, a method name and supplemental text (e.g., HTTP-like method or category) centered in the remaining content area, and a right‑aligned chevron icon indicating interactivity. Rows are separated by 8–12px of vertical space, with about 16–20px padding inside each card (12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal), mirroring the endpoint card style from the mockup. There is about 24–32px of space below the last row before any expanded method details appear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main column; selecting an item does not move the list but triggers the method details panel to appear or update below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The method details panel appears directly beneath the method list in the right main column, aligned to the same left and right bounds as the list so that it visually reads as the next step in the vertical flow. When no method is selected, this space is either collapsed or shows a minimal placeholder; once a method is selected, the panel expands to a full‑width block with its own heading (the method name), a brief description, parameter and response information, and one or more nested code example blocks. The top of the panel sits about 24–32px below the bottom of the method list to clearly separate list and details. Internally, sections within the panel (e.g., 'Parameters', 'Response', 'Examples') are separated by 16–24px, keeping the content readable and aligned with the page’s overall spacious rhythm. The bottom of this panel is followed by generous spacing before any subsequent sections or footer content, ensuring it feels self‑contained.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expanding/collapsing block within the main column; height adjusts based on the selected method’s content but it scrolls as part of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each code example block lives inside the method details panel, occupying the full available width of the right main column minus consistent inner padding. It appears as a single, horizontally stretched card with a monospaced code area and a small top‑right copy button. The block has 16–20px of vertical padding and about 20–24px of horizontal padding, with a slightly darker or tinted background to distinguish it from the surrounding text while still fitting the flat, minimal look. Above the code itself, there may be a slim header strip with a label like 'Python' or the file name, extending across 100% of the block’s width. Vertically, each code example sits 16–24px below the descriptive text and 16–24px above any following content, so each example reads as a clear, standalone element in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block nested within the method details; scrolls with the method details content and does not change page layout outside its parent panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is located inside the fixed top header bar, positioned toward the right side while leaving space on the left for the logo and a 'Documentation' label or navigation tabs. It occupies roughly 30–40% of the header width, appearing as a horizontally oriented input field with subtle borders and no heavy decoration, consistent with the minimal design. Vertically, the search input is centered within the header bar, with about 12–16px of padding around the clickable area to meet comfortable click targets. There is ample horizontal spacing (16–24px) between the search form and any adjacent header items, preventing visual crowding. The search field’s width stays constrained so that the header retains a balanced, uncluttered appearance across common viewport sizes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in place within the top navigation bar; remains visible at the top of the viewport at all times while the underlying content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not present any visible UI; it lives logically alongside the main content infrastructure, synchronizing the selected module, method, and scroll positions with the URL. It occupies no dedicated on‑screen space and does not alter the visual layout. Its presence is only reflected in behaviors such as deep‑linked methods opening with their details panel expanded and the correct method row highlighted in the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic layer; attaches to navigation and selection events without affecting spatial layout or dimensions.

name: Authentication
title: Authentication
description: Page that explains supported authentication schemes and provides interactive, multi-language code examples for authenticating API requests, including cURL commands with Authorization headers and copy-to-clipboard actions.

core_features: Load and display an overview of authentication and the list of available authentication methods using getAuthenticationOverview.
Allow users to deep-link into a specific authentication method via the URL method parameter and auto-select the corresponding method section on page load.
Fetch and display detailed instructions and security notes for a selected authentication method using getAuthMethodDetails.
Fetch and display language-specific authentication code examples (including cURL with Authorization headers) for the selected method using getAuthCodeExamplesForMethod.
Provide language tabs for switching between code examples and pre-select a language tab based on the URL lang parameter.
Allow users to select the cURL language tab when available and view the full authenticated cURL command returned by getAuthCodeExamplesForMethod.
Allow users to copy any authentication code example using performCopyCodeSnippet, including the full authenticated cURL command when the cURL tab is selected.
Support documentation-wide search from this page using searchDocumentation, allowing users to quickly jump to related docs.
Provide navigation links to return to the main API Reference and to the SDKs documentation after reviewing authentication details.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Arrive on Authentication page and see methods:
1. User navigates to authentication.html (optionally with method and lang URL parameters).
2. On load, the page container component calls getAuthenticationOverview with the current Authentication Page identifier.
3. The page renders the introMarkdown and the list of AuthMethod entries returned in methods.
