component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A simple inline navigation element within the normal flow. It does not stick or float; it scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-faq-cta sits directly beneath the status page link in the same 'Related resources' band. It aligns to the same left and right edges and may either share the same card-like row style as the status link or be presented as a more textual call-to-action (e.g., a line of text with a right-arrow icon). In either case, there is a small top margin of 8–16px from the status link above. The CTA text is slightly more prominent than a regular inline link (e.g., bolder weight or a subtle accent color) but still visually secondary to the main channel list and search form. The row or text block is kept compact with modest padding (8–12px vertically), ensuring it reads as a clean exit path at the bottom of the page rather than a primary interactive zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level navigation element that scrolls with the page. It does not expand or collapse and has no special spatial behavior.

name: Changelog
title: API Changelog
description: Chronological list of API, SDK, and documentation changes with filters for tags, endpoints, and breaking changes, supporting deep links and navigation to related reference pages.

core_features: Retrieve and display a chronological list of changelog entries by calling getChangelogEntries with filters derived from URL parameters and in-page controls.
Filter changelog entries by tag and endpoint identifier, synchronizing the selected filters with URL query parameters so that views are deep-linkable and shareable.
Highlight and optionally isolate entries marked as breaking changes using the isBreaking field, with a dedicated toggle for showing only breaking changes.
Allow users to expand changelog entries to see full summaries, tags, and related endpoints, and navigate from those endpoints to their detailed API reference pages.
Provide an in-page way to refine results (e.g., text filter, tags, endpoint filter chips) using getChangelogEntries filters without relying on any hardcoded tag, endpoint, or date values.

Expose a documentation search input on this page that calls searchDocumentation so users can search across all documentation (including but not limited to changelog entries) from within the changelog context.
Link to the roadmap information on about.html from relevant changelog content so users can understand how specific changes relate to the long-term direction of the API.
user_workflows:
Browse recent changes:
1. User opens changelog.html without query parameters.
2. On initial load, the page reads the current URL (no tag or endpointId present) and constructs a default filters object.
3. The page calls getChangelogEntries with this default filters object (e.g., ordered by most recent first, order handled server-side).
4. The changelog-entry-list component renders entries returned by getChangelogEntries in date order, displaying key fields such as date, title, summary, isBreaking, and tags.
5. The user clicks a specific entry row to expand it; the changelog-entry-item component reveals additional details (e.g., full summary text and relatedEndpointIds) without leaving the page.

View changes related to a specific tag (deep link from another page):
1. From index.html or faq.html, the user clicks a link that navigates to changelog.html with a tag query parameter in the URL.
2. On load, the changelog page parses the tag parameter and reflects it in the filter UI (e.g., shows the tag as selected in the tag picker or as an active chip).
3. The page constructs a filters object that includes the tag parameter and calls getChangelogEntries.
4. Only entries whose tags match the supplied tag parameter are rendered in the changelog-entry-list.
5. If the user clears the tag filter via the UI, the tag parameter is removed from the URL, and getChangelogEntries is called again with the updated filters (now without that tag).

Investigate changes to a specific endpoint (deep link from API reference):
1. In api_reference.html, the user interacts with a control (e.g., a link near an endpoint) that sends them to changelog.html with an endpointId parameter in the URL.
2. On load, the changelog page reads the endpointId parameter and updates the endpoint filter UI to show that a specific endpoint-based filter is active.
3. The page calls getChangelogEntries with a filters object that includes the endpointId, so only entries that reference this endpoint (via relatedEndpointIds) are returned.
4. The user scans the filtered changelog-entry-list to see all changes that impacted this endpoint (including breaking and non-breaking changes, visually distinguished by isBreaking).
5. If the user disables the endpoint filter from the filter bar, the endpointId parameter is removed from the URL and getChangelogEntries is re-run without that filter.

Focus on breaking changes and navigate to affected endpoints:
1. While viewing the changelog list, the user enables a "breaking changes only" toggle in the filter bar.
2. The page updates the URL by adding or updating a dedicated breaking-related parameter that indicates this filter is on.
3. The page calls getChangelogEntries with a filters object that includes a constraint on isBreaking.
4. The changelog-entry-list now shows only entries where isBreaking is true.
5. Within a breaking entry, the user clicks one of the related endpoints (resolved from relatedEndpointIds). This triggers navigation to api_reference.html with endpointId set to the clicked endpoint identifier and version set based on metadata associated with that changelog entry (clickedEntryVersion), passed through the URL.
6. The user lands on api_reference.html filtered or focused on that specific endpoint version to review its detailed documentation.

Search documentation from the changelog page:
1. The user starts typing in the documentation search input available on the changelog page (docs-search-bar component).
2. On submission (or as the user types, depending on the interaction design), the page calls searchDocumentation with the entered query string and filters that constrain results to relevant documentation scope (for example, to changelog-related entities or to the entire documentation set, as configured server-side).
3. The docs-search-results component displays the returned results list, each showing a title and snippet for context.
4. The user clicks one of the search results to navigate to the corresponding page (pageId mapped to a filename, such as api_reference.html, guides pages, or the changelog page itself), with any required query parameters applied as indicated by the result metadata.
5. If the user selects a result that points back to the changelog page with specific filters, the page reloads or updates in-place, parses the new query parameters, and calls getChangelogEntries with the new filters.

Explore roadmap context from breaking or significant changes:
1. While reading a particular changelog entry, the user sees a link or control labeled to indicate more information about long-term plans.
2. When the user activates this control, the page navigates to about.html with no additional query parameters, as defined in outgoing connections.
3. The user reads the roadmap or strategy content on about.html.
4. If about.html links back to the changelog with specific parameters (e.g., to show entries associated with a particular initiative), the user can follow that link and the changelog page will reinitialize its state based on those query parameters and re-fetch entries via getChangelogEntries.
interactions: Page load and URL parsing: On every load or when the browser URL changes (e.g., via back/forward navigation), the page parses query parameters such as tag, endpointId, and any additional parameters used for filtering or pagination. The result is mapped into an internal filters object that is passed into getChangelogEntries.

Tag filter interaction: When the user selects or deselects a tag in the changelog-filter-bar (for example, using a dropdown or chips), the component updates the tag parameter in the URL to match the newly selected tag set and triggers a re-fetch via getChangelogEntries with the updated filters.

Endpoint filter interaction: When the user chooses an endpoint filter within the page (e.g., from an endpoint selector or from a tag-like chip representing an endpoint), the endpointId parameter in the URL is set or updated. The changelog list is then refreshed by calling getChangelogEntries so only entries with relatedEndpointIds matching the selected endpoint are shown.

Breaking changes toggle: When the user toggles a control that indicates whether only breaking changes should be shown, the component updates a breaking-related parameter in the URL to reflect the state of this toggle. A new call to getChangelogEntries is made with a filter that constrains results to entries where isBreaking matches the toggle state.

Entry expand/collapse: When the user clicks an entry header (e.g., on the title or on an explicit expand icon), the changelog-entry-item toggles between collapsed and expanded states. Expanded state reveals additional textual details and relatedEndpointIds; this interaction does not modify URL parameters or re-fetch data, as it relies on data from the existing getChangelogEntries response.

Endpoint link click inside entry: In an expanded entry, each related endpoint is rendered as a clickable control. When the user clicks one, the page uses the corresponding endpoint identifier (from relatedEndpointIds) and any associated version metadata to navigate to api_reference.html with endpointId and version set as query parameters, matching the outgoing_connections specification.

Clear all filters: When the user activates a "clear filters" action, the changelog-filter-bar removes filtering-related parameters (such as tag, endpointId, and breaking-only) from the URL. The page then calls getChangelogEntries with the newly simplified filters object and rerenders the list.

Pagination or incremental loading: When the user requests more results (via explicit page controls or a "load more" button), the page adjusts a pagination-related query parameter (e.g., page index or cursor token) in the URL and calls getChangelogEntries with this pagination state included in the filters object. The changelog-entry-list updates to show the new subset of entries or append them, depending on the pagination model.

Documentation search submission: When the user submits a query in the docs-search-bar component on the changelog page, an event handler calls searchDocumentation with the query and any configured filters. The docs-search-results component then binds to the returned results and renders them for the user to interact with.

Roadmap link click: When the user clicks the roadmap link associated with a changelog entry or a general control on the page, the browser navigates to about.html with no additional query parameters, following the outgoing_connections definition.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state of the changelog page is derived entirely from URL query parameters and the responses from getChangelogEntries and searchDocumentation.

1. URL parameters -> filter state mapping:
- tag: If present, it is treated as the currently active tag filter. The filter bar shows this tag as selected, and getChangelogEntries is called with a filters object that includes this tag value.
- endpointId: If present, it is interpreted as the currently selected endpoint filter. The filter bar displays that an endpoint is selected, and getChangelogEntries includes this endpoint identifier in its filters so that only entries with relatedEndpointIds matching this value are returned.
- breaking-related parameter (e.g., a boolean or flag): If present, it determines whether only entries with isBreaking set to true should be returned. The toggle UI is initialized according to this parameter and passed into getChangelogEntries via its filters.
- Pagination-related parameter: If present (such as a page index or cursor token), it defines which subset of the changelog entries the user is viewing. This value is passed into getChangelogEntries as part of the filters object.
- Optional text filter parameter: If the page supports a text-based filter for the entry list itself, this parameter is parsed and included in the filters object sent to getChangelogEntries, allowing server-side matching of entry titles or summaries.

2. State initialization:
- On initial load, the page reads the current URL query string and constructs an internal representation of the filter state that includes tag, endpointId, breaking-only, pagination, and any text filter values. No assumptions are made about the specific values; they are treated as raw strings or booleans derived from the URL.
- This filter state is passed to getChangelogEntries as the filters argument. The response is stored in memory for the duration of the page view and bound to the changelog-entry-list and related components.

3. State updates and URL synchronization:
- Whenever the user interacts with the filter bar (tag selection, endpoint selection, breaking toggle, pagination controls, or clearing filters), the page updates the URL query parameters using standard navigation methods (e.g., updating the query string) without full page reload where supported.
- After the URL has been updated, the filter state is recomputed from the updated URL, ensuring that the URL remains the single source of truth for filter state.
- A new call to getChangelogEntries is made with the updated filters object, and the list of entries is re-rendered.

4. Search state using searchDocumentation:
- The docs-search-bar maintains its input value locally in the component, but its submitted query may also be reflected in a query parameter if the design requires sharing or bookmarking of search state.
- Upon submission, searchDocumentation is called with the query and any conceptual filters relevant to documentation type or scope. The returned results are stored as transient state associated with the docs-search-results component and do not modify the changelog entry list unless the user follows a result that leads back to changelog.html with its own query parameters.

5. Navigation to other pages:
- When navigating to api_reference.html from an endpoint link, the target URL is constructed using endpointId and version values derived from the clicked changelog entry context. No localStorage is used; the receiving page (api_reference.html) will read these parameters from its own URL to initialize its state.
- When navigating to about.html, no additional state is carried in local storage; only standard navigation occurs as explicit in the outgoing_connections definition.

Throughout, localStorage or any other client-side persistent storage is not used. All persistent state is encoded in the URL, and all dynamic data is obtained by calling getChangelogEntries and searchDocumentation with filter and query arguments derived from that URL-driven state.

components.functionality: Reads current filter-related URL parameters (tag, endpointId, breaking-only, pagination where relevant) and presents controls to adjust them. On user interaction, it updates the URL query parameters accordingly and triggers a refresh of the changelog list via getChangelogEntries.

Displays a list of changelog entries returned by getChangelogEntries, respecting the current filters and order determined by the server. Each list item includes key fields such as date, title, summary snippet, isBreaking indicator, and tags, and delegates details to the changelog-entry-item component.

Represents a single changelog entry with expandable details. In collapsed state, shows minimal summary information. In expanded state, shows full summary, tags, breaking-change indicator, and related endpoints derived from relatedEndpointIds, and exposes navigation actions to related API endpoints and the roadmap page.

Within an expanded changelog entry, renders a list of clickable links for each endpoint referenced in relatedEndpointIds. When a link is clicked, constructs a target URL to api_reference.html including endpointId and any available version metadata associated with the changelog entry, and initiates navigation.

Provides controls for changing the current page or loading additional entries. Updates a pagination-related URL parameter to reflect the newly selected page or cursor, and triggers a new getChangelogEntries call using this value in the filters object.

Offers an input field on the changelog page for users to search across documentation content. On submission, calls searchDocumentation with the current query and optional filters, and passes results to the docs-search-results component.

Displays the list of documentation search results returned by searchDocumentation, including titles and snippets. Each result links to its associated page using pageId and any applicable mapping to filenames, optionally including query parameters that further scope the target page.

Centralizes parsing of URL query parameters into a filter state object and orchestrates calls to getChangelogEntries whenever relevant parameters change. Ensures that filter state is always derived from the URL and that UI components remain in sync with this state.
Provides a reusable control that navigates the user to about.html when activated, in order to view roadmap or long-term change information related to the changelog.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s two-column documentation layout: a fixed full-width top header, a narrow left sidebar for documentation navigation, and a wide right main content column. Inside the main column, content flows linearly top-to-bottom: page title with underline, brief intro text, an optional roadmap link, then the docs search bar, followed by a horizontal changelog filter bar, then the chronological list of changelog entries. Each entry expands vertically to show details and affected endpoints. Pagination controls appear at the very bottom. Search results reuse the same list area, temporarily replacing the default changelog list when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s two-column documentation layout: a fixed full-width top header, a narrow left sidebar for documentation navigation, and a wide right main content column. Inside the main column, content flows linearly top-to-bottom: page title with underline, brief intro text, an optional roadmap link, then the docs search bar, followed by a horizontal changelog filter bar, then the chronological list of changelog entries. Each entry expands vertically to show details and affected endpoints. Pagination controls appear at the very bottom. Search results reuse the same list area, temporarily replacing the default changelog list when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies ~10–12% of viewport height and remains fixed. Below it, the content container is centered with generous left/right padding. The left sidebar takes roughly 18–22% of the container width; the right main column takes 78–82%. Within the main column, key horizontal components (search bar, filter bar, lists) span ~90–100% of the main column width. Vertical spacing follows the scale: 32px between major sections (title, intro, filters, list), 16–24px between related elements (paragraphs, cards, controls), and 12–16px internal padding within cards and bars.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page stays moderately spacious, prioritizing legibility: short paragraphs, restrained number of controls visible at once, and comfortable white space between entries. Each changelog item is a distinct block with clear breathing room above and below (~16–24px). The filter bar and search bar are compact but not cramped, with clear separation between controls (~8–16px gaps). Pagination and secondary links use smaller sizes and more whitespace to avoid crowding the main reading flow.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye moves from the dark fixed header to the bold 'Changelog' title in the main column, then down through the intro text and roadmap link. Next it naturally hits the search bar (prominent input), then the filter bar directly beneath, which anchors scanning across tags, endpoints, and breaking changes. From there, the vertical stack of changelog entry cards guides the eye downward chronologically. Within each card, a subtle left-colored bar and strong entry title catch attention, then the eye moves down to the summary text and across to the affected endpoint links. The left sidebar forms a subdued vertical reference, while pagination and secondary navigation sit at the bottom and top-right respectively, outside the main reading lane.

layout.overall_description: Below a fixed, full-width documentation header, the page content sits in a centered container split into a narrow left navigation sidebar and a wider right main column. The left column lists documentation sections and collections in small, understated text, while the right column holds the Changelog content and commands attention. At the top of the main column, the word 'Changelog' appears as a strong page title aligned left, with a thin underline stretching partway across the column. A short introductory paragraph sits just below, explaining the purpose of the changelog. On the same horizontal band or just beneath the intro, a subtle 'View roadmap' link is aligned to the right, offering a secondary navigation path without competing with the title. Below this header area, a wide search bar runs horizontally across most of the main column, visually centered in the content lane but left-aligned with the title. Directly under the search bar, a compact, horizontal filter bar spans the column with pill-like controls and dropdowns for tags, endpoints, and breaking changes, forming a stable reference line that may stick just under the global header when scrolling. The primary content region beneath is a vertically stacked list of changelog entry cards, each stretching nearly the full width of the main column, with comfortable gaps between them. Each card has a subtle colored bar along its left edge, a clearly styled date and title at the top, a concise description below, and a small cluster of endpoint links grouped near the bottom or right side. As the user scrolls, the left sidebar and header remain in place, while the main content column scrolls. At the very bottom of the main column, centered pagination controls appear with generous top margin, demarcating the end of the list. When a search is performed, the central list area switches to show search results in the same stacked format, preserving the vertical reading rhythm and overall minimalist, documentation-style aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The changelog filter bar is positioned in the main content column, directly below the docs search bar and above the changelog entry list. It spans nearly the full width of the main column (about 90–100%, aligned with the content grid) and has a low-profile, horizontally oriented design. The bar height is roughly 48–56px, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding inside. From left to right, it contains filter controls: a tags selector, an endpoints selector, a 'breaking changes only' toggle, and possibly a date range or sort control. Controls are laid out in a single row with 8–16px gaps, left-aligned; if space runs out on smaller widths, items wrap onto a second line while maintaining the same left alignment. The bar maintains at least 16–24px of vertical space above (from the search bar) and 16–24px below (to the first changelog entry card), reinforcing the separation of filtering from the content list. Visually, it feels like a subtle, flat card with a light background and a faint bottom border rather than a heavy panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The filter bar is horizontally fluid within the main column but vertically anchored just below the header/search area. On scroll, it becomes sticky relative to the viewport, locking to just beneath the fixed global header so that filters remain accessible while the list of changelog entries scrolls underneath. It does not overlap content; instead, the content starts below it when it sticks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The changelog entry list occupies the central vertical portion of the main column, starting just below the filter bar (or below the search results header when searching). It spans the full content width of the main column, with consistent left and right alignment to the title and filters. The list is purely vertical: each changelog-entry-item appears as a separate block card, stacked top-to-bottom with about 16–24px of vertical gap between each item. There is 24–32px of margin above the first entry to clearly separate it from the filter bar. The list extends downward as far as needed, forming the core scrollable content in the main column between the filter bar and the pagination controls. No borders are drawn around the list as a whole; the rhythm and spacing of the cards define its boundaries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This list scrolls normally within the main content area. It dynamically hosts either the default chronological changelog-entry-items or, when a search is active, the docs-search-results items in the same stacked format. It does not have fixed or sticky behavior; its position is defined by its top margin below the filter bar and ends above the pagination controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each changelog entry item is rendered as a horizontally stretched, card-like block aligned with the main content grid. The card spans approximately 100% of the main column width minus internal padding, with 16–24px of padding on all sides. Along the far left edge of the card, a slim vertical colored bar (4–6px wide) runs the full height of the card, echoing the endpoint row style from the mockup and indicating status or severity (e.g., a more intense color for breaking changes). Immediately to the right of this bar, within the card, the date and version label sit at the top left, followed by the entry title in a slightly larger, bold font on the same line or just beneath. Beneath the title, a short summary paragraph occupies one or two lines, constrained to maintain a comfortable line length. Below the summary and aligned left is a horizontal row of small tags or badges (e.g., 'SDK', 'API', 'Docs') with 8px gaps. At the bottom of the card, the changelog-endpoint-link-list is aligned to the left, either in the same row as the tags (if space allows) or as a line directly beneath them, visually grouped with modest vertical separation (~8–12px). On the far right of the card’s top row, aligned with the title, a subtle chevron icon suggests click-through to a detailed view, mirroring the endpoint row behavior in the mockup. Cards have 16–24px vertical spacing between each other, ensuring clear separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each card is a static block within the vertical list. When focused or hovered, the card can slightly elevate (subtle shadow or border darkening) without changing its footprint, reinforcing interactivity. It does not stick or float; it flows with the document as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The endpoint link list sits inside each changelog-entry-item, near the lower portion of the card. It is aligned flush left with the card’s content text, adhering to the same inner padding. It consists of a compact horizontal row of inline links representing affected endpoints, possibly styled as small pills or underlined text links. Links are separated by 8–12px horizontal gaps; if there are many endpoints, the list wraps onto multiple lines while staying aligned left, preserving a neat rectangular block. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing above the first endpoint link (from the preceding summary/tags) and 8–16px below to the bottom padding of the card, so the links feel like part of the card content rather than an attached footer. The density is restrained: no heavy borders or icons, just clear text links in a simple, flat style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The endpoint link list has no special spatial behavior; it occupies only the interior of its parent changelog-entry-item and grows vertically as links wrap. It respects the card’s padding and never extends beyond its inner content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are placed at the bottom of the main content column, directly beneath the last changelog entry card or search result. They are centered horizontally within the main column and occupy a relatively small vertical height (around 40–48px). There is a generous top margin of about 32–40px to separate the pagination clearly from the last list item, creating a visual sense of an endpoint to the current page of content. The controls consist of previous/next buttons and page numbers arranged in a horizontal row with 8–12px spacing. The overall width of the pagination group is modest, leaving ample whitespace to the left and right for visual balance. The style is flat and minimal, using subtle borders or background fills on the current page indicator but avoiding heavy UI treatments.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Pagination is a static block anchored to the bottom of the list region. It scrolls with the content and is visible only when the user has reached the lower part of the main column. It does not stick to the viewport and does not overlap any other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs search bar is positioned in the main column below the page title and introductory text, and above the changelog filter bar. It is aligned to the left edge of the main content grid but spans a majority of the width, roughly 60–70% of the main column, keeping a comfortable margin on the right for visual balance. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of space above the search bar (from the intro paragraph) and 16–24px below it (to the filter bar). The search bar itself is a single-line input with a clear left-aligned search icon and placeholder text, styled in a flat, understated way consistent with developer docs: a light border, subtle background, and 12–16px internal padding. Its height is around 40–48px, making it a prominent but not oversized interactive element. The search bar defines a strong horizontal anchor early in the flow, but remains visually subordinate to the main 'Changelog' title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The search bar is a static element in the main column’s upper region. It scrolls away as the user moves down the page; it does not stick to the viewport. Activating it (entering a query) affects the content shown in the list area below, but the bar itself remains in place and maintains its size and position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The docs search results appear in the same general vertical region as the changelog entry list, directly beneath the filter bar (or a small results header, if implemented). When search is active, this results list replaces or overlays the default chronological changelog-entry-list content, but it maintains the same full-width alignment and stacked-card structure to preserve continuity. Each result item aligns with the main column grid and uses a similar card style to changelog-entry-item: full-width blocks with 16–24px internal padding, 16–24px gaps between cards, and a left-to-right top layout of title and metadata. If the results are specifically changelog entries, they may reuse the same card pattern; if they span other doc types, each result still appears as a simple, one- or two-line card with title, snippet, and possibly a small badge indicating type. The list extends downward and ends above the pagination controls, which may be repurposed or hidden depending on search behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The search results share the same scrollable area as the changelog-entry-list. They are mutually exclusive with the default list: when results are present, the default list is hidden from view. The results list itself has no special behavior; it scrolls with the page, and its height adjusts to the number of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The changelog-route-sync component operates behind the scenes within the main content area. It has no visible UI and therefore no explicit on-screen footprint. Conceptually, it is associated with the main column container and the changelog list: it listens to scroll position, active filters, search queries, and selected entries, and synchronizes these with the URL for deep linking. Its functional role is to ensure that navigating directly to a particular changelog entry or filtered state restores the correct scroll position and filter configuration in the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This component is non-visual and occupies no layout space. It is logically attached to the main column and may influence scroll behavior (e.g., scrolling to a specific entry on load), but it does not alter the physical layout or introduce sticky or floating UI on its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The changelog roadmap link is positioned near the top of the main content column within the page header area. On the first visible row of content, the 'Changelog' page title is aligned to the left, and the roadmap link sits on the same horizontal line, aligned to the right edge of the main column. If space is constrained, the roadmap link may drop to the line immediately beneath the title but remain right-aligned. There is about 8–12px of vertical spacing between the title underline and the roadmap link if they fall on separate lines, keeping the header visually cohesive. The link is styled as a subtle textual link or a light-outline button with minimal padding (8–12px horizontal, 6–8px vertical), signaling secondary importance. It does not extend across the column; its width is just enough to fit the label, preserving plenty of surrounding whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The roadmap link is static within the header region and scrolls away with the main content. It does not stick or float. Its position is anchored relative to the page title and the right edge of the main column, maintaining consistent alignment across viewport sizes.

name: FAQ
title: FAQ
description: FAQ page where developers can browse and search common questions about the API, filter by category, and navigate into deeper reference and guide documentation.

core_features: Load all FAQ entries via getFaqEntries and present them as a browsable, collapsible list of questions and answers.
Filter FAQs by category using the incoming category URL parameter and user-selectable category controls.
Provide a search form that calls searchDocumentation so users can search across FAQs and related documentation.
Show search results from searchDocumentation with direct navigation into the relevant documentation pages.
From each FAQ answer, offer related documentation links based on relatedPageIds (e.g., auth, rate limits, webhooks, SDKs, API reference) and navigate with appropriate URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Browse FAQs by category:
1. User navigates to faq.html, optionally with a category query parameter set by another page.
2. The page controller reads the category URL parameter.
3. The page calls getFaqEntries (once) to retrieve all available FAQ entries.
4. The controller derives the available categories from the returned categorySlug values.
5. If the URL category matches one of the available categories, that category is selected; otherwise a default (such as all categories) is used.
6. The FAQ list component receives the full FAQ collection plus the currently selected category and displays only entries whose categorySlug matches (or all entries if no category is selected).
7. The user can change the category using the category filter component; this updates the category URL parameter and re-filters the list without extra API calls.

Search for help across documentation:
1. On faq.html, the user enters a search term into the FAQ search form.
2. On form submit, the search form invokes searchDocumentation with the query text and a filters object that can include the current FAQ category and any documentation scope configured for this page.
3. The page stores the search results returned by searchDocumentation and switches the view into a 'search mode' where search results are shown alongside or instead of the FAQ list.
4. The user reviews the results, which show title, snippet, and associated page information from searchDocumentation.
5. Clicking a search result navigates the user to the corresponding documentation page (for example, a detailed authentication, rate limit, webhook, SDK, or API reference section), using the mapped filename and any required URL parameters (such as resource identifiers or method hints).

Open a FAQ answer and navigate to deeper docs:
1. User sees a filtered list of FAQ questions.
2. The user clicks on a question row; the corresponding FAQ item expands, showing the rendered answerMarkdown.
3. The answer may contain inline links rendered from markdown and/or a separate list of related links derived from relatedPageIds.
4. When the user clicks a related link, the FAQ item component uses the relatedPageId to resolve the target Page and constructs the appropriate navigation URL (for example, adding any required query parameters for that target page).
5. The browser navigates to the target documentation page (e.g., authentication, rate limits, webhooks overview, SDK docs, or API reference) with the appropriate URL parameters set so that the target page can focus on the relevant section.
interactions: Initial load: on page mount, read the category query parameter from the URL and call getFaqEntries. When the promise resolves, populate internal FAQ state and compute distinct category values.

Category selection: when the user selects a category in the category filter component, update the category query parameter in the URL (using client-side navigation) and re-derive the filtered FAQ list based on categorySlug.
FAQ expand/collapse: clicking a question toggles its expanded state; only the state for that faqId is changed, and the corresponding answerMarkdown is shown or hidden.
Deep-link opening: if the URL includes a hash fragment matching a faqId, the matching FAQ item auto-expands after getFaqEntries data is available.

Search submission: when the user submits the search form, validate that the query is non-empty, then call searchDocumentation with the query and filters (including current category and any page-specific filter configuration). Store the results and switch view mode to show search results.

Search result click: clicking a search result retrieves its pageId and other metadata from the searchDocumentation result object and navigates to the resolved page filename, optionally adding query parameters or hash anchors derived from entityType/entityId.

Related link click in an FAQ answer: clicking a related link uses relatedPageIds (and any associated configuration) to construct navigation URLs such as auth, rate limits, webhooks, SDK, or API reference pages, with their expected query parameters. The page then navigates to that URL.

URL parameter change: when the category query parameter changes due to navigation from another page or user interaction, the page controller listens to URL changes, updates the current category state, and re-filters the FAQs without re-calling getFaqEntries unless a full reload occurs.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the category query parameter in the URL. On initial load, the page reads category from the query string. After getFaqEntries returns, the page computes a list of distinct categories based on the categorySlug property of each FAQ entry. If the URL's category matches one of those categorySlug values, the page sets currentCategory to that value; otherwise currentCategory is set to a neutral value (such as no filter) so all FAQs are shown. When the user changes the selected category via the category filter component, the page updates the URL's category query parameter to the newly chosen categorySlug using client-side navigation. This keeps the URL shareable and ensures that reloading the page or arriving from another page reproduces the same filtered FAQ view. The page may also derive a 'viewMode' state (e.g., 'browse' vs 'search') and an 'expandedFaqIds' set to handle UI behavior (expanded questions and whether search results are visible). These UI states are maintained in memory for the current session and are not persisted in localStorage; they are recomputed when the page is reloaded based on the URL parameters and freshly fetched getFaqEntries data.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter reading, category selection, and distribution of FAQ data and search results to child components.
data_binding: Page (for resolving relatedPageIds and search results pageId to filenames)
event_handlers: onInit: Called when faq.html is loaded. Reads URL.query.category, invokes getFaqEntries, and stores the resulting FAQ entries in page state.
onGetFaqEntriesSuccess: Runs after getFaqEntries resolves; computes the list of distinct categories from categorySlug and initializes currentCategory based on URL.query.category.
onUrlChange: Listens for URL query parameter changes (especially category) and updates currentCategory, causing filtered FAQ lists to recompute.
onCategoryChange: Triggered by the faq-category-filter component when a user selects a new category; updates URL.query.category and currentCategory state.
onSearchSubmit: Triggered by the faq-search-form component; calls searchDocumentation with the provided query and filters (including current category) and stores the returned results.
onSearchResultsLoaded: Updates view mode to show search results and passes them to the faq-search-results-panel component.
onSearchClear: Resets search results and view mode back to browsing FAQs.
onFaqExpandToggle: Updates the expandedFaqIds state when a faq-item is toggled, ensuring that the correct answerMarkdown is visible.
onDeepLinkMatch: After data load, matches any URL hash fragment to a faqId and marks that entry as expanded.

components.functionality: Displays the list of available FAQ categories and allows the user to select which category of FAQs to view. The component reflects the currentCategory derived from the URL and updates it when the user interacts.
data_binding: getFaqEntries (categorySlug values aggregated into a category list)
event_handlers: onMount: Receives the list of categories and currentCategory from the faq-page-controller and initializes the selected state.
onCategoryClick: When a user clicks or selects a category, emits the selected categorySlug to faq-page-controller via onCategoryChange.
onCategorySyncFromUrl: When faq-page-controller notifies that URL.query.category changed, updates the selected category UI to stay in sync.

components.functionality: Captures a free-text query and submits it to searchDocumentation, optionally including the current category or other configured filters. Controls the transition into and out of 'search mode' for the page.
data_binding: URL.query.category (used to build filters for searchDocumentation)
event_handlers: onInputChange: Updates local text input state as the user types.

onSubmit: Prevents default form submission, validates the query, constructs a filters object (e.g., including current category and documentation scope), calls searchDocumentation, and informs faq-page-controller via onSearchSubmit.
onClear: Clears the search input, signals faq-page-controller to return to FAQ browsing (onSearchClear), and optionally clears any searchDocumentation results being displayed.

components.functionality: Shows the results returned by searchDocumentation, including titles, snippets, and links into the appropriate documentation pages. Only visible when the page is in search mode.
data_binding: searchDocumentation.results (title, snippet, pageId, pageType, entityType, entityId)
Page (to resolve pageId into filename and construct navigation URLs)
event_handlers: onResultsUpdate: Called when faq-page-controller updates search results; renders the new list of results.

onResultClick: Uses the clicked result's pageId, pageType, entityType, and entityId to construct a navigation URL and navigates to that page. Any additional query parameters or hash fragments are derived from configuration tied to the Page or entity metadata.
onResultHover: Optional behavior to show additional context (e.g., full title or snippet) without changing navigation state.

components.functionality: Renders a filtered list of FAQ items based on the entries from getFaqEntries and the currently selected category. Manages which items are visible and passes individual items to faq-item components.
event_handlers: onFaqEntriesUpdate: When faq-page-controller provides or updates the FAQ entries, recomputes the filtered list for the currentCategory.
onCategoryFilterChange: When currentCategory changes, re-filters the FAQs displayed to those whose categorySlug matches the selected category (or all if no category is selected).
onFaqClick: Forwards the click event on a FAQ question to faq-page-controller via onFaqExpandToggle along with the faqId.
onHashMatch: After initial render, checks if any faqId matches the URL hash and requests that the corresponding faq-item be expanded.

components.functionality: Represents a single FAQ question and its answer. Handles expand/collapse behavior, renders the answerMarkdown as HTML, and includes related links derived from relatedPageIds.
data_binding: Page (for mapping relatedPageIds to actual documentation pages and URLs)
event_handlers: onHeaderClick: Toggles the expanded state for this faqId and notifies faq-page-controller via onFaqExpandToggle.
onExpandFromController: When faq-page-controller decides this item should be expanded (e.g., due to a URL hash match), updates its internal expanded state.
onMarkdownRendered: Runs after answerMarkdown is converted to HTML to attach click handlers to any internal links that should trigger navigation rather than full page reload (if applicable).

onRelatedLinkClick: When a related link is clicked, uses the associated relatedPageId and configuration to construct and navigate to the target URL, including any needed query parameters for the destination page.

components.functionality: Within an expanded FAQ answer, shows a structured list of related documentation links constructed from relatedPageIds and the Page model. This provides quick navigation from a FAQ answer to deeper docs such as authentication, rate limits, webhooks, SDKs, or specific API reference resources.
data_binding: Page (for resolving each relatedPageId to a target filename and default query parameters)
ApiResource (optional, when relatedPageIds refer to specific API resources for API reference navigation)
Sdk (optional, when relatedPageIds refer to specific SDK documentation pages)
AuthMethod (optional, when relatedPageIds refer to authentication method sections)
event_handlers: onMount: Receives relatedPageIds and resolves them to link descriptors (target filename and any query parameters) using configuration that maps IDs to the appropriate Page and entity metadata.

onLinkClick: Navigates to the resolved URL for the clicked related link. Any required parameters (such as authentication method hints, rate limit contexts, webhook groups, SDK languages, or API resource slugs) are attached to the URL based on the mapping configuration rather than hardcoded values in this component.
onLinksUpdate: When the FAQ entry's relatedPageIds change (e.g., due to updated data from getFaqEntries), regenerates the list of navigation targets.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column style grid with a fixed-width main content column and a narrow left sidebar, aligned to a central content container within the viewport.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow inside the main content column to structure the FAQ page. At the top of the main column, place the FAQ page title with an underline, followed by a short introductory paragraph. Under this, group the interactive discovery tools: first the FAQ search form, then a search results panel directly under it. Below that, start a new section for browsing: a section heading, the category filter controls, and then the FAQ list (accordion items) in a vertical stack. At the very bottom of the main column, add a smaller 'Related documentation' section containing the related links list. The left sidebar follows the same pattern as the mockup: a subtle vertical navigation with a section label (e.g., ‘Reference’) and a link to this FAQ page or FAQ categories, visually secondary to the main column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow inside the main content column to structure the FAQ page. At the top of the main column, place the FAQ page title with an underline, followed by a short introductory paragraph. Under this, group the interactive discovery tools: first the FAQ search form, then a search results panel directly under it. Below that, start a new section for browsing: a section heading, the category filter controls, and then the FAQ list (accordion items) in a vertical stack. At the very bottom of the main column, add a smaller 'Related documentation' section containing the related links list. The left sidebar follows the same pattern as the mockup: a subtle vertical navigation with a section label (e.g., ‘Reference’) and a link to this FAQ page or FAQ categories, visually secondary to the main column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Replicate the mockup’s proportions: a fixed full-width top header at about 10–12% of the viewport height. Below, the content area is split into a narrow left sidebar (roughly 18–22% of the central container width) and a wider main content column (about 78–82%). The main column is centered within the viewport with generous horizontal padding (roughly 24–32px from the viewport edges). Major vertical separations—between intro, search, browse section, and related links—use 32–40px gaps. Smaller elements such as the spacing between the search form and results panel, or between individual FAQ items, use 12–24px. All list-like components (search results, FAQ list, related links) stretch almost the full width of the main column, with internal padding around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious, documentation-style density. Text lines are kept at comfortable lengths within the main column. Each major section (intro, search, browse, related links) has its own breathing room and clear heading, with no tight stacking of controls. The search form is a single, prominent field rather than a busy complex form. The category filter is compact but not crowded, with clearly separated chips/buttons or a single dropdown and adequate padding. FAQ items follow the mockup’s endpoint-row feel: one-line question text in the collapsed state with plenty of horizontal padding and a simple icon on the right, and when expanded, answer text flows in short paragraphs with 8–16px spacing between paragraphs. The search results panel is similarly sparse: short titles, one line of context per result, and clear separation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s vertical-first flow. After the user’s eye lands on the fixed dark header and its 'Documentation' tab, it naturally moves down to the bold 'FAQ' page title in the main column. From there, the intro paragraph leads the eye down to the search form as the primary interactive element. The search results panel appears directly beneath it, so users see immediate feedback. The flow then continues into the browsing section: a heading introduces the category filter, then the eye follows the left-aligned filter controls into the vertical FAQ list. Each FAQ item uses a colored left bar and right-chevron (or expand icon) to create a micro horizontal flow, similar to endpoint cards in the mockup: enter at the color bar and question text, exit toward the right-facing control. The left sidebar remains a subtle secondary navigation anchor in the peripheral vision, never overpowering the main content.

layout.overall_description: The page sits beneath a fixed, full-width top header bar that spans the viewport. The header is dark and compact, occupying about a tenth of the screen height and containing the site logo on the left and a 'Documentation' tab or primary nav to the right, mirroring a typical developer docs shell. Below this, the main content area is centered and divided into two columns: a narrow, light-colored left sidebar and a wider, primary content column on the right. The sidebar is aligned to the same central container width as the main column and uses smaller, quieter text to list reference links, with a 'Reference' label followed by a link to the FAQ section, keeping it visually subordinate. The right-hand main content column begins with a bold 'FAQ' page title aligned left, stretched across the column, with a thin underline or divider running horizontally just beneath. A short introductory paragraph sits immediately below, with generous vertical spacing. Under the intro, a dedicated search area appears: a prominent, horizontally oriented search field positioned near the top-left of the column, occupying roughly two-thirds of the column’s width, with moderate whitespace above and below. Directly beneath the search field lies the search results panel: a full-width, lightly outlined or subtly shaded panel that will show matching FAQs or a neutral empty state message, but remains visually clean. Further down, a new section heading (e.g., 'Browse FAQs') introduces the browse-by-category area. The category filter runs horizontally under this heading, left-aligned, and the FAQ list flows vertically beneath it as a series of card-like accordion rows. Each FAQ row stretches almost the full width of the column, with a narrow colored strip on the far left, question text next to it, and an expand/collapse icon on the far right, echoing the endpoint card pattern from the mockup. When expanded, each item reveals an answer block within the same card, indented and padded. After the FAQ list, the page ends with a smaller 'Related documentation' section, again left-aligned, containing a simple vertical list of text links. Throughout, margins and padding follow the 8px-based scale, producing a calm, orderly, and legible documentation experience, with the left sidebar staying visually in the background and the main column guiding the user from page title, to search, to exploration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ page controller occupies the entire main content column to the right of the left sidebar, beneath the fixed top header. Horizontally, it takes up approximately 78–82% of the central content container, leaving the remaining 18–22% to the left sidebar. It is left-aligned with the sidebar so that their top edges line up just below the header, and its content is padded inward from the viewport edges by about 24–32px. At the top of this controller, the 'FAQ' page title sits flush left, spanning the full width of the main column, with a horizontal underline or divider directly beneath it. About 16–24px below the title is a short introductory paragraph, also left aligned and limited to the main column width. From there, the controller lays out its child components in a clean vertical stack: first the faq-search-form directly below the intro with around 24–32px of vertical gap, then the faq-search-results-panel with about 16–24px gap, then a new section heading (part of this controller’s template) for browsing FAQs. Immediately under that heading, the faq-category-filter is placed, and directly beneath it, with roughly 16px of separation, the faq-list is rendered. Finally, at the bottom of the main column, 32–40px below the last FAQ item, the controller renders a 'Related documentation' heading and the faq-related-links-list. The entire column scrolls vertically under the fixed header, maintaining the strong top-to-bottom flow of a documentation page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary scrollable document column beneath a fixed header, positioned in normal flow to the right of the left sidebar and respecting the global content container margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category filter sits in the main content column under a browse-related section heading controlled by the faq-page-controller. It is left aligned and occupies a full-width horizontal strip across the main column, with its content constrained to the same inner width as the text above it. Vertically, it appears approximately 16–24px below the section heading and 16px above the start of the faq-list. The filter components themselves (e.g., a 'Category' label followed by pills, tabs, or a dropdown) are arranged along a horizontal row starting at the left edge of the column, leaving a comfortable amount of empty space to the right to match the mockup’s spacious feel. The row height is around 40–48px, with 8–12px of vertical padding inside the filter area and around 16–24px horizontal padding between individual filter elements. On narrower viewports, this strip can wrap its controls onto multiple lines while still maintaining left alignment and consistent vertical spacing. There is no heavy border; at most a subtle bottom divider or light background tint to differentiate the filter area from the FAQ list below, in keeping with the minimalist documentation style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in standard document flow within the main column, expanding or wrapping horizontally as needed but not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ search form is positioned near the top of the main column as the first interactive block after the intro paragraph. It is left aligned, with its main input field extending across roughly 60–70% of the main content column’s width, leaving a wide band of whitespace on the right to maintain the open, documentation-style feel. The search input is a single horizontal field about 40–48px tall, with 12–16px of internal side padding and a quiet border or subtle outline. A search icon may be placed inside the left side of the field, while a small, unobtrusive button or embedded icon for submission could be at the right edge of the field. The entire search form row sits about 24–32px below the introductory paragraph and about 16–24px above the faq-search-results-panel. On smaller screens, the input can grow to nearly 100% of the column width while preserving ample vertical spacing above and below. There are no extra visual dividers beyond the field itself, keeping the block clean and in line with the minimalist aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Placed in normal vertical flow inside the main column; stretches responsively within the main column width but does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search results panel is positioned directly underneath the faq-search-form, separated from it by about 16–24px of vertical whitespace. It spans nearly the full width of the main content column, aligning with the left edge of the search form and other text content. Visually, it reads as a light, unobtrusive container: a subtle border or lightly shaded background distinguishes it from the page background, and it has internal padding of around 12–16px on all sides. Inside, search results (if any) are stacked vertically in a list, each result row separated by about 8–12px. Each row may consist of a result title in a slightly stronger weight and a single line of secondary text beneath or next to it, but overall the content is minimal to match the low-density documentation feel. When there are no active results, the panel either collapses or shows a simple, centered empty-state message (e.g., 'No FAQs match your search yet') in muted text, still respecting the same padding and width. The bottom of the panel is spaced 32–40px above the next section heading (for the browsing area) to clearly separate search-driven exploration from category-based browsing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly under the search form within the main content column, expanding vertically as results are added but remaining within the column’s natural scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The FAQ list occupies a substantial vertical section of the main content column, starting just below the faq-category-filter. A browse section heading sits above (managed by the page controller), and the top of the faq-list begins approximately 16px below the filter controls. Horizontally, the list uses nearly the full width of the main column, aligning with the page title, paragraphs, and search area. The list is a simple vertical stack of faq-item components, one per row, with a consistent gap of roughly 8–16px between items to keep the stack legible and airy. There is no separate surrounding card; the list is implied by the repeated FAQ cards themselves. The entire FAQ list section extends downward and can become quite tall, but the generous top and bottom margins (32–40px from the preceding section and from the related links section below) keep it visually grouped. The last FAQ item sits about 24–32px above the 'Related documentation' heading, creating a clear transition between FAQs and secondary navigation content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard, vertically expanding list that scrolls with the main content column, with its internal width constrained to the main column container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each FAQ item is rendered as a horizontally stretched, card-like accordion row that visually echoes the endpoint rows from the mockup. In its collapsed state, the item is essentially a single-row card. It spans almost the full width of the main content column, with outer margins of only a few pixels relative to neighboring items. Along the far left edge runs a narrow, colored vertical bar (around 3–4px wide) from the top to the bottom of the question header area, signaling the interactive nature or category of the FAQ. Immediately to the right of this bar, within the main card content, sits the question text in a medium–bold weight, left aligned and vertically centered within a 48–64px-tall header row. The card content area has around 12–16px of padding on all sides. On the far right, aligned horizontally with the question text, a chevron or plus/minus icon indicates the accordion’s state; it is right aligned and separated from the question text by flexible space so the question can wrap before reaching the icon. The entire header row creates a subtle horizontal flow from the colored bar on the left to the interaction icon on the right. When expanded, the card grows vertically, revealing the answer content directly beneath the header. The answer area is indented within the same card boundary with about 12–16px of top padding and 12–16px bottom padding; paragraphs are left aligned, with 8–12px spacing between them to maintain readability. There are no heavy drop shadows—just a light outline or minimal background tint to keep the item flat and consistent with the rest of the documentation layout. Each faq-item is separated from the next by 8–16px of vertical whitespace, reinforcing the clean, structured list appearance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expands and collapses vertically in place within the faq-list, affecting only the vertical height of the list; remains in normal flow with no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related links list is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, below the faq-list, and is treated as a smaller, tertiary section. A 'Related documentation' or similar heading sits directly above it, aligned left, with about 24–32px of vertical space separating this heading from the last FAQ item. The faq-related-links-list begins about 8–12px below that heading. Horizontally, it aligns with the rest of the content in the main column, spanning nearly the full column width but containing simple, low-contrast text links. Links are arranged in a vertical list, one per row, with modest vertical spacing of 4–8px between them. Each link row remains flat—no card backgrounds or heavy dividers—possibly accompanied by a small, subtle icon (like an external-link or document icon) to the left. This list does not compete visually with the main FAQ content: fonts are slightly smaller or less bold than the FAQ questions, and colors are more muted while still clearly clickable. The section ends with 32–40px of bottom padding before the end of the scrollable area, giving it a clean visual endpoint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the end of the main content flow, scrolling like any other section, with no fixed or sticky behavior.
